revision.comment: 『国史大辞典』（執筆:田中宣一）『日本史大事典』（執筆:薗田稔）の「[[冬祭]]」項目を参考に立ち上げ。
revision.text:
'''冬祭り'''（ふゆまつり）とは、[[冬]]に行われる[[祭り]]のこと。[[立冬]]から[[立春]]にかけての3ヶ月間（[[11月]]・[[12月]]・[[1月]]）に行われるものを指す場合が多い。
冬祭りは[[秋祭り]]が持つ収穫祭的要素と[[春祭り]]が持つ祈年祭的要素の両方を併せ持っており、その位置づけについては不明な部分も多い。元々[[10月 (旧暦)|旧暦10月]]は忌月とされたために月が変わって冬に突入した[[11月 (旧暦)|旧暦11月]]に収穫祭を行ったが、後にその禁忌が失われて収穫直後に秋祭りが成立したとも言われている。[[折口信夫]]は秋祭り・冬祭り・春祭りは元々一続きの祭り（収穫・魂の更新・祈年）であったのが、秋祭りと春祭りが分離して残りが冬祭りとなったと説く。[[神祇官]]の[[四時祭]]では、旧暦11月の[[相嘗祭]]・[[鎮魂祭]]・[[新嘗祭]]が冬祭りとされ、翌月の[[月次]]・[[鎮火]]・[[道饗]]も冬特有の祭事ではないものの、冬祭りとして挙行されている。
現在、冬祭りとして行われるものは、鎮魂祭の要素を残すものが多く、鎮魂祭の際に行われた[[霜月神楽]]を祖とする[[湯立]]・[[神楽]]などが行われることが多い。

'''冬祭り'''（ふゆまつり）とは、[[冬]]に行われる[[祭り]]のこと。[[立冬]]から[[立春]]にかけての3ヶ月間（[[11月]]・[[12月]]・[[1月]]）に行われるものを指す場合が多い。
冬祭りは[[秋祭り]]が持つ収穫祭的要素と[[春祭り]]が持つ祈年祭的要素の両方を併せ持っており、その位置づけについては不明な部分も多い。元々[[10月 (旧暦)|旧暦10月]]は忌月とされたために月が変わって冬に突入した[[11月 (旧暦)|旧暦11月]]に収穫祭を行ったが、後にその禁忌が失われて収穫直後に秋祭りが成立したとも言われている。[[折口信夫]]は秋祭り・冬祭り・春祭りは元々一続きの祭り（収穫・魂の更新・祈年）であったのが、秋祭りと春祭りが分離して残りが冬祭りとなったと説く。[[神祇官]]の[[四時祭]]では、旧暦11月の[[相嘗祭]]・[[鎮魂祭]]・[[新嘗祭]]が冬祭りとされ、翌月の[[月次]]・[[鎮火]]・[[道饗]]も冬特有の祭事ではないものの、冬祭りとして挙行されている。
現在、冬祭りとして行われるものは、鎮魂祭の要素を残すものが多く、鎮魂祭の際に行われた[[霜月神楽]]を祖とする[[湯立]]・[[神楽]]などが行われることが多い。
== 関連項目 == * [[夏祭り]] * [[冬休み]]

revision.comment: 吉川弘文館『日本民俗大辞典』「冬祭」（執筆:伊藤好英）を参考に加筆
revision.text:
'''冬祭り'''（ふゆまつり）とは、[[冬]]に行われる[[祭り]]のこと。[[立冬]]から[[立春]]にかけての3ヶ月間（[[11月]]・[[12月]]・[[1月]]）に行われるものを指す場合が多い。
冬祭りは[[秋祭り]]が持つ収穫祭的要素と[[春祭り]]が持つ祈年祭的要素の両方を併せ持っており、その位置づけについては不明な部分も多い。元々[[10月 (旧暦)|旧暦10月]]は忌月とされたために月が変わって冬に突入した[[11月 (旧暦)|旧暦11月]]に収穫祭を行ったが、後にその禁忌が失われて収穫直後に秋祭りが成立したとも言われている。[[折口信夫]]は秋から翌春にかけてのこの時期を[[魂振]]を行って人間を含めた全ての魂が更新されるための時期として捉え、秋祭り・冬祭り・春祭りは元々一続きの祭り（収穫・魂の更新・祈年）であったのが、秋祭りと春祭りが分離して残りが冬祭りとなったと説く。また、[[柳田国男]]は冬祭りの中心となる旧暦11月が[[冬至]]を含む時期であり春に向かう転機であることを指摘し、日本の祭りは本来旧暦11月を重要視したと説く。[[朝廷]]の[[神祇官]]の[[四時祭]]では、旧暦11月の[[相嘗祭]]・[[鎮魂祭]]・[[新嘗祭]]が冬祭りとされ、翌月の[[月次]]・[[鎮火]]・[[道饗]]も冬特有の祭事ではないものの、冬祭りとして挙行されている。
現在、冬祭りとして行われるものは、鎮魂祭の要素を残すものが多く、鎮魂祭の際に行われた[[霜月神楽]]を祖とする[[湯立]]・[[神楽]]などが行われることが多い。
== 関連項目 == * [[夏祭り]] * [[冬休み]]
{{event-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:ふゆまつり}} [[Category:日本の文化]] [[Category:祭]] [[Category:日本の祭り]]

'''冬祭り'''（ふゆまつり）とは、[[冬]]に行われる[[祭り]]のこと。[[立冬]]から[[立春]]にかけての3ヶ月間（[[11月]]・[[12月]]・[[1月]]）に行われるものを指す場合が多い。
冬祭りは[[秋祭り]]が持つ収穫祭的要素と[[春祭り]]が持つ祈年祭的要素の両方を併せ持っており、その位置づけについては不明な部分も多い。元々[[10月 (旧暦)|旧暦10月]]は忌月とされたために月が変わって冬に突入した[[11月 (旧暦)|旧暦11月]]に収穫祭を行ったが、後にその禁忌が失われて収穫直後に秋祭りが成立したとも言われている。[[折口信夫]]は秋から翌春にかけてのこの時期を[[魂振]]を行って人間を含めた全ての魂が更新されるための時期として捉え、秋祭り・冬祭り・春祭りは元々一続きの祭り（収穫・魂の更新・祈年）であったのが、秋祭りと春祭りが分離して残りが冬祭りとなったと説く。また、[[柳田国男]]は冬祭りの中心となる旧暦11月が[[冬至]]を含む時期であり春に向かう転機であることを指摘し、日本の祭りは本来旧暦11月を重要視したと説く。[[朝廷]]の[[神祇官]]の[[四時祭]]では、旧暦11月の[[相嘗祭]]・[[鎮魂祭]]・[[新嘗祭]]が冬祭りとされ、翌月の[[月次]]・[[鎮火]]・[[道饗]]も冬特有の祭事ではないものの、冬祭りとして挙行されている。
現在、冬祭りとして行われるものは、鎮魂祭の要素を残すものが多く、鎮魂祭の際に行われた[[霜月神楽]]を祖とする[[湯立]]・[[神楽]]などが行われることが多い。

'''冬祭り'''（ふゆまつり）とは[[冬]]に行われる[[祭り]]のことである。[[立冬]]から[[立春]]にかけての3ヶ月間（[[11月]]・[[12月]]・[[1月]]）に行われるものを指す場合が多い。
冬祭りは[[秋祭り]]が持つ収穫祭的要素と[[春祭り]]が持つ祈年祭的要素の両方を併せ持っており、その位置づけについては不明な部分も多い。元々[[10月 (旧暦)|旧暦10月]]は忌月とされたために月が変わって冬に突入した[[11月 (旧暦)|旧暦11月]]に収穫祭を行ったが、後にその禁忌が失われて収穫直後に秋祭りが成立したとも言われている。[[折口信夫]]は秋から翌春にかけてのこの時期を[[魂振]]を行って人間を含めた全ての魂が更新されるための時期として捉え、秋祭り・冬祭り・春祭りは元々一続きの祭り（収穫・魂の更新・祈年）であったのが秋祭りと春祭りが分離して残りが冬祭りとなったと説く。また、[[柳田國男]]は冬祭りの中心となる旧暦11月が[[冬至]]を含む時期であり春に向かう転機であることを指摘し、日本の祭りは本来旧暦11月を重要視したと説く。[[朝廷]]の[[神祇官]]の[[四時祭]]では旧暦11月の[[相嘗祭]]・[[鎮魂祭]]・[[新嘗祭]]が冬祭りとされ、翌月の[[月次]]・[[鎮火]]・[[道饗]]も冬特有の祭事ではないものの冬祭りとして挙行されている。
現在、冬祭りとして行われるものは鎮魂祭の要素を残すものが多く鎮魂祭の際に行われた[[霜月神楽]]を祖とする[[湯立]]・[[神楽]]などが行われることが多い。

{{出典の明記|date=2017年2月8日 (水) 11:32 (UTC)}}
'''冬祭り'''（ふゆまつり）とは[[冬]]に行われる[[祭り]]のことである。[[立冬]]から[[立春]]にかけての3ヶ月間（[[11月]]・[[12月]]・[[1月]]）に行われるものを指す場合が多い。
冬祭りは[[秋祭り]]が持つ収穫祭的要素と[[春祭り]]が持つ祈年祭的要素の両方を併せ持っており、その位置づけについては不明な部分も多い。元々[[10月 (旧暦)|旧暦10月]]は忌月とされたために月が変わって冬に突入した[[11月 (旧暦)|旧暦11月]]に収穫祭を行ったが、後にその禁忌が失われて収穫直後に秋祭りが成立したとも言われている。[[折口信夫]]は秋から翌春にかけてのこの時期を[[魂振]]を行って人間を含めた全ての魂が更新されるための時期として捉え、秋祭り・冬祭り・春祭りは元々一続きの祭り（収穫・魂の更新・祈年）であったのが秋祭りと春祭りが分離して残りが冬祭りとなったと説く。また、[[柳田國男]]は冬祭りの中心となる旧暦11月が[[冬至]]を含む時期であり春に向かう転機であることを指摘し、日本の祭りは本来旧暦11月を重要視したと説く。[[朝廷]]の[[神祇官]]の[[四時祭]]では旧暦11月の[[相嘗祭]]・[[鎮魂祭]]・[[新嘗祭]]が冬祭りとされ、翌月の[[月次祭]]・[[鎮火]]・[[道饗]]も冬特有の祭事ではないものの冬祭りとして挙行されている。
現在、冬祭りとして行われるものは鎮魂祭の要素を残すものが多く鎮魂祭の際に行われた[[霜月神楽]]を祖とする[[湯立]]・[[神楽]]などが行われることが多い。

contributor.username: ja>シェン,アーナリー,ン,アーバァ.

revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2017年2月8日 (水) 11:32 (UTC)}} {{画像募集中|cat=日本の画像募集依頼}} 冬祭および'''冬祭り'''（ふゆまつり）とは[[冬]]に行われる[[祭り]]のことである。[[立冬]]から[[立春]]にかけての3か月間（[[11月]]・[[12月]]・[[1月]]）に行われるものを指す場合が多い。
== 概要 == 冬祭りは、[[秋祭り]]が持つ'''[[収穫祭]]的要素'''と、[[春祭り]]が持つ'''[[祈年祭]]的要素'''の両方を併せ持っており、その位置づけについては不明な部分も多い。古来、[[旧暦]]の[[10月 (旧暦)|10月]]は[[忌月]]とされ<ref>忌月は、新暦においては1月・5月・9月と紹介されるが、旧暦では約1か月づつ遅れるため旧暦の10月が忌月というのは正しい。</ref>、したがって月が変わって冬に突入した旧暦の[[11月 (旧暦)|11月]]に収穫祭を行ったが、後にその[[忌み|禁忌]]が失われて'''収穫直後の秋祭り'''が成立したともされる。
[[折口信夫]]は、秋から翌春にかけてのこの時期が、[[魂振]]、つまり鎮魂を行い、人間を含めた全ての魂が更新されるための時期として捉えた。さらに秋祭り・冬祭り・春祭りを、元来、収穫・魂の更新・祈年を扱う一続きの祭りであったとし、これから秋祭りと春祭りが分離し、残りが冬祭りとなったと説く。
また、[[柳田國男]]は、冬祭りの中心となる旧暦の11月が[[冬至]]を含む時期であり、春に向かう転機であることを指摘し、日本の祭りは本来、旧暦の11月を重要視したと説く。
[[朝廷]]の[[神祇官]]における[[四時祭]]では旧暦の11月の[[相嘗祭]]や[[鎮魂祭]]、[[新嘗祭]]が冬祭りとされ、翌月の[[月次祭]]や[[鎮火]]、[[道饗]]も、冬特有の祭事ではないものの冬祭りとして挙行されている。
現在、冬祭りとして行われるものは鎮魂祭の要素を残すものが多く、鎮魂祭の際に行われた[[霜月神楽]]を祖とする[[湯立]]や[[神楽]]などが行われることが多い。
== 関連項目 == * [[春祭り]] * [[夏祭り]] * [[秋祭り]] * [[冬休み]] * [[ユール]] - 古代ヨーロッパの冬至の頃の祭り
{{Event-stub}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:ふゆまつり}} [[Category:日本の文化]] [[Category:日本の祭り]] [[Category:祭]] [[Category:冬のイベント]]

{{出典の明記|date=2017年2月8日 (水) 11:32 (UTC)}} {{画像募集中|cat=日本の画像募集依頼}} 冬祭および'''冬祭り'''（ふゆまつり）とは[[冬]]に行われる[[祭り]]のことである。[[立冬]]から[[立春]]にかけての3か月間（[[11月]]・[[12月]]・[[1月]]）に行われるものを指す場合が多い。
== 概要 == 冬祭りは、[[秋祭り]]が持つ'''[[収穫祭]]的要素'''と、[[春祭り]]が持つ'''[[祈年祭]]的要素'''の両方を併せ持っており、その位置づけについては不明な部分も多い。古来、[[旧暦]]の[[10月 (旧暦)|10月]]は[[忌月]]とされ<ref>忌月は、新暦においては1月・5月・9月と紹介されるが、旧暦では約1か月づつ遅れるため旧暦の10月が忌月というのは正しい。</ref>、したがって月が変わって冬に突入した旧暦の[[11月 (旧暦)|11月]]に収穫祭を行ったが、後にその[[忌み|禁忌]]が失われて'''収穫直後の秋祭り'''が成立したともされる。
[[折口信夫]]は、秋から翌春にかけてのこの時期が、[[魂振]]、つまり鎮魂を行い、人間を含めた全ての魂が更新されるための時期として捉えた。さらに秋祭り・冬祭り・春祭りを、元来、収穫・魂の更新・祈年を扱う一続きの祭りであったとし、これから秋祭りと春祭りが分離し、残りが冬祭りとなったと説く。
また、[[柳田國男]]は、冬祭りの中心となる旧暦の11月が[[冬至]]を含む時期であり、春に向かう転機であることを指摘し、日本の祭りは本来、旧暦の11月を重要視したと説く。
[[朝廷]]の[[神祇官]]における[[四時祭]]では旧暦の11月の[[相嘗祭]]や[[鎮魂祭]]、[[新嘗祭]]が冬祭りとされ、翌月の[[月次祭]]や[[鎮火]]、[[道饗]]も、冬特有の祭事ではないものの冬祭りとして挙行されている。
現在、冬祭りとして行われるものは鎮魂祭の要素を残すものが多く、鎮魂祭の際に行われた[[霜月神楽]]を祖とする[[湯立]]や[[神楽]]などが行われることが多い。
== 脚注 == {{reflist}}
== 関連項目 == * [[春祭り]] * [[夏祭り]] * [[秋祭り]] * [[冬休み]] * [[ユール]] - 古代ヨーロッパの冬至の頃の祭り
{{Event-stub}}

{{出典の明記|date=2017年2月8日 (水) 11:32 (UTC)}} {{画像募集中|cat=Images of Japan}} Winter festivals or '''Winter Matsuri''' (冬祭り) refer to [[festivals]] held during the [[winter]] season. These events typically occur over the three-month period from [[Rittō]] (the beginning of winter) to [[Risshun]] (the beginning of spring), corresponding to [[November]], [[December]], and [[January]].
== Overview == Winter festivals embody elements of both '''[[harvest festivals]]''' traditionally associated with [[autumn festivals]] and '''[[kinensai]] (prayer festivals)''' tied to [[spring festivals]]. Their historical and cultural positioning remains somewhat unclear. In ancient times, the [[10th month (lunar calendar)]] of the [[lunisolar calendar]] was considered a [[Month of Abstinence]] (忌月, **Imitsuki**). <ref>The Month of Abstinence corresponds to January, May, and September in the Gregorian calendar. However, in the lunisolar calendar, it occurs approximately a month later, making the 10th lunar month the true abstinence period.</ref> Consequently, harvest festivals were traditionally held in the following [[11th month (lunar calendar)]], marking the onset of winter. Over time, these prohibitions faded, leading to the establishment of harvest-focused autumn festivals instead.
[[Nobuo Orikuchi]] theorized that this transitional period, from autumn to the following spring, was fundamentally tied to the practice of [[Tama Furihime]] or soul-shaking. This tradition was believed to rejuvenate and renew the souls of all living beings. He proposed that autumn, winter, and spring festivals were originally a single continuum, addressing themes of harvest, soul renewal, and prayers for the coming year. According to this view, autumn and spring festivals later diverged, leaving the remainder as winter festivals.
Meanwhile, [[Kunio Yanagita]] emphasized the 11th lunar month, which included the [[winter solstice]] as a critical turning point toward spring. He argued that the prominence of festivals during this month reflected its traditional importance in Japan.
In the [[Imperial Court]]'s [[Jingi-kan]] (Department of Divinities), [[shijisai]] (四時祭, seasonal festivals) included the [[Aenokoto]] and [[Chinkon-sai]] (鎮魂祭), [[Niinamesai]] (新嘗祭), and similar rites held during the 11th lunar month, regarded as winter festivals. Additional ceremonies such as the [[Tsukinamisai]] (月次祭), [[Chinka]] (鎮火, fire pacification), and [[Michiage]] (道饗, road offerings) followed in the next month. While these were not inherently tied to the winter season, they were often celebrated as part of the winter festival period.
Today, many winter festivals retain elements of chinkon (鎮魂, soul pacification), including rituals such as [[Simotsuki Kagura]] (霜月神楽), which involves [[hot water purification]] rites and [[Kagura]] performances.
== Notes == {{reflist}}
== Related Topics == * [[Spring festivals]] * [[Summer festivals]] * [[Autumn festivals]] * [[Winter break]] * [[Yule]] – Ancient European winter solstice celebrations
{{Event-stub}}

{{出典の明記|date=2017年2月8日 (水) 11:32 (UTC)}} {{画像募集中|cat=Images of Japan}} Winter festivals or '''Winter Matsuri''' (冬祭り) refer to [[festivals]] held during the [[winter]] season. These events typically occur over the three-month period from [[Rittō]] (the beginning of winter) to [[Risshun]] (the beginning of spring), corresponding to [[November]], [[December]], and [[January]].
== Overview == Winter festivals embody elements of both '''[[harvest festivals]]''' traditionally associated with [[autumn festivals]] and '''[[kinensai]] (prayer festivals)''' tied to [[spring festivals]]. Their historical and cultural positioning remains somewhat unclear. In ancient times, the [[10th month (lunar calendar)]] of the [[lunisolar calendar]] was considered a [[Month of Abstinence]] (忌月, **Imitsuki**). <ref>The Month of Abstinence corresponds to January, May, and September in the Gregorian calendar. However, in the lunisolar calendar, it occurs approximately a month later, making the 10th lunar month the true abstinence period.</ref> Consequently, harvest festivals were traditionally held in the following [[11th month (lunar calendar)]], marking the onset of winter. Over time, these prohibitions faded, leading to the establishment of harvest-focused autumn festivals instead.
[[Nobuo Orikuchi]] theorized that this transitional period, from autumn to the following spring, was fundamentally tied to the practice of [[Tama Furihime]] or soul-shaking. This tradition was believed to rejuvenate and renew the souls of all living beings. He proposed that autumn, winter, and spring festivals were originally a single continuum, addressing themes of harvest, soul renewal, and prayers for the coming year. According to this view, autumn and spring festivals later diverged, leaving the remainder as winter festivals.
Meanwhile, [[Kunio Yanagita]] emphasized the 11th lunar month, which included the [[winter solstice]] as a critical turning point toward spring. He argued that the prominence of festivals during this month reflected its traditional importance in Japan.
In the [[Imperial Court]]'s [[Jingi-kan]] (Department of Divinities), [[shijisai]] (四時祭, seasonal festivals) included the [[Aenokoto]] and [[Chinkon-sai]] (鎮魂祭), [[Niinamesai]] (新嘗祭), and similar rites held during the 11th lunar month, regarded as winter festivals. Additional ceremonies such as the [[Tsukinamisai]] (月次祭), [[Chinka]] (鎮火, fire pacification), and [[Michiage]] (道饗, road offerings) followed in the next month. While these were not inherently tied to the winter season, they were often celebrated as part of the winter festival period.
Today, many winter festivals retain elements of chinkon (鎮魂, soul pacification), including rituals such as [[Simotsuki Kagura]] (霜月神楽), which involves [[hot water purification]] rites and [[Kagura]] performances.

{{出典の明記|date=2017年2月8日 (水) 11:32 (UTC)}} {{画像募集中|cat=Images of Japan}} Winter festivals or '''Winter Matsuri''' (冬祭り) refer to [[festivals]] held during the [[winter]] season. These events typically occur over the three-month period from [[Rittō]] (the beginning of winter) to [[Risshun]] (the beginning of spring), corresponding to [[November]], [[December]], and [[January]].
== Overview == Winter festivals embody elements of both '''[[harvest festivals]]''' traditionally associated with [[autumn festivals]] and '''[[kinensai]] (prayer festivals)''' tied to [[spring festivals]]. Their historical and cultural positioning remains somewhat unclear. In ancient times, the [[10th month (lunar calendar)]] of the [[lunisolar calendar]] was considered a [[Month of Abstinence]] (忌月, **Imitsuki**). <ref>The Month of Abstinence corresponds to January, May, and September in the Gregorian calendar. However, in the lunisolar calendar, it occurs approximately a month later, making the 10th lunar month the true abstinence period.</ref> Consequently, harvest festivals were traditionally held in the following [[11th month (lunar calendar)]], marking the onset of winter. Over time, these prohibitions faded, leading to the establishment of harvest-focused autumn festivals instead.
[[Nobuo Orikuchi]] theorized that this transitional period, from autumn to the following spring, was fundamentally tied to the practice of [[Tama Furihime]] or soul-shaking. This tradition was believed to rejuvenate and renew the souls of all living beings. He proposed that autumn, winter, and spring festivals were originally a single continuum, addressing themes of harvest, soul renewal, and prayers for the coming year. According to this view, autumn and spring festivals later diverged, leaving the remainder as winter festivals.
Meanwhile, [[Kunio Yanagita]] emphasized the 11th lunar month, which included the [[winter solstice]] as a critical turning point toward spring. He argued that the prominence of festivals during this month reflected its traditional importance in Japan.
In the [[Imperial Court]]'s [[Jingi-kan]] (Department of Divinities), [[shijisai]] (四時祭, seasonal festivals) included the [[Aenokoto]] and [[Chinkon-sai]] (鎮魂祭), [[Niinamesai]] (新嘗祭), and similar rites held during the 11th lunar month, regarded as winter festivals. Additional ceremonies such as the [[Tsukinamisai]] (月次祭), [[Chinka]] (鎮火, fire pacification), and [[Michiage]] (道饗, road offerings) followed in the next month. While these were not inherently tied to the winter season, they were often celebrated as part of the winter festival period.
Today, many winter festivals retain elements of chinkon (鎮魂, soul pacification), including rituals such as [[Simotsuki Kagura]] (霜月神楽), which involves [[hot water purification]] rites and [[Kagura]] performances.
== Notes == {{reflist}}
* [[Spring festivals]] * [[Summer festivals]] * [[Autumn festivals]] * [[Winter break]] * [[Yule]] – Ancient European winter solstice celebrations
{{Event-stub}}

revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2017年2月8日 (水) 11:32 (UTC)}} {{画像募集中|cat=Images of Japan}} Winter festivals or '''Winter Matsuri''' (冬祭り) refer to [[festivals]] held during the [[winter]] season. These events typically occur over the three-month period from [[Rittō]] (the beginning of winter) to [[Risshun]] (the beginning of spring), corresponding to [[November]], [[December]], and [[January]].
== Overview == Winter festivals embody elements of both '''[[harvest festivals]]''' traditionally associated with [[autumn festivals]] and '''[[kinensai]] (prayer festivals)''' tied to [[spring festivals]]. Their historical and cultural positioning remains somewhat unclear. In ancient times, the [[10th month (lunar calendar)]] of the [[lunisolar calendar]] was considered a [[Month of Abstinence]] (忌月, **Imitsuki**). <ref>The Month of Abstinence corresponds to January, May, and September in the Gregorian calendar. However, in the lunisolar calendar, it occurs approximately a month later, making the 10th lunar month the true abstinence period.</ref> Consequently, harvest festivals were traditionally held in the following [[11th month (lunar calendar)]], marking the onset of winter. Over time, these prohibitions faded, leading to the establishment of harvest-focused autumn festivals instead.
[[Nobuo Orikuchi]] theorized that this transitional period, from autumn to the following spring, was fundamentally tied to the practice of [[Tama Furihime]] or soul-shaking. This tradition was believed to rejuvenate and renew the souls of all living beings. He proposed that autumn, winter, and spring festivals were originally a single continuum, addressing themes of harvest, soul renewal, and prayers for the coming year. According to this view, autumn and spring festivals later diverged, leaving the remainder as winter festivals.
Meanwhile, [[Kunio Yanagita]] emphasized the 11th lunar month, which included the [[winter solstice]] as a critical turning point toward spring. He argued that the prominence of festivals during this month reflected its traditional importance in Japan.
In the [[Imperial Court]]'s [[Jingi-kan]] (Department of Divinities), [[shijisai]] (四時祭, seasonal festivals) included the [[Aenokoto]] and [[Chinkon-sai]] (鎮魂祭), [[Niinamesai]] (新嘗祭), and similar rites held during the 11th lunar month, regarded as winter festivals. Additional ceremonies such as the [[Tsukinamisai]] (月次祭), [[Chinka]] (鎮火, fire pacification), and [[Michiage]] (道饗, road offerings) followed in the next month. While these were not inherently tied to the winter season, they were often celebrated as part of the winter festival period.
Today, many winter festivals retain elements of chinkon (鎮魂, soul pacification), including rituals such as [[Simotsuki Kagura]] (霜月神楽), which involves [[hot water purification]] rites and [[Kagura]] performances.
== Notes == {{reflist}}
* [[Spring festivals]] * [[Summer festivals]] * [[Autumn festivals]] * [[Winter break]] * [[Yule]] – Ancient European winter solstice celebrations
{{Event-stub}}

{{出典の明記|date=2017年2月8日 (水) 11:32 (UTC)}} {{画像募集中|cat=Images of Japan}} Winter festivals or '''Winter Matsuri''' (冬祭り) refer to [[festivals]] held during the [[winter]] season. These events typically occur over the three-month period from [[Rittō]] (the beginning of winter) to [[Risshun]] (the beginning of spring), corresponding to [[November]], [[December]], and [[January]].
== Overview == Winter festivals embody elements of both '''[[harvest festivals]]''' traditionally associated with [[autumn festivals]] and '''[[kinensai]] (prayer festivals)''' tied to [[spring festivals]]. Their historical and cultural positioning remains somewhat unclear. In ancient times, the [[10th month (lunar calendar)]] of the [[lunisolar calendar]] was considered a [[Month of Abstinence]] (忌月, **Imitsuki**). <ref>The Month of Abstinence corresponds to January, May, and September in the Gregorian calendar. However, in the lunisolar calendar, it occurs approximately a month later, making the 10th lunar month the true abstinence period.</ref> Consequently, harvest festivals were traditionally held in the following [[11th month (lunar calendar)]], marking the onset of winter. Over time, these prohibitions faded, leading to the establishment of harvest-focused autumn festivals instead.
[[Nobuo Orikuchi]] theorized that this transitional period, from autumn to the following spring, was fundamentally tied to the practice of [[Tama Furihime]] or soul-shaking. This tradition was believed to rejuvenate and renew the souls of all living beings. He proposed that autumn, winter, and spring festivals were originally a single continuum, addressing themes of harvest, soul renewal, and prayers for the coming year. According to this view, autumn and spring festivals later diverged, leaving the remainder as winter festivals.
Meanwhile, [[Kunio Yanagita]] emphasized the 11th lunar month, which included the [[winter solstice]] as a critical turning point toward spring. He argued that the prominence of festivals during this month reflected its traditional importance in Japan.
In the [[Imperial Court]]'s [[Jingi-kan]] (Department of Divinities), [[shijisai]] (四時祭, seasonal festivals) included the [[Aenokoto]] and [[Chinkon-sai]] (鎮魂祭), [[Niinamesai]] (新嘗祭), and similar rites held during the 11th lunar month, regarded as winter festivals. Additional ceremonies such as the [[Tsukinamisai]] (月次祭), [[Chinka]] (鎮火, fire pacification), and [[Michiage]] (道饗, road offerings) followed in the next month. While these were not inherently tied to the winter season, they were often celebrated as part of the winter festival period.
Today, many winter festivals retain elements of chinkon (鎮魂, soul pacification), including rituals such as [[Simotsuki Kagura]] (霜月神楽), which involves [[hot water purification]] rites and [[Kagura]] performances.

{{出典の明記|date=2017年2月8日 (水) 11:32 (UTC)}} {{画像募集中|cat=Images of Japan}} Winter festivals or '''Winter Matsuri''' (冬祭り) refer to [[festivals]] held during the [[winter]] season. These events typically occur over the three-month period from [[Rittō]] (the beginning of winter) to [[Risshun]] (the beginning of spring), corresponding to [[November]], [[December]], and [[January]].
== Overview == Winter festivals embody elements of both '''[[harvest festivals]]''' traditionally associated with [[autumn festivals]] and '''[[kinensai]] (prayer festivals)''' tied to [[spring festivals]]. Their historical and cultural positioning remains somewhat unclear. In ancient times, the [[10th month (lunar calendar)]] of the [[lunisolar calendar]] was considered a [[Month of Abstinence]] (忌月, **Imitsuki**). <ref>The Month of Abstinence corresponds to January, May, and September in the Gregorian calendar. However, in the lunisolar calendar, it occurs approximately a month later, making the 10th lunar month the true abstinence period.</ref> Consequently, harvest festivals were traditionally held in the following [[11th month (lunar calendar)]], marking the onset of winter. Over time, these prohibitions faded, leading to the establishment of harvest-focused autumn festivals instead.
[[Nobuo Orikuchi]] theorized that this transitional period, from autumn to the following spring, was fundamentally tied to the practice of [[Tama Furihime]] or soul-shaking. This tradition was believed to rejuvenate and renew the souls of all living beings. He proposed that autumn, winter, and spring festivals were originally a single continuum, addressing themes of harvest, soul renewal, and prayers for the coming year. According to this view, autumn and spring festivals later diverged, leaving the remainder as winter festivals.
Meanwhile, [[Kunio Yanagita]] emphasized the 11th lunar month, which included the [[winter solstice]] as a critical turning point toward spring. He argued that the prominence of festivals during this month reflected its traditional importance in Japan.
In the [[Imperial Court]]'s [[Jingi-kan]] (Department of Divinities), [[shijisai]] (四時祭, seasonal festivals) included the [[Aenokoto]] and [[Chinkon-sai]] (鎮魂祭), [[Niinamesai]] (新嘗祭), and similar rites held during the 11th lunar month, regarded as winter festivals. Additional ceremonies such as the [[Tsukinamisai]] (月次祭), [[Chinka]] (鎮火, fire pacification), and [[Michiage]] (道饗, road offerings) followed in the next month. While these were not inherently tied to the winter season, they were often celebrated as part of the winter festival period.

{{出典の明記|date=2017年2月8日 (水) 11:32 (UTC)}} {{画像募集中|cat=Images of Japan}} Winter festivals or '''Winter Matsuri''' (冬祭り) refer to [[festivals]] held during the [[winter]] season. These events typically occur over the three-month period from [[Rittō]] (the beginning of winter) to [[Risshun]] (the beginning of spring), corresponding to [[November]], [[December]], and [[January]].
== Overview == The **Winter Festival** incorporates both the **harvest festival elements** of the {{ill|Autumn Festival (Japan)|ja|秋祭り}} and the **prayer for the year's blessings elements** of the {{ill|Spring Festival (Japan)|ja|春祭り}}, making its exact positioning somewhat unclear. In ancient times, the **10th month of the old calendar** ({{ill|Kanazuki|ja|神無月}}) was considered an **unlucky month** ({{ill|Imizuki|ja|忌月}}).<ref>Although the unlucky months are commonly introduced as January, May, and September in the modern calendar, in the old calendar, they were roughly one month later, making the 10th month of the old calendar an unlucky month.</ref> Thus, the **harvest festival** was originally held in the **11th month of the old calendar**, after the month changed and winter began. However, as the **taboos** surrounding the 10th month faded, the **Autumn Festival, held immediately after the harvest**, became established.
{{ill|Orikuchi Shinobu|ja|折口信夫}} viewed this period, spanning from autumn to the following spring, as a time of **spiritual renewal** ({{ill|Tama furi|ja|魂振}}), during which all souls, including human ones, were refreshed. He theorized that the **Autumn Festival, Winter Festival, and Spring Festival** were originally **one continuous ritual cycle**, dealing with **harvest, soul renewal, and prayers for the coming year**. Over time, he suggested, the **Autumn and Spring Festivals split off**, leaving the remainder as the **Winter Festival**.
Similarly, {{ill|Yanagita Kunio|ja|柳田國男}} pointed out that the **11th month of the old calendar**, which includes the **Winter Solstice**, was a turning point toward spring. He argued that Japanese festivals traditionally placed great importance on this month.
In the {{ill|Jingikan|ja|神祇官}} (Department of Shinto Affairs) of the {{ill|Imperial Court of Japan|ja|朝廷 (日本)}}, the {{ill|Four Seasonal Festivals|ja|四時祭}} designated the **Ainame Festival** ({{ill|Ainame-sai|ja|相嘗祭}}), **Chinkon Festival** ({{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭}}), and **Niiname Festival** ({{ill|Niiname-sai|ja|新嘗祭}}) in the **11th month of the old calendar** as **Winter Festivals**. Additionally, although the **Tsukinami Festival** ({{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭}}), **Chinka (Fire-Calming Ritual)** ({{ill|Chinka|ja|鎮火}}), and **Dōkyō Festival** ({{ill|Dōkyō|ja|道饗}}) were not inherently winter-specific, they were conducted as part of the Winter Festival period.
Today, many **Winter Festivals** retain elements of the **Chinkon Festival (Ritual for Soul-Pacification)**, and practices such as **Yutate (Hot Water Purification Rituals)** ({{ill|Yutate|ja|湯立}}) and **Kagura (Sacred Dance and Music)** ({{ill|Kagura|ja|神楽}}), which originated from **Shimotsuki Kagura** ({{ill|Shimotsuki Kagura|ja|霜月神楽}}), are often performed.

{{出典の明記|date=2017年2月8日 (水) 11:32 (UTC)}} {{画像募集中|cat=Images of Japan}} Winter festivals or '''Winter Matsuri''' (冬祭り) refer to [[festivals]] held during the [[winter]] season. These events typically occur over the three-month period from [[Rittō]] (the beginning of winter) to [[Risshun]] (the beginning of spring), corresponding to [[November]], [[December]], and [[January]].
== Overview == The '''Winter Festival''' incorporates both the '''harvest festival elements''' of the {{ill|Autumn Festival (Japan)|ja|秋祭り}} and the '''prayer for the year's blessings elements''' of the {{ill|Spring Festival (Japan)|ja|春祭り}}, making its exact positioning somewhat unclear. In ancient times, the '''10th month of the old calendar''' ({{ill|Kanazuki|ja|神無月}}) was considered an '''unlucky month''' ({{ill|Imizuki|ja|忌月}}).<ref>Although the unlucky months are commonly introduced as January, May, and September in the modern calendar, in the old calendar, they were roughly one month later, making the 10th month of the old calendar an unlucky month.</ref> Thus, the '''harvest festival''' was originally held in the '''11th month of the old calendar''', after the month changed and winter began. However, as the '''taboos''' surrounding the 10th month faded, the '''Autumn Festival, held immediately after the harvest''', became established.
{{ill|Orikuchi Shinobu|ja|折口信夫}} viewed this period, spanning from autumn to the following spring, as a time of '''spiritual renewal''' ({{ill|Tama furi|ja|魂振}}), during which all souls, including human ones, were refreshed. He theorized that the '''Autumn Festival, Winter Festival, and Spring Festival''' were originally '''one continuous ritual cycle''', dealing with '''harvest, soul renewal, and prayers for the coming year'''. Over time, he suggested, the '''Autumn and Spring Festivals split off''', leaving the remainder as the '''Winter Festival'''.
Similarly, {{ill|Yanagita Kunio|ja|柳田國男}} pointed out that the '''11th month of the old calendar''', which includes the '''Winter Solstice''', was a turning point toward spring. He argued that Japanese festivals traditionally placed great importance on this month.
In the {{ill|Jingikan|ja|神祇官}} (Department of Shinto Affairs) of the {{ill|Imperial Court of Japan|ja|朝廷 (日本)}}, the {{ill|Four Seasonal Festivals|ja|四時祭}} designated the '''Ainame Festival''' ({{ill|Ainame Festival|ja|相嘗祭|lt=Ainame-sai|Ainame Festival=Ainame Festival}}), '''Chinkon Festival''' ({{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭}}), and '''Niiname Festival''' ({{ill|Niiname-sai|ja|新嘗祭}}) in the '''11th month of the old calendar''' as '''Winter Festivals'''. Additionally, although the '''Tsukinami Festival''' ({{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭}}), '''Chinka (Fire-Calming Ritual)''' ({{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|ja|鎮火|Fire-Pacification Festival=Fire-Pacification Festival|lt=Chinka}}), and '''Dōkyō Festival''' ({{ill|Dōkyō|ja|道饗}}) were not inherently winter-specific, they were conducted as part of the Winter Festival period.
Today, many '''Winter Festivals''' retain elements of the '''Chinkon Festival (Ritual for Soul-Pacification)''', and practices such as '''Yutate (Hot Water Purification Rituals)''' ({{ill|Yutate|ja|湯立}}) and '''Kagura (Sacred Dance and Music)''' ({{ill|Kagura|ja|神楽}}), which originated from '''Shimotsuki Kagura''' ({{ill|Shimotsuki Kagura|ja|霜月神楽}}), are often performed.

{{出典の明記|date=2017年2月8日 (水) 11:32 (UTC)}} [[File:Ako Gishisai De09 13.jpg|thumb]] 冬祭および'''冬祭り'''（ふゆまつり）とは[[冬]]に行われる[[祭り]]のことである。[[立冬]]から[[立春]]にかけての3か月間（[[11月]]・[[12月]]・[[1月]]）に行われるものを指す場合が多い。
== 概要 == 冬祭りは、[[秋祭り]]が持つ'''[[収穫祭]]的要素'''と、[[春祭り]]が持つ'''[[祈年祭]]的要素'''の両方を併せ持っており、その位置づけについては不明な部分も多い。古来、[[旧暦]]の[[10月 (旧暦)|10月]]は[[忌月]]とされ<ref>忌月は、新暦においては1月・5月・9月と紹介されるが、旧暦では約1か月づつ遅れるため旧暦の10月が忌月というのは正しい。</ref>、したがって月が変わって冬に突入した旧暦の[[11月 (旧暦)|11月]]に収穫祭を行ったが、後にその[[忌み|禁忌]]が失われて'''収穫直後の秋祭り'''が成立したともされる。
[[折口信夫]]は、秋から翌春にかけてのこの時期が、[[魂振]]、つまり鎮魂を行い、人間を含めた全ての魂が更新されるための時期として捉えた。さらに秋祭り・冬祭り・春祭りを、元来、収穫・魂の更新・祈年を扱う一続きの祭りであったとし、これから秋祭りと春祭りが分離し、残りが冬祭りとなったと説く。
また、[[柳田國男]]は、冬祭りの中心となる旧暦の11月が[[冬至]]を含む時期であり、春に向かう転機であることを指摘し、日本の祭りは本来、旧暦の11月を重要視したと説く。
[[朝廷 (日本)|朝廷]]の[[神祇官]]における[[四時祭]]では旧暦の11月の[[相嘗祭]]や[[鎮魂祭]]、[[新嘗祭]]が冬祭りとされ、翌月の[[月次祭]]や[[鎮火]]、[[道饗]]も、冬特有の祭事ではないものの冬祭りとして挙行されている。
現在、冬祭りとして行われるものは鎮魂祭の要素を残すものが多く、鎮魂祭の際に行われた[[霜月神楽]]を祖とする[[湯立]]や[[神楽]]などが行われることが多い。

{{出典の明記|date=2017年2月8日 (水) 11:32 (UTC)}} [[File:Ako Gishisai De09 13.jpg|thumb|[[赤穂義士祭]]]] 冬祭および'''冬祭り'''（ふゆまつり）とは[[冬]]に行われる[[祭り]]のことである。[[立冬]]から[[立春]]にかけての3か月間（[[11月]]・[[12月]]・[[1月]]）に行われるものを指す場合が多い。
== 概要 == 冬祭りは、[[秋祭り]]が持つ'''[[収穫祭]]的要素'''と、[[春祭り]]が持つ'''[[祈年祭]]的要素'''の両方を併せ持っており、その位置づけについては不明な部分も多い。古来、[[旧暦]]の[[10月 (旧暦)|10月]]は[[忌月]]とされ<ref>忌月は、新暦においては1月・5月・9月と紹介されるが、旧暦では約1か月づつ遅れるため旧暦の10月が忌月というのは正しい。</ref>、したがって月が変わって冬に突入した旧暦の[[11月 (旧暦)|11月]]に収穫祭を行ったが、後にその[[忌み|禁忌]]が失われて'''収穫直後の秋祭り'''が成立したともされる。
[[折口信夫]]は、秋から翌春にかけてのこの時期が、[[魂振]]、つまり鎮魂を行い、人間を含めた全ての魂が更新されるための時期として捉えた。さらに秋祭り・冬祭り・春祭りを、元来、収穫・魂の更新・祈年を扱う一続きの祭りであったとし、これから秋祭りと春祭りが分離し、残りが冬祭りとなったと説く。
また、[[柳田國男]]は、冬祭りの中心となる旧暦の11月が[[冬至]]を含む時期であり、春に向かう転機であることを指摘し、日本の祭りは本来、旧暦の11月を重要視したと説く。
[[朝廷 (日本)|朝廷]]の[[神祇官]]における[[四時祭]]では旧暦の11月の[[相嘗祭]]や[[鎮魂祭]]、[[新嘗祭]]が冬祭りとされ、翌月の[[月次祭]]や[[鎮火]]、[[道饗]]も、冬特有の祭事ではないものの冬祭りとして挙行されている。
現在、冬祭りとして行われるものは鎮魂祭の要素を残すものが多く、鎮魂祭の際に行われた[[霜月神楽]]を祖とする[[湯立]]や[[神楽]]などが行われることが多い。
== 脚注 == {{reflist}}
== 関連項目 == * [[春祭り]] * [[夏祭り]] * [[秋祭り]] * [[冬休み]] * [[ユール]] - 古代ヨーロッパの冬至の頃の祭り
{{Event-stub}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:ふゆまつり}} [[Category:日本の文化]] [[Category:日本の祭り]] [[Category:祭]] [[Category:冬のイベント]]

{{出典の明記|date=2017年2月8日 (水) 11:32 (UTC)}} [[File:Ako Gishisai De09 13.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Ako Goshi Festival|ja|赤穂義士祭}}]] Winter festivals or '''Winter Matsuri''' (冬祭り) refer to [[festivals]] held during the [[winter]] season. These events typically occur over the three-month period from [[Rittō]] (the beginning of winter) to [[Risshun]] (the beginning of spring), corresponding to [[November]], [[December]], and [[January]].
== Overview == The '''Winter Festival''' incorporates both the '''harvest festival elements''' of the {{ill|Autumn festival|ja|秋祭り}} and the '''prayer for the year's blessings elements''' of the {{ill|Spring festival|ja|春祭り}}, making its exact positioning somewhat unclear. In ancient times, the '''10th month of the old calendar''' ({{ill|Kanazuki|ja|神無月}}) was considered an '''unlucky month''' ({{ill|Imizuki|ja|忌月}}).<ref>Although the unlucky months are commonly introduced as January, May, and September in the modern calendar, in the old calendar, they were roughly one month later, making the 10th month of the old calendar an unlucky month.</ref> Thus, the '''harvest festival''' was originally held in the '''11th month of the old calendar''', after the month changed and winter began. However, as the '''taboos''' surrounding the 10th month faded, the '''Autumn Festival, held immediately after the harvest''', became established.
{{ill|Orikuchi Shinobu|ja|折口信夫}} viewed this period, spanning from autumn to the following spring, as a time of '''spiritual renewal''' ({{ill|Tama furi|ja|魂振}}), during which all souls, including human ones, were refreshed. He theorized that the '''Autumn Festival, Winter Festival, and Spring Festival''' were originally '''one continuous ritual cycle''', dealing with '''harvest, soul renewal, and prayers for the coming year'''. Over time, he suggested, the '''Autumn and Spring Festivals split off''', leaving the remainder as the '''Winter Festival'''.
Similarly, {{ill|Yanagita Kunio|ja|柳田國男}} pointed out that the '''11th month of the old calendar''', which includes the '''Winter Solstice''', was a turning point toward spring. He argued that Japanese festivals traditionally placed great importance on this month.
In the {{ill|Jingikan|ja|神祇官}} (Department of Shinto Affairs) of the {{ill|Imperial Court of Japan|ja|朝廷 (日本)}}, the {{ill|Four Seasonal Festivals|ja|四時祭}} designated the '''Ainame Festival''' ({{ill|Ainame Festival|ja|相嘗祭|lt=Ainame-sai|Ainame Festival=Ainame Festival}}), '''Chinkon Festival''' ({{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭}}), and '''Niiname Festival''' ({{ill|Niiname-sai|ja|新嘗祭}}) in the '''11th month of the old calendar''' as '''Winter Festivals'''. Additionally, although the '''Tsukinami Festival''' ({{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭}}), '''Chinka (Fire-Calming Ritual)''' ({{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|ja|鎮火|Fire-Pacification Festival=Fire-Pacification Festival|lt=Chinka}}), and '''Dōkyō Festival''' ({{ill|Dōkyō|ja|道饗}}) were not inherently winter-specific, they were conducted as part of the Winter Festival period.
Today, many '''Winter Festivals''' retain elements of the '''Chinkon Festival (Ritual for Soul-Pacification)''', and practices such as '''Yutate (Hot Water Purification Rituals)''' ({{ill|Yutate|ja|湯立}}) and '''Kagura (Sacred Dance and Music)''' ({{ill|Kagura|ja|神楽}}), which originated from '''Shimotsuki Kagura''' ({{ill|Shimotsuki Kagura|ja|霜月神楽}}), are often performed.
== Notes == {{reflist}}

* [[:en:Yule|Yule]] – Ancient European winter solstice celebrations

Over time, he suggested, the '''Autumn and Spring Festivals split off''', leaving the remainder as the '''Winter Festival'''.

In the {{ill|Jingikan|ja|神祇官}} (Department of Shinto Affairs) of the {{ill|Imperial Court of Japan|ja|朝廷 (日本)}}, the {{ill|Four Seasonal Festivals|ja|四時祭}} designated the '''Ainame Festival''' ({{ill|Ainame Festival|ja|相嘗祭|lt=Ainame-sai|Ainame Festival=Ainame Festival}}), '''Chinkon Festival''' ({{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭}}), and '''Niiname Festival''' ({{ill|Niiname-sai|ja|新嘗祭}}) in the '''11th month of the old calendar''' as '''Winter Festivals'''. Additionally, although the '''Tsukinami Festival''' ({{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭}}), '''Chinka (Fire-Calming Ritual)''' ({{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|ja|鎮火|Fire-Pacification Festival=Fire-Pacification Festival|lt=Chinka}}), and '''Dōkyō Festival''' ({{ill|Dōkyō|ja|道饗}}) were not inherently winter-specific, they were conducted as part of the Winter Festival period.
Today, many '''Winter Festivals''' retain elements of the '''Chinkon Festival (Ritual for Soul-Pacification)''', and practices such as '''Yutate (Hot Water Purification Rituals)''' ({{ill|Yutate|ja|湯立}}) and '''Kagura (Sacred Dance and Music)''' ({{ill|Kagura|ja|神楽}}), which originated from '''Shimotsuki Kagura''' ({{ill|Shimotsuki Kagura|ja|霜月神楽}}), are often performed.

revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2017年2月8日 (水) 11:32 (UTC)}} [[File:Ako Gishisai De09 13.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Ako Goshi Festival|ja|赤穂義士祭}}]] Winter festivals or '''Winter Matsuri''' (冬祭り) refer to [[festivals]] held during the [[winter]] season. These events typically occur over the three-month period from [[:en:Lidong|Rittō]] (the beginning of winter) to [[Risshun]] (the beginning of spring), corresponding to [[November]], [[December]], and [[January]].
== Overview == The '''Winter Festival''' incorporates both the '''harvest festival elements''' of the {{ill|Autumn festival|ja|秋祭り}} and the '''prayer for the year's blessings elements''' of the {{ill|Spring festival|ja|春祭り}}, making its exact positioning somewhat unclear. In ancient times, the '''10th month of the old calendar''' ({{ill|Kanazuki|ja|神無月}}) was considered an '''unlucky month''' ({{ill|Imizuki|ja|忌月}}).<ref>Although the unlucky months are commonly introduced as January, May, and September in the modern calendar, in the old calendar, they were roughly one month later, making the 10th month of the old calendar an unlucky month.</ref> Thus, the '''harvest festival''' was originally held in the '''11th month of the old calendar''', after the month changed and winter began. However, as the '''taboos''' surrounding the 10th month faded, the '''Autumn Festival, held immediately after the harvest''', became established.
{{ill|Orikuchi Shinobu|ja|折口信夫}} viewed this period, spanning from autumn to the following spring, as a time of '''spiritual renewal''' ({{ill|Chinkon|ja|鎮魂}}), during which all souls, including human ones, were refreshed. He theorized that the '''Autumn Festival, Winter Festival, and Spring Festival''' were originally '''one continuous ritual cycle''', dealing with '''harvest, soul renewal, and prayers for the coming year'''. Over time, he suggested, the '''Autumn and Spring Festivals split off''', leaving the remainder as the '''Winter Festival'''.
Similarly, {{ill|Yanagita Kunio|ja|柳田國男}} pointed out that the '''11th month of the old calendar''', which includes the '''Winter Solstice''', was a turning point toward spring. He argued that Japanese festivals traditionally placed great importance on this month.
In the {{ill|Jingikan|ja|神祇官}} (Department of Shinto Affairs) of the {{ill|Imperial Court of Japan|ja|朝廷 (日本)}}, the {{ill|Four Seasonal Festivals|ja|四時祭}} designated the '''Ainame Festival''' ({{ill|Ainame Festival|ja|相嘗祭|lt=Ainame-sai|Ainame Festival=Ainame Festival}}), '''Chinkon Festival''' ({{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭}}), and '''Niiname Festival''' ({{ill|Niiname-sai|ja|新嘗祭}}) in the '''11th month of the old calendar''' as '''Winter Festivals'''. Additionally, although the '''Tsukinami Festival''' ({{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭}}), '''Chinka (Fire-Calming Ritual)''' ({{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|ja|鎮火|Fire-Pacification Festival=Fire-Pacification Festival|lt=Chinka}}), and '''Dōkyō Festival''' ({{ill|Dōkyō|ja|道饗}}) were not inherently winter-specific, they were conducted as part of the Winter Festival period.
Today, many '''Winter Festivals''' retain elements of the '''Chinkon Festival (Ritual for Soul-Pacification)''', and practices such as '''Yutate (Hot Water Purification Rituals)''' ({{ill|Yutate|ja|湯立}}) and '''Kagura (Sacred Dance and Music)''' ({{ill|Kagura|ja|神楽}}), which originated from '''Shimotsuki Kagura''' ({{ill|Shimotsuki Kagura|ja|霜月神楽}}), are often performed.
== Notes == {{reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Spring Festivals|en}} * [[Summer festivals]] * {{ill|Autumn Festivals|en}} * [[:en:Winter_break|Winter break]] * {{DEFAULTSORT:Fuyumatsuri}} [[Category:Japanese culture]] [[Category:Festivals in Japan]] [[Category:Festivals]] [[Category:Winter events]]
* [[:en:Yule|Yule]] – Ancient European winter solstice celebrations
[[Category:new pages]] [[ja:冬祭り]] * {{ill|Tanabata|en}} [[Category:new pages]] [[ja:七夕]] * {{ill|Community building|en}} * [[Category:new pages]] [[ja:地域おこし]] [[Category:new pages]] [[ja:帰省]] [[Category:new pages]] [[ja:夏休み]]

revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2017年2月8日 (水) 11:32 (UTC)}} [[File:Ako Gishisai De09 13.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Ako Goshi Festival|ja|赤穂義士祭}}]] Winter festivals or '''Winter Matsuri''' (冬祭り) refer to [[festivals]] held during the [[winter]] season. These events typically occur over the three-month period from [[:en:Lidong|Rittō]] (the beginning of winter) to [[Risshun]] (the beginning of spring), corresponding to [[November]], [[December]], and [[January]].
== Overview == The '''Winter Festival''' incorporates both the '''harvest festival elements''' of the {{ill|Autumn festival|ja|秋祭り}} and the '''prayer for the year's blessings elements''' of the {{ill|Spring festival|ja|春祭り}}, making its exact positioning somewhat unclear. In ancient times, the '''10th month of the old calendar''' ({{ill|Kanazuki|ja|神無月}}) was considered an '''unlucky month''' ({{ill|Imizuki|ja|忌月}}).<ref>Although the unlucky months are commonly introduced as January, May, and September in the modern calendar, in the old calendar, they were roughly one month later, making the 10th month of the old calendar an unlucky month.</ref> Thus, the '''harvest festival''' was originally held in the '''11th month of the old calendar''', after the month changed and winter began. However, as the '''taboos''' surrounding the 10th month faded, the '''Autumn Festival, held immediately after the harvest''', became established.
{{ill|Orikuchi Shinobu|ja|折口信夫}} viewed this period, spanning from autumn to the following spring, as a time of '''spiritual renewal''' ({{ill|Chinkon|ja|鎮魂}}), during which all souls, including human ones, were refreshed. He theorized that the '''Autumn Festival, Winter Festival, and Spring Festival''' were originally '''one continuous ritual cycle''', dealing with '''harvest, soul renewal, and prayers for the coming year'''. Over time, he suggested, the '''Autumn and Spring Festivals split off''', leaving the remainder as the '''Winter Festival'''.
Similarly, {{ill|Yanagita Kunio|ja|柳田國男}} pointed out that the '''11th month of the old calendar''', which includes the '''Winter Solstice''', was a turning point toward spring. He argued that Japanese festivals traditionally placed great importance on this month.
In the {{ill|Jingikan|ja|神祇官}} (Department of Shinto Affairs) of the {{ill|Imperial Court of Japan|ja|朝廷 (日本)}}, the {{ill|Four Seasonal Festivals|ja|四時祭}} designated the '''Ainame Festival''' ({{ill|Ainame Festival|ja|相嘗祭|lt=Ainame-sai|Ainame Festival=Ainame Festival}}), '''Chinkon Festival''' ({{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭}}), and '''Niiname Festival''' ({{ill|Niiname-sai|ja|新嘗祭}}) in the '''11th month of the old calendar''' as '''Winter Festivals'''. Additionally, although the '''Tsukinami Festival''' ({{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭}}), '''Chinka (Fire-Calming Ritual)''' ({{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|ja|鎮火|Fire-Pacification Festival=Fire-Pacification Festival|lt=Chinka}}), and '''Dōkyō Festival''' ({{ill|Dōkyō|ja|道饗}}) were not inherently winter-specific, they were conducted as part of the Winter Festival period.
Today, many '''Winter Festivals''' retain elements of the '''Chinkon Festival (Ritual for Soul-Pacification)''', and practices such as '''Yutate (Hot Water Purification Rituals)''' ({{ill|Yutate|ja|湯立}}) and '''Kagura (Sacred Dance and Music)''' ({{ill|Kagura|ja|神楽}}), which originated from '''Shimotsuki Kagura''' ({{ill|Shimotsuki Kagura|ja|霜月神楽}}), are often performed.
== Notes == {{reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Spring Festivals|en}} * [[Summer festivals]] * {{ill|Autumn Festivals|en}} * [[:en:Winter_break|Winter break]] * {{DEFAULTSORT:Fuyumatsuri}} [[Category:Japanese culture]] [[Category:Festivals in Japan]] [[Category:Festivals]] [[Category:Winter events]]
* [[:en:Yule|Yule]] – Ancient European winter solstice celebrations
[[Category:New pages]] [[Category:Pages needing attribution]] [[Category:nnn]] [[Category:Winter Festivals]][[Category:Pages needing attribution]] [[Category:nnn]] [[ja:冬祭り]] * {{ill|Tanabata|en}} [[Category:New pages]] [[Category:Pages needing attribution]] [[Category:nnn]] [[Category:Winter Festivals]][[Category:Pages needing attribution]] [[Category:nnn]] [[ja:七夕]] * {{ill|Community building|en}} * [[Category:New pages]] [[Category:Pages needing attribution]] [[Category:nnn]] [[Category:Winter Festivals]][[Category:Pages needing attribution]] [[Category:nnn]] [[ja:地域おこし]] [[Category:New pages]] [[Category:Pages needing attribution]] [[Category:nnn]] [[Category:Winter Festivals]][[Category:Pages needing attribution]] [[Category:nnn]] [[ja:帰省]] [[Category:New pages]] [[Category:Pages needing attribution]] [[Category:nnn]] [[Category:Winter Festivals]][[Category:Pages needing attribution]] [[Category:nnn]] [[ja:夏休み]]

revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2017年2月8日 (水) 11:32 (UTC)}} [[File:Ako Gishisai De09 13.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Ako Goshi Festival|ja|赤穂義士祭}}]] Winter festivals or '''Winter Matsuri''' (冬祭り) refer to [[festivals]] held during the [[winter]] season. These events typically occur over the three-month period from [[:en:Lidong|Rittō]] (the beginning of winter) to [[Risshun]] (the beginning of spring), corresponding to [[November]], [[December]], and [[January]].
== Overview == The '''Winter Festival''' incorporates both the '''harvest festival elements''' of the {{ill|Autumn festival|ja|秋祭り}} and the '''prayer for the year's blessings elements''' of the {{ill|Spring festival|ja|春祭り}}, making its exact positioning somewhat unclear. In ancient times, the '''10th month of the old calendar''' ({{ill|Kanazuki|ja|神無月}}) was considered an '''unlucky month''' ({{ill|Imizuki|ja|忌月}}).<ref>Although the unlucky months are commonly introduced as January, May, and September in the modern calendar, in the old calendar, they were roughly one month later, making the 10th month of the old calendar an unlucky month.</ref> Thus, the '''harvest festival''' was originally held in the '''11th month of the old calendar''', after the month changed and winter began. However, as the '''taboos''' surrounding the 10th month faded, the '''Autumn Festival, held immediately after the harvest''', became established.
{{ill|Orikuchi Shinobu|ja|折口信夫}} viewed this period, spanning from autumn to the following spring, as a time of '''spiritual renewal''' ({{ill|Chinkon|ja|鎮魂}}), during which all souls, including human ones, were refreshed. He theorized that the '''Autumn Festival, Winter Festival, and Spring Festival''' were originally '''one continuous ritual cycle''', dealing with '''harvest, soul renewal, and prayers for the coming year'''. Over time, he suggested, the '''Autumn and Spring Festivals split off''', leaving the remainder as the '''Winter Festival'''.
Similarly, {{ill|Yanagita Kunio|ja|柳田國男}} pointed out that the '''11th month of the old calendar''', which includes the '''Winter Solstice''', was a turning point toward spring. He argued that Japanese festivals traditionally placed great importance on this month.
In the {{ill|Jingikan|ja|神祇官}} (Department of Shinto Affairs) of the {{ill|Imperial Court of Japan|ja|朝廷 (日本)}}, the {{ill|Four Seasonal Festivals|ja|四時祭}} designated the '''Ainame Festival''' ({{ill|Ainame Festival|ja|相嘗祭|lt=Ainame-sai|Ainame Festival=Ainame Festival}}), '''Chinkon Festival''' ({{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭}}), and '''Niiname Festival''' ({{ill|Niiname-sai|ja|新嘗祭}}) in the '''11th month of the old calendar''' as '''Winter Festivals'''. Additionally, although the '''Tsukinami Festival''' ({{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭}}), '''Chinka (Fire-Calming Ritual)''' ({{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|ja|鎮火|Fire-Pacification Festival=Fire-Pacification Festival|lt=Chinka}}), and '''Dōkyō Festival''' ({{ill|Dōkyō|ja|道饗}}) were not inherently winter-specific, they were conducted as part of the Winter Festival period.
Today, many '''Winter Festivals''' retain elements of the '''Chinkon Festival (Ritual for Soul-Pacification)''', and practices such as '''Yutate (Hot Water Purification Rituals)''' ({{ill|Yutate|ja|湯立}}) and '''Kagura (Sacred Dance and Music)''' ({{ill|Kagura|ja|神楽}}), which originated from '''Shimotsuki Kagura''' ({{ill|Shimotsuki Kagura|ja|霜月神楽}}), are often performed.
== Notes == {{reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Spring Festivals|en}} * [[Summer festivals]] * {{ill|Autumn Festivals|en}} * [[:en:Winter_break|Winter break]] * {{DEFAULTSORT:Fuyumatsuri}} [[Category:Japanese culture]] [[Category:Festivals in Japan]] [[Category:Festivals]] [[Category:Winter events]]
* [[:en:Yule|Yule]] – Ancient European winter solstice celebrations

[[Category:nnn]] [[Category:Winter Festivals]][[Category:nnn]] [[ja:夏祭り]] [[category:Summer festivals forks]] [[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Winter Festivals]][[Category:properly attributed pages]]

{{出典の明記|date=2017年2月8日 (水) 11:32 (UTC)}} [[File:Ako Gishisai De09 13.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Ako Goshi Festival|ja|赤穂義士祭}}]] Winter festivals or '''Winter Matsuri''' (冬祭り) refer to [[festivals]] held during the [[winter]] season. These events typically occur over the three-month period from [[:en:Lidong|Rittō]] (the beginning of winter) to [[Risshun]] (the beginning of spring), corresponding to [[November]], [[December]], and [[January]].
== Overview == The '''Winter Festival''' incorporates both the '''harvest festival elements''' of the {{ill|Autumn festival|ja|秋祭り}} and the '''prayer for the year's blessings elements''' of the {{ill|Spring festival|ja|春祭り}}, making its exact positioning somewhat unclear. In ancient times, the '''10th month of the old calendar''' ({{ill|Kannazuki|ja|神無月}}) was considered an '''unlucky month''' ({{ill|Imizuki|ja|忌月}}).<ref>Although the unlucky months are commonly introduced as January, May, and September in the modern calendar, in the old calendar, they were roughly one month later, making the 10th month of the old calendar an unlucky month.</ref> Thus, the '''harvest festival''' was originally held in the '''11th month of the old calendar''', after the month changed and winter began. However, as the '''taboos''' surrounding the 10th month faded, the '''Autumn Festival, held immediately after the harvest''', became established.
{{ill|Orikuchi Shinobu|ja|折口信夫}} viewed this period, spanning from autumn to the following spring, as a time of '''spiritual renewal''' ({{ill|Chinkon|ja|鎮魂}}), during which all souls, including human ones, were refreshed. He theorized that the '''Autumn Festival, Winter Festival, and Spring Festival''' were originally '''one continuous ritual cycle''', dealing with '''harvest, soul renewal, and prayers for the coming year'''. Over time, he suggested, the '''Autumn and Spring Festivals split off''', leaving the remainder as the '''Winter Festival'''.
Similarly, {{ill|Yanagita Kunio|ja|柳田國男}} pointed out that the '''11th month of the old calendar''', which includes the '''Winter Solstice''', was a turning point toward spring. He argued that Japanese festivals traditionally placed great importance on this month.
In the {{ill|Jingikan|ja|神祇官}} (Department of Shinto Affairs) of the {{ill|Imperial Court of Japan|ja|朝廷 (日本)}}, the {{ill|Four Seasonal Festivals|ja|四時祭}} designated the '''Ainame Festival''' ({{ill|Ainame Festival|ja|相嘗祭|lt=Ainame-sai|Ainame Festival=Ainame Festival}}), '''Chinkon Festival''' ({{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭}}), and '''Niiname Festival''' ({{ill|Niiname-sai|ja|新嘗祭}}) in the '''11th month of the old calendar''' as '''Winter Festivals'''. Additionally, although the '''Tsukinami Festival''' ({{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭}}), '''Chinka (Fire-Calming Ritual)''' ({{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|ja|鎮火|Fire-Pacification Festival=Fire-Pacification Festival|lt=Chinka}}), and '''Dōkyō Festival''' ({{ill|Dōkyō|ja|道饗}}) were not inherently winter-specific, they were conducted as part of the Winter Festival period.
Today, many '''Winter Festivals''' retain elements of the '''Chinkon Festival (Ritual for Soul-Pacification)''', and practices such as '''Yutate (Hot Water Purification Rituals)''' ({{ill|Yutate|ja|湯立}}) and '''Kagura (Sacred Dance and Music)''' ({{ill|Kagura|ja|神楽}}), which originated from '''Shimotsuki Kagura''' ({{ill|Shimotsuki Kagura|ja|霜月神楽}}), are often performed.

revision.text:
[[File:Aoi Matsuri.jpg|thumb|230px|{{ill|Aoi Matsuri|ja|葵祭}} (the {{ill|Reisai|ja|例祭}} of {{ill|Kamo Shrine|ja|賀茂御祖神社}} and {{ill|Kamo-wakeikazuchi Shrine|ja|賀茂別雷神社}})]] [[File:神楽台 (岐阜県高山市) - panoramio (2).jpg|thumb|230px|{{ill|Hie Shrine (Takayama)|ja|日枝神社 (高山市)}} Reisai "Haru no Sannō Matsuri" (part of {{ill|Takayama Festival|ja|高山祭}})]] [[File:はやし屋台.JPG|thumb|230px|{{ill|Hidaka Shrine|ja|日高神社}} Reisai (part of {{ill|Hidaka Hibuse Festival|ja|日高火防祭り}})]] [[File:Okunitama-jinja-30.jpg|thumb|230px|{{ill|Okunitama Shrine|ja|大國魂神社}} Reisai (part of {{ill|Kurayami Festival|ja|くらやみ祭}})]]
'''Haru Matsuri''' (春祭)<ref name="Daijisen">『{{ill|Daijisen|ja|大辞泉}}』</ref><ref>Heibonsha 『{{ill|MyPedia|ja|マイペディア}}』</ref><ref>Heibonsha 『{{ill|Sekai Daihyakka Jiten|ja|世界大百科事典}}』, 2nd Edition</ref><ref>Shogakukan 『{{ill|Nipponica|ja|日本大百科全書}}』</ref>, also called '''Spring Festival''' or '''Haru no Matsuri''' (はるまつり), is a general term for {{ill|festivals|ja|祭}} held in the {{ill|spring|ja|春}}.
== Seasonal Word == As a {{ill|kigo|ja|季語}}, the term '''Haru Matsuri''' (春祭り) represents a seasonal word for spring (either for {{ill|early spring|ja|初春}} or the three months of spring, depending on the {{ill|saijiki|ja|歳時記}}).<ref name="Kigosai">{{Cite web|和書|date=2011-02-16 |url=http://kigosai.sub.jp/kigo500e/341.html |title=春祭 |work=Kigosai Saijiki |publisher=Kigosai Saijiki Association |accessdate=2018-02-17}}</ref> It falls under the classification of {{ill|events|ja|行事}} or human affairs.<ref>"Events" and "human affairs" refer here to activities performed by humans.</ref> In terms of seasonal words, it encompasses festivals held during the spring season.<ref name="Kigosai" /> {{quotation| * Example: The '''Haru Matsuri''' begins with the sprouting of old mulberries – {{ill|Shūson Mizuhara|ja|水原秋桜子}}<ref name="Daijisen" /> * Example: '''Haru Matsuri''' beneath the mountains, few fresh monpe in sight – {{ill|Hakyō Ishida|ja|石田波郷}} * Example: Valleys open to the sight of Norikura at '''Haru Matsuri''' – {{ill|Fura Maeda|ja|前田普羅}} 『Hida Tsumugi』 (published 1947)<ref name="Kigosai" /> * Example: Shellfish alive in a tank at '''Haru Matsuri''' – Yaehiro Hiroe 『Chikuma Literary Anthology』<ref name="Kigosai" /> }} There are no sub-seasonal words for it. While no directly related seasonal words are listed, many spring festivals, such as the {{ill|Kinensai|ja|祈年祭}}, are seasonal words, which naturally relate to Haru Matsuri.
== Notes and References == {{Reflist}}

Shinkyoku Darake no Haru Matsuri』

{{出典の明記|date=2017年2月8日 (水) 11:32 (UTC)}} [[File:Ako Gishisai De09 13.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Ako Goshi Festival|ja|赤穂義士祭}}]] Winter festivals or '''Winter Matsuri''' (冬祭り) refer to [[festivals]] held during the [[winter]] season. These events typically occur over the three-month period from [[:en:Lidong|Rittō]] (the beginning of winter) to [[Risshun]] (the beginning of spring), corresponding to [[November]], [[December]], and [[January]].
== Overview == The '''Winter Festival''' incorporates both the '''harvest festival elements''' of the {{ill|Autumn festival|ja|秋祭り}} and the '''prayer for the year's blessings elements''' of the {{ill|Spring festival|ja|春祭り}}, making its exact positioning somewhat unclear. In ancient times, the '''10th month of the old calendar''' ({{ill|Kannazuki|ja|神無月}}) was considered an '''unlucky month''' ({{ill|Imizuki|ja|忌月}}).<ref>Although the unlucky months are commonly introduced as January, May, and September in the modern calendar, in the old calendar, they were roughly one month later, making the 10th month of the old calendar an unlucky month.</ref> Thus, the '''harvest festival''' was originally held in the '''11th month of the old calendar''', after the month changed and winter began. However, as the '''taboos''' surrounding the 10th month faded, the '''Autumn Festival, held immediately after the harvest''', became established.
{{ill|Orikuchi Shinobu|ja|折口信夫}} viewed this period, spanning from autumn to the following spring, as a time of '''spiritual renewal''' ({{ill|Chinkon|ja|鎮魂}}), during which all souls, including human ones, were refreshed. He theorized that the '''Autumn Festival, Winter Festival, and Spring Festival''' were originally '''one continuous ritual cycle''', dealing with '''harvest, soul renewal, and prayers for the coming year'''. Over time, he suggested, the '''Autumn and Spring Festivals split off''', leaving the remainder as the '''Winter Festival'''.
Similarly, {{ill|Yanagita Kunio|ja|柳田國男}} pointed out that the '''11th month of the old calendar''', which includes the '''Winter Solstice''', was a turning point toward spring. He argued that Japanese festivals traditionally placed great importance on this month.
In the {{ill|Jingikan|ja|神祇官}} (Department of Shinto Affairs) of the {{ill|Imperial Court of Japan|ja|朝廷 (日本)}}, the {{ill|Four Seasonal Festivals|ja|四時祭}} designated the '''Ainame Festival''' ({{ill|Ainame Festival|ja|相嘗祭|lt=Ainame-sai|Ainame Festival=Ainame Festival}}), '''Chinkon Festival''' ({{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭}}), and '''Niiname Festival''' ({{ill|Niiname-sai|ja|新嘗祭}}) in the '''11th month of the old calendar''' as '''Winter Festivals'''. Additionally, although the '''Tsukinami Festival''' ({{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭}}), '''Chinka (Fire-Calming Ritual)''' ({{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|ja|鎮火|Fire-Pacification Festival=Fire-Pacification Festival|lt=Chinka}}), and '''Dōkyō Festival''' ({{ill|Dōkyō|ja|道饗}}) were not inherently winter-specific, they were conducted as part of the Winter Festival period.
Today, many '''Winter Festivals''' retain elements of the '''Chinkon Festival (Ritual for Soul-Pacification)''', and practices such as '''Yutate (Hot Water Purification Rituals)''' ({{ill|Yutate|ja|湯立}}) and '''Kagura (Sacred Dance and Music)''' ({{ill|Kagura|ja|神楽}}), which originated from '''Shimotsuki Kagura''' ({{ill|Shimotsuki Kagura|ja|霜月神楽}}), are often performed.

{{出典の明記|date=2017年2月8日 (水) 11:32 (UTC)}} [[File:Ako Gishisai De09 13.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Ako Goshi Festival|ja|赤穂義士祭}}]] Winter festivals or '''Winter Matsuri''' (冬祭り) refer to [[festivals]] held during the [[winter]] season. These events typically occur over the three-month period from [[:en:Lidong|Rittō]] (the beginning of winter) to [[Risshun]] (the beginning of spring), corresponding to [[November]], [[December]], and [[January]].
== Overview == The '''Winter Festival''' incorporates both the '''harvest festival elements''' of the {{ill|Autumn festival|ja|秋祭り}} and the '''prayer for the year's blessings elements''' of the {{ill|Spring festival|ja|春祭り}}, making its exact positioning somewhat unclear. In ancient times, the '''10th month of the old calendar''' ({{ill|Kannazuki|ja|神無月}}) was considered an '''unlucky month''' ({{ill|Imizuki|ja|忌月}}).<ref>Although the unlucky months are commonly introduced as January, May, and September in the modern calendar, in the old calendar, they were roughly one month later, making the 10th month of the old calendar an unlucky month.</ref> Thus, the '''harvest festival''' was originally held in the '''11th month of the old calendar''', after the month changed and winter began. However, as the '''taboos''' surrounding the 10th month faded, the '''Autumn Festival, held immediately after the harvest''', became established.
{{ill|Orikuchi Shinobu|ja|折口信夫}} viewed this period, spanning from autumn to the following spring, as a time of '''spiritual renewal''' ({{ill|Chinkon|ja|鎮魂}}), during which all souls, including human ones, were refreshed. He theorized that the '''Autumn Festival, Winter Festival, and Spring Festival''' were originally '''one continuous ritual cycle''', dealing with '''harvest, soul renewal, and prayers for the coming year'''. Over time, he suggested, the '''Autumn and Spring Festivals split off''', leaving the remainder as the '''Winter Festival'''.
Similarly, {{ill|Yanagita Kunio|ja|柳田國男}} pointed out that the '''11th month of the old calendar''', which includes the '''Winter Solstice''', was a turning point toward spring. He argued that Japanese festivals traditionally placed great importance on this month.
In the {{ill|Jingikan|ja|神祇官}} (Department of Shinto Affairs) of the {{ill|Imperial Court of Japan|ja|朝廷 (日本)}}, the {{ill|Four Seasonal Festivals|ja|四時祭}} designated the '''Ainame Festival''' ({{ill|Ainame Festival|ja|相嘗祭|lt=Ainame-sai|Ainame Festival=Ainame Festival}}), '''Chinkon Festival''' ({{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭}}), and '''Niiname Festival''' ({{ill|Niiname-sai|ja|新嘗祭}}) in the '''11th month of the old calendar''' as '''Winter Festivals'''. Additionally, although the '''Tsukinami Festival''' ({{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭}}), '''Chinka (Fire-Calming Ritual)''' ({{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|ja|鎮火|Fire-Pacification Festival=Fire-Pacification Festival|lt=Chinka}}), and '''Dōkyō Festival''' ({{ill|Dōkyō|ja|道饗}}) were not inherently winter-specific, they were conducted as part of the Winter Festival period.
Today, many '''Winter Festivals''' retain elements of the '''Chinkon Festival (Ritual for Soul-Pacification)''', and practices such as '''Yutate (Hot Water Purification Rituals)''' ({{ill|Yutate|ja|湯立}}) and '''Kagura (Sacred Dance and Music)''' ({{ill|Kagura|ja|神楽}}), which originated from '''Shimotsuki Kagura''' ({{ill|Shimotsuki Kagura|ja|霜月神楽}}), are often performed.

{{出典の明記|date=2017年2月8日 (水) 11:32 (UTC)}} [[File:Ako Gishisai De09 13.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Ako Goshi Festival|ja|赤穂義士祭}}]] Winter festivals or '''Winter Matsuri''' (冬祭り) refer to [[festivals]] held during the [[winter]] season. These events typically occur over the three-month period from [[:en:Lidong|Rittō]] (the beginning of winter) to [[Risshun]] (the beginning of spring), corresponding to [[November]], [[December]], and [[January]].
== Overview == The '''Winter Festival''' incorporates both the '''harvest festival elements''' of the {{ill|Autumn festival|ja|秋祭り}} and the '''prayer for the year's blessings elements''' of the {{ill|Spring festival|ja|春祭り}}, making its exact positioning somewhat unclear. In ancient times, the '''10th month of the old calendar''' ({{ill|Kannazuki|ja|神無月}}) was considered an '''unlucky month''' ({{ill|Imizuki|ja|忌月}}).<ref>Although the unlucky months are commonly introduced as January, May, and September in the modern calendar, in the old calendar, they were roughly one month later, making the 10th month of the old calendar an unlucky month.</ref> Thus, the '''harvest festival''' was originally held in the '''11th month of the old calendar''', after the month changed and winter began. However, as the '''taboos''' surrounding the 10th month faded, the '''Autumn Festival, held immediately after the harvest''', became established.
{{ill|Orikuchi Shinobu|ja|折口信夫}} viewed this period, spanning from autumn to the following spring, as a time of '''spiritual renewal''' ({{ill|Chinkon|ja|鎮魂}}), during which all souls, including human ones, were refreshed. He theorized that the '''Autumn Festival, Winter Festival, and Spring Festival''' were originally '''one continuous ritual cycle''', dealing with '''harvest, soul renewal, and prayers for the coming year'''. Over time, he suggested, the '''Autumn and Spring Festivals split off''', leaving the remainder as the '''Winter Festival'''.
Similarly, {{ill|Yanagita Kunio|ja|柳田國男}} pointed out that the '''11th month of the old calendar''', which includes the '''Winter Solstice''', was a turning point toward spring. He argued that Japanese festivals traditionally placed great importance on this month.
In the {{ill|Jingikan|ja|神祇官}} (Department of Shinto Affairs) of the {{ill|Imperial Court of Japan|ja|朝廷 (日本)}}, the {{ill|Four Seasonal Festivals|ja|四時祭}} designated the '''Ainame Festival''' ({{ill|Ainame Festival|ja|相嘗祭|lt=Ainame-sai|Ainame Festival=Ainame Festival}}), '''Chinkon Festival''' ({{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭}}), and '''Niiname Festival''' ({{ill|Niiname-sai|ja|新嘗祭}}) in the '''11th month of the old calendar''' as '''Winter Festivals'''. Additionally, although the '''Tsukinami Festival''' ({{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭}}), '''Chinka (Fire-Calming Ritual)''' ({{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|ja|鎮火|Fire-Pacification Festival=Fire-Pacification Festival|lt=Chinka}}), and '''Dōkyō Festival''' ({{ill|Dōkyō|ja|道饗}}) were not inherently winter-specific, they were conducted as part of the Winter Festival period.
Today, many '''Winter Festivals''' retain elements of the '''Chinkon Festival (Ritual for Soul-Pacification)''', and practices such as '''Yutate (Hot Water Purification Rituals)''' ({{ill|Yutate|ja|湯立}}) and '''Kagura (Sacred Dance and Music)''' ({{ill|Kagura|ja|神楽}}), which originated from '''Shimotsuki Kagura''' ({{ill|Shimotsuki Kagura|ja|霜月神楽}}), are often performed.

{{出典の明記|date=2017年2月8日 (水) 11:32 (UTC)}} [[File:Ako Gishisai De09 13.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Ako Goshi Festival|ja|赤穂義士祭}}]] Winter festivals or '''Winter Matsuri''' (冬祭り) refer to [[festivals]] held during the [[winter]] season. These events typically occur over the three-month period from [[:en:Lidong|Rittō]] (the beginning of winter) to [[Risshun]] (the beginning of spring), corresponding to [[November]], [[December]], and [[January]].
== Overview == The '''Winter Festival''' incorporates both the '''harvest festival elements''' of the {{ill|Autumn festival|ja|秋祭り}} and the '''prayer for the year's blessings elements''' of the {{ill|Spring festival|ja|春祭り}}, making its exact positioning somewhat unclear. In ancient times, the '''10th month of the old calendar''' ({{ill|Kannazuki|ja|神無月}}) was considered an '''unlucky month''' ({{ill|Imizuki|ja|忌月}}).<ref>Although the unlucky months are commonly introduced as January, May, and September in the modern calendar, in the old calendar, they were roughly one month later, making the 10th month of the old calendar an unlucky month.</ref> Thus, the '''harvest festival''' was originally held in the '''11th month of the old calendar''', after the month changed and winter began. However, as the '''taboos''' surrounding the 10th month faded, the '''Autumn Festival, held immediately after the harvest''', became established.
{{ill|Orikuchi Shinobu|ja|折口信夫}} viewed this period, spanning from autumn to the following spring, as a time of '''spiritual renewal''' ({{ill|Chinkon|ja|鎮魂}}), during which all souls, including human ones, were refreshed. He theorized that the '''Autumn Festival, Winter Festival, and Spring Festival''' were originally '''one continuous ritual cycle''', dealing with '''harvest, soul renewal, and prayers for the coming year'''. Over time, he suggested, the '''Autumn and Spring Festivals split off''', leaving the remainder as the '''Winter Festival'''.
Similarly, {{ill|Yanagita Kunio|ja|柳田國男}} pointed out that the '''11th month of the old calendar''', which includes the '''Winter Solstice''', was a turning point toward spring. He argued that Japanese festivals traditionally placed great importance on this month.
In the {{ill|Jingikan|ja|神祇官}} (Department of Shinto Affairs) of the {{ill|Imperial Court of Japan|ja|朝廷 (日本)}}, the {{ill|Four Seasonal Festivals|ja|四時祭}} designated the '''Ainame Festival''' ({{ill|Ainame Festival|ja|相嘗祭|lt=Ainame-sai|Ainame Festival=Ainame Festival}}), '''Chinkon Festival''' ({{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭}}), and '''Niiname Festival''' ({{ill|Niiname-sai|ja|新嘗祭}}) in the '''11th month of the old calendar''' as '''Winter Festivals'''. Additionally, although the '''Tsukinami Festival''' ({{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭}}), '''Chinka (Fire-Calming Ritual)''' ({{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|ja|鎮火|Fire-Pacification Festival=Fire-Pacification Festival|lt=Chinka}}), and '''Dōkyō Festival''' ({{ill|Dōkyō|ja|道饗}}) were not inherently winter-specific, they were conducted as part of the Winter Festival period.
Today, many '''Winter Festivals''' retain elements of the '''Chinkon Festival (Ritual for Soul-Pacification)''', and practices such as '''Yutate (Hot Water Purification Rituals)''' ({{ill|Yutate|ja|湯立}}) and '''Kagura (Sacred Dance and Music)''' ({{ill|Kagura|ja|神楽}}), which originated from '''Shimotsuki Kagura''' ({{ill|Shimotsuki Kagura|ja|霜月神楽}}), are often performed.
== Notes == {{reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Spring Festivals|en}} * [[Summer festivals]] * {{ill|Autumn Festivals|en}} * [[:en:Winter_break|Winter break]] * {{DEFAULTSORT:Fuyumatsuri}} [[Category:Japanese culture]] [[Category:Festivals in Japan]] [[Category:Festivals]] [[Category:Winter events]]

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] to [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2017年2月8日 (水) 11:32 (UTC)}} [[File:Ako Gishisai De09 13.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Ako Goshi Festival|ja|赤穂義士祭}}]] Winter festivals or '''Winter Matsuri''' (冬祭り) refer to [[festivals]] held during the [[winter]] season. These events typically occur over the three-month period from [[:en:Lidong|Rittō]] (the beginning of winter) to [[Risshun]] (the beginning of spring), corresponding to [[November]], [[December]], and [[January]].
== Overview == The '''Winter Festival''' incorporates both the '''harvest festival elements''' of the {{ill|Autumn festival|ja|秋祭り}} and the '''prayer for the year's blessings elements''' of the {{ill|Spring festival|ja|春祭り}}, making its exact positioning somewhat unclear. In ancient times, the '''10th month of the old calendar''' ({{ill|Kannazuki|ja|神無月}}) was considered an '''unlucky month''' ({{ill|Imizuki|ja|忌月}}).<ref>Although the unlucky months are commonly introduced as January, May, and September in the modern calendar, in the old calendar, they were roughly one month later, making the 10th month of the old calendar an unlucky month.</ref> Thus, the '''harvest festival''' was originally held in the '''11th month of the old calendar''', after the month changed and winter began. However, as the '''taboos''' surrounding the 10th month faded, the '''Autumn Festival, held immediately after the harvest''', became established.
{{ill|Orikuchi Shinobu|ja|折口信夫}} viewed this period, spanning from autumn to the following spring, as a time of '''spiritual renewal''' ({{ill|Chinkon|ja|鎮魂}}), during which all souls, including human ones, were refreshed. He theorized that the '''Autumn Festival, Winter Festival, and Spring Festival''' were originally '''one continuous ritual cycle''', dealing with '''harvest, soul renewal, and prayers for the coming year'''. Over time, he suggested, the '''Autumn and Spring Festivals split off''', leaving the remainder as the '''Winter Festival'''.
Similarly, {{ill|Yanagita Kunio|ja|柳田國男}} pointed out that the '''11th month of the old calendar''', which includes the '''Winter Solstice''', was a turning point toward spring. He argued that Japanese festivals traditionally placed great importance on this month.
In the {{ill|Jingikan|ja|神祇官}} (Department of Shinto Affairs) of the {{ill|Imperial Court of Japan|ja|朝廷 (日本)}}, the {{ill|Four Seasonal Festivals|ja|四時祭}} designated the '''Ainame Festival''' ({{ill|Ainame Festival|ja|相嘗祭|lt=Ainame-sai|Ainame Festival=Ainame Festival}}), '''Chinkon Festival''' ({{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭}}), and '''Niiname Festival''' ({{ill|Niiname-sai|ja|新嘗祭}}) in the '''11th month of the old calendar''' as '''Winter Festivals'''. Additionally, although the '''Tsukinami Festival''' ({{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭}}), '''Chinka (Fire-Calming Ritual)''' ({{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|ja|鎮火|Fire-Pacification Festival=Fire-Pacification Festival|lt=Chinka}}), and '''Dōkyō Festival''' ({{ill|Dōkyō|ja|道饗}}) were not inherently winter-specific, they were conducted as part of the Winter Festival period.
Today, many '''Winter Festivals''' retain elements of the '''Chinkon Festival (Ritual for Soul-Pacification)''', and practices such as '''Yutate (Hot Water Purification Rituals)''' ({{ill|Yutate|ja|湯立}}) and '''Kagura (Sacred Dance and Music)''' ({{ill|Kagura|ja|神楽}}), which originated from '''Shimotsuki Kagura''' ({{ill|Shimotsuki Kagura|ja|霜月神楽}}), are often performed.
== Notes == {{reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Spring Festivals|en}} * [[Summer festivals]] * {{ill|Autumn Festivals|en}} * [[:en:Winter_break|Winter break]] * {{DEFAULTSORT:Fuyumatsuri}} [[Category:Japanese culture]] [[Category:Festivals in Japan]] [[Category:Festivals]] [[Category:Winter events]]
* [[:en:Yule|Yule]] – Ancient European winter solstice celebrations

{{出典の明記|date=2017年2月8日 (水) 11:32 (UTC)}} [[File:Ako Gishisai De09 13.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Ako Goshi Festival|ja|赤穂義士祭}}]] Winter festivals or '''Winter Matsuri''' (冬祭り) refer to [[festivals]] held during the [[winter]] season. These events typically occur over the three-month period from [[:en:Lidong|Rittō]] (the beginning of winter) to [[Risshun]] (the beginning of spring), corresponding to [[November]], [[December]], and [[January]].
== Overview == The '''Winter Festival''' incorporates both the '''harvest festival elements''' of the {{ill|Autumn festival|ja|秋祭り}} and the '''prayer for the year's blessings elements''' of the {{ill|Spring festival|ja|春祭り}}, making its exact positioning somewhat unclear. In ancient times, the '''10th month of the old calendar''' ({{ill|Kannazuki|ja|神無月}}) was considered an '''unlucky month''' ({{ill|Imizuki|ja|忌月}}).<ref>Although the unlucky months are commonly introduced as January, May, and September in the modern calendar, in the old calendar, they were roughly one month later, making the 10th month of the old calendar an unlucky month.</ref> Thus, the '''harvest festival''' was originally held in the '''11th month of the old calendar''', after the month changed and winter began. However, as the '''taboos''' surrounding the 10th month faded, the '''Autumn Festival, held immediately after the harvest''', became established.
{{ill|Orikuchi Shinobu|ja|折口信夫}} viewed this period, spanning from autumn to the following spring, as a time of '''spiritual renewal''' ({{ill|Chinkon|ja|鎮魂}}), during which all souls, including human ones, were refreshed. He theorized that the '''Autumn Festival, Winter Festival, and Spring Festival''' were originally '''one continuous ritual cycle''', dealing with '''harvest, soul renewal, and prayers for the coming year'''. Over time, he suggested, the '''Autumn and Spring Festivals split off''', leaving the remainder as the '''Winter Festival'''.
Similarly, {{ill|Yanagita Kunio|ja|柳田國男}} pointed out that the '''11th month of the old calendar''', which includes the '''Winter Solstice''', was a turning point toward spring. He argued that Japanese festivals traditionally placed great importance on this month.
In the {{ill|Jingikan|ja|神祇官}} (Department of Shinto Affairs) of the {{ill|Imperial Court of Japan|ja|朝廷 (日本)}}, the {{ill|Four Seasonal Festivals|ja|四時祭}} designated the '''Ainame Festival''' ({{ill|Ainame Festival|ja|相嘗祭|lt=Ainame-sai|Ainame Festival=Ainame Festival}}), '''Chinkon Festival''' ({{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭}}), and '''Niiname Festival''' ({{ill|Niiname-sai|ja|新嘗祭}}) in the '''11th month of the old calendar''' as '''Winter Festivals'''. Additionally, although the '''Tsukinami Festival''' ({{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭}}), '''Chinka (Fire-Calming Ritual)''' ({{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|ja|鎮火|Fire-Pacification Festival=Fire-Pacification Festival|lt=Chinka}}), and '''Dōkyō Festival''' ({{ill|Dōkyō|ja|道饗}}) were not inherently winter-specific, they were conducted as part of the Winter Festival period.
Today, many '''Winter Festivals''' retain elements of the '''Chinkon Festival (Ritual for Soul-Pacification)''', and practices such as '''Yutate (Hot Water Purification Rituals)''' ({{ill|Yutate|ja|湯立}}) and '''Kagura (Sacred Dance and Music)''' ({{ill|Kagura|ja|神楽}}), which originated from '''Shimotsuki Kagura''' ({{ill|Shimotsuki Kagura|ja|霜月神楽}}), are often performed.
