{{出典の明記|date=2017年2月8日 (水) 11:32 (UTC)}} [[File:Ako Gishisai De09 13.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Ako Goshi Festival|ja|赤穂義士祭}}]] Winter festivals or '''Winter Matsuri''' (冬祭り) refer to [[festivals]] held during the [[winter]] season. These events typically occur over the three-month period from [[:en:Lidong|Rittō]] (the beginning of winter) to [[Risshun]] (the beginning of spring), corresponding to [[November]], [[December]], and [[January]].
== Overview == The '''Winter Festival''' incorporates both the '''harvest festival elements''' of the {{ill|Autumn festival|ja|秋祭り}} and the '''prayer for the year's blessings elements''' of the {{ill|Spring festival|ja|春祭り}}, making its exact positioning somewhat unclear. In ancient times, the '''10th month of the old calendar''' ({{ill|Kannazuki|ja|神無月}}) was considered an '''unlucky month''' ({{ill|Imizuki|ja|忌月}}).<ref>Although the unlucky months are commonly introduced as January, May, and September in the modern calendar, in the old calendar, they were roughly one month later, making the 10th month of the old calendar an unlucky month.</ref> Thus, the '''harvest festival''' was originally held in the '''11th month of the old calendar''', after the month changed and winter began. However, as the '''taboos''' surrounding the 10th month faded, the '''Autumn Festival, held immediately after the harvest''', became established.
{{ill|Orikuchi Shinobu|ja|折口信夫}} viewed this period, spanning from autumn to the following spring, as a time of '''spiritual renewal''' ({{ill|Chinkon|ja|鎮魂}}), during which all souls, including human ones, were refreshed. He theorized that the '''Autumn Festival, Winter Festival, and Spring Festival''' were originally '''one continuous ritual cycle''', dealing with '''harvest, soul renewal, and prayers for the coming year'''. Over time, he suggested, the '''Autumn and Spring Festivals split off''', leaving the remainder as the '''Winter Festival'''.
Similarly, {{ill|Yanagita Kunio|ja|柳田國男}} pointed out that the '''11th month of the old calendar''', which includes the '''Winter Solstice''', was a turning point toward spring. He argued that Japanese festivals traditionally placed great importance on this month.
In the {{ill|Jingikan|ja|神祇官}} (Department of Shinto Affairs) of the {{ill|Imperial Court of Japan|ja|朝廷 (日本)}}, the {{ill|Four Seasonal Festivals|ja|四時祭}} designated the '''Ainame Festival''' ({{ill|Ainame Festival|ja|相嘗祭|lt=Ainame-sai|Ainame Festival=Ainame Festival}}), '''Chinkon Festival''' ({{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭}}), and '''Niiname Festival''' ({{ill|Niiname-sai|ja|新嘗祭}}) in the '''11th month of the old calendar''' as '''Winter Festivals'''. Additionally, although the '''Tsukinami Festival''' ({{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭}}), '''Chinka (Fire-Calming Ritual)''' ({{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|ja|鎮火|Fire-Pacification Festival=Fire-Pacification Festival|lt=Chinka}}), and '''Dōkyō Festival''' ({{ill|Dōkyō|ja|道饗}}) were not inherently winter-specific, they were conducted as part of the Winter Festival period.
Today, many '''Winter Festivals''' retain elements of the '''Chinkon Festival (Ritual for Soul-Pacification)''', and practices such as '''Yutate (Hot Water Purification Rituals)''' ({{ill|Yutate|ja|湯立}}) and '''Kagura (Sacred Dance and Music)''' ({{ill|Kagura|ja|神楽}}), which originated from '''Shimotsuki Kagura''' ({{ill|Shimotsuki Kagura|ja|霜月神楽}}), are often performed.

{{出典の明記|date=2017年2月8日 (水) 11:32 (UTC)}} [[File:Ako Gishisai De09 13.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Ako Goshi Festival|ja|赤穂義士祭}}]] Winter festivals or '''Winter Matsuri''' (冬祭り) refer to [[festivals]] held during the [[winter]] season. These events typically occur over the three-month period from [[:en:Lidong|Rittō]] (the beginning of winter) to [[Risshun]] (the beginning of spring), corresponding to [[November]], [[December]], and [[January]].
== Overview == The '''Winter Festival''' incorporates both the '''harvest festival elements''' of the {{ill|Autumn festival|ja|秋祭り}} and the '''prayer for the year's blessings elements''' of the {{ill|Spring festival|ja|春祭り}}, making its exact positioning somewhat unclear. In ancient times, the '''10th month of the old calendar''' ({{ill|Kannazuki|ja|神無月}}) was considered an '''unlucky month''' ({{ill|Imizuki|ja|忌月}}).<ref>Although the unlucky months are commonly introduced as January, May, and September in the modern calendar, in the old calendar, they were roughly one month later, making the 10th month of the old calendar an unlucky month.</ref> Thus, the '''harvest festival''' was originally held in the '''11th month of the old calendar''', after the month changed and winter began. However, as the '''taboos''' surrounding the 10th month faded, the '''Autumn Festival, held immediately after the harvest''', became established.
{{ill|Orikuchi Shinobu|ja|折口信夫}} viewed this period, spanning from autumn to the following spring, as a time of '''spiritual renewal''' ({{ill|Chinkon|ja|鎮魂}}), during which all souls, including human ones, were refreshed. He theorized that the '''Autumn Festival, Winter Festival, and Spring Festival''' were originally '''one continuous ritual cycle''', dealing with '''harvest, soul renewal, and prayers for the coming year'''. Over time, he suggested, the '''Autumn and Spring Festivals split off''', leaving the remainder as the '''Winter Festival'''.
Similarly, {{ill|Yanagita Kunio|ja|柳田國男}} pointed out that the '''11th month of the old calendar''', which includes the '''Winter Solstice''', was a turning point toward spring. He argued that Japanese festivals traditionally placed great importance on this month.
In the {{ill|Jingikan|ja|神祇官}} (Department of Shinto Affairs) of the {{ill|Imperial Court of Japan|ja|朝廷 (日本)}}, the {{ill|Four Seasonal Festivals|ja|四時祭}} designated the '''Ainame Festival''' ({{ill|Ainame Festival|ja|相嘗祭|lt=Ainame-sai|Ainame Festival=Ainame Festival}}), '''Chinkon Festival''' ({{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭}}), and '''Niiname Festival''' ({{ill|Niiname-sai|ja|新嘗祭}}) in the '''11th month of the old calendar''' as '''Winter Festivals'''. Additionally, although the '''Tsukinami Festival''' ({{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭}}), '''Chinka (Fire-Calming Ritual)''' ({{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|ja|鎮火|Fire-Pacification Festival=Fire-Pacification Festival|lt=Chinka}}), and '''Dōkyō Festival''' ({{ill|Dōkyō|ja|道饗}}) were not inherently winter-specific, they were conducted as part of the Winter Festival period.
Today, many '''Winter Festivals''' retain elements of the '''Chinkon Festival (Ritual for Soul-Pacification)''', and practices such as '''Yutate (Hot Water Purification Rituals)''' ({{ill|Yutate|ja|湯立}}) and '''Kagura (Sacred Dance and Music)''' ({{ill|Kagura|ja|神楽}}), which originated from '''Shimotsuki Kagura''' ({{ill|Shimotsuki Kagura|ja|霜月神楽}}), are often performed.

{{出典の明記|date=2017年2月8日 (水) 11:32 (UTC)}} [[File:Ako Gishisai De09 13.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Ako Goshi Festival|ja|赤穂義士祭}}]] Winter festivals or '''Winter Matsuri''' (冬祭り) refer to [[festivals]] held during the [[winter]] season. These events typically occur over the three-month period from [[:en:Lidong|Rittō]] (the beginning of winter) to [[Risshun]] (the beginning of spring), corresponding to [[November]], [[December]], and [[January]].
== Overview == The '''Winter Festival''' incorporates both the '''harvest festival elements''' of the {{ill|Autumn festival|ja|秋祭り}} and the '''prayer for the year's blessings elements''' of the {{ill|Spring festival|ja|春祭り}}, making its exact positioning somewhat unclear. In ancient times, the '''10th month of the old calendar''' ({{ill|Kannazuki|ja|神無月}}) was considered an '''unlucky month''' ({{ill|Imizuki|ja|忌月}}).<ref>Although the unlucky months are commonly introduced as January, May, and September in the modern calendar, in the old calendar, they were roughly one month later, making the 10th month of the old calendar an unlucky month.</ref> Thus, the '''harvest festival''' was originally held in the '''11th month of the old calendar''', after the month changed and winter began. However, as the '''taboos''' surrounding the 10th month faded, the '''Autumn Festival, held immediately after the harvest''', became established.
{{ill|Orikuchi Shinobu|ja|折口信夫}} viewed this period, spanning from autumn to the following spring, as a time of '''spiritual renewal''' ({{ill|Chinkon|ja|鎮魂}}), during which all souls, including human ones, were refreshed. He theorized that the '''Autumn Festival, Winter Festival, and Spring Festival''' were originally '''one continuous ritual cycle''', dealing with '''harvest, soul renewal, and prayers for the coming year'''. Over time, he suggested, the '''Autumn and Spring Festivals split off''', leaving the remainder as the '''Winter Festival'''.
Similarly, {{ill|Yanagita Kunio|ja|柳田國男}} pointed out that the '''11th month of the old calendar''', which includes the '''Winter Solstice''', was a turning point toward spring. He argued that Japanese festivals traditionally placed great importance on this month.
In the {{ill|Jingikan|ja|神祇官}} (Department of Shinto Affairs) of the {{ill|Imperial Court of Japan|ja|朝廷 (日本)}}, the {{ill|Four Seasonal Festivals|ja|四時祭}} designated the '''Ainame Festival''' ({{ill|Ainame Festival|ja|相嘗祭|lt=Ainame-sai|Ainame Festival=Ainame Festival}}), '''Chinkon Festival''' ({{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭}}), and '''Niiname Festival''' ({{ill|Niiname-sai|ja|新嘗祭}}) in the '''11th month of the old calendar''' as '''Winter Festivals'''. Additionally, although the '''Tsukinami Festival''' ({{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭}}), '''Chinka (Fire-Calming Ritual)''' ({{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|ja|鎮火|Fire-Pacification Festival=Fire-Pacification Festival|lt=Chinka}}), and '''Dōkyō Festival''' ({{ill|Dōkyō|ja|道饗}}) were not inherently winter-specific, they were conducted as part of the Winter Festival period.
Today, many '''Winter Festivals''' retain elements of the '''Chinkon Festival (Ritual for Soul-Pacification)''', and practices such as '''Yutate (Hot Water Purification Rituals)''' ({{ill|Yutate|ja|湯立}}) and '''Kagura (Sacred Dance and Music)''' ({{ill|Kagura|ja|神楽}}), which originated from '''Shimotsuki Kagura''' ({{ill|Shimotsuki Kagura|ja|霜月神楽}}), are often performed.

{{出典の明記|date=2017年2月8日 (水) 11:32 (UTC)}} [[File:Ako Gishisai De09 13.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Ako Goshi Festival|ja|赤穂義士祭}}]] Winter festivals or '''Winter Matsuri''' (冬祭り) refer to [[festivals]] held during the [[winter]] season. These events typically occur over the three-month period from [[:en:Lidong|Rittō]] (the beginning of winter) to [[Risshun]] (the beginning of spring), corresponding to [[November]], [[December]], and [[January]].
== Overview == The '''Winter Festival''' incorporates both the '''harvest festival elements''' of the {{ill|Autumn festival|ja|秋祭り}} and the '''prayer for the year's blessings elements''' of the {{ill|Spring festival|ja|春祭り}}, making its exact positioning somewhat unclear. In ancient times, the '''10th month of the old calendar''' ({{ill|Kannazuki|ja|神無月}}) was considered an '''unlucky month''' ({{ill|Imizuki|ja|忌月}}).<ref>Although the unlucky months are commonly introduced as January, May, and September in the modern calendar, in the old calendar, they were roughly one month later, making the 10th month of the old calendar an unlucky month.</ref> Thus, the '''harvest festival''' was originally held in the '''11th month of the old calendar''', after the month changed and winter began. However, as the '''taboos''' surrounding the 10th month faded, the '''Autumn Festival, held immediately after the harvest''', became established.
{{ill|Orikuchi Shinobu|ja|折口信夫}} viewed this period, spanning from autumn to the following spring, as a time of '''spiritual renewal''' ({{ill|Chinkon|ja|鎮魂}}), during which all souls, including human ones, were refreshed. He theorized that the '''Autumn Festival, Winter Festival, and Spring Festival''' were originally '''one continuous ritual cycle''', dealing with '''harvest, soul renewal, and prayers for the coming year'''. Over time, he suggested, the '''Autumn and Spring Festivals split off''', leaving the remainder as the '''Winter Festival'''.
Similarly, {{ill|Yanagita Kunio|ja|柳田國男}} pointed out that the '''11th month of the old calendar''', which includes the '''Winter Solstice''', was a turning point toward spring. He argued that Japanese festivals traditionally placed great importance on this month.
In the {{ill|Jingikan|ja|神祇官}} (Department of Shinto Affairs) of the {{ill|Imperial Court of Japan|ja|朝廷 (日本)}}, the {{ill|Four Seasonal Festivals|ja|四時祭}} designated the '''Ainame Festival''' ({{ill|Ainame Festival|ja|相嘗祭|lt=Ainame-sai|Ainame Festival=Ainame Festival}}), '''Chinkon Festival''' ({{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭}}), and '''Niiname Festival''' ({{ill|Niiname-sai|ja|新嘗祭}}) in the '''11th month of the old calendar''' as '''Winter Festivals'''. Additionally, although the '''Tsukinami Festival''' ({{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭}}), '''Chinka (Fire-Calming Ritual)''' ({{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|ja|鎮火|Fire-Pacification Festival=Fire-Pacification Festival|lt=Chinka}}), and '''Dōkyō Festival''' ({{ill|Dōkyō|ja|道饗}}) were not inherently winter-specific, they were conducted as part of the Winter Festival period.
Today, many '''Winter Festivals''' retain elements of the '''Chinkon Festival (Ritual for Soul-Pacification)''', and practices such as '''Yutate (Hot Water Purification Rituals)''' ({{ill|Yutate|ja|湯立}}) and '''Kagura (Sacred Dance and Music)''' ({{ill|Kagura|ja|神楽}}), which originated from '''Shimotsuki Kagura''' ({{ill|Shimotsuki Kagura|ja|霜月神楽}}), are often performed.

Similarly, {{ill|Yanagita Kunio|ja|柳田國男}} pointed out that the '''11th month of the old calendar''', which includes the '''Winter Solstice''', was a turning point toward spring. He argued that Japanese festivals traditionally placed great importance on this month.
In the {{ill|Jingikan|ja|神祇官}} (Department of Shinto Affairs) of the {{ill|Imperial Court of Japan|ja|朝廷 (日本)}}, the {{ill|Four Seasonal Festivals|ja|四時祭}} designated the '''Ainame Festival''' ({{ill|Ainame Festival|ja|相嘗祭|lt=Ainame-sai|Ainame Festival=Ainame Festival}}), '''Chinkon Festival''' ({{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭}}), and '''Niiname Festival''' ({{ill|Niiname-sai|ja|新嘗祭}}) in the '''11th month of the old calendar''' as '''Winter Festivals'''. Additionally, although the '''Tsukinami Festival''' ({{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭}}), '''Chinka (Fire-Calming Ritual)''' ({{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|ja|鎮火|Fire-Pacification Festival=Fire-Pacification Festival|lt=Chinka}}), and '''Dōkyō Festival''' ({{ill|Dōkyō|ja|道饗}}) were not inherently winter-specific, they were conducted as part of the Winter Festival period.
Today, many '''Winter Festivals''' retain elements of the '''Chinkon Festival (Ritual for Soul-Pacification)''', and practices such as '''Yutate (Hot Water Purification Rituals)''' ({{ill|Yutate|ja|湯立}}) and '''Kagura (Sacred Dance and Music)''' ({{ill|Kagura|ja|神楽}}), which originated from '''Shimotsuki Kagura''' ({{ill|Shimotsuki Kagura|ja|霜月神楽}}), are often performed.

{{出典の明記|date=2017年2月8日 (水) 11:32 (UTC)}} [[File:Ako Gishisai De09 13.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Ako Goshi Festival|ja|赤穂義士祭}}]] Winter festivals or '''Winter Matsuri''' (冬祭り) refer to [[festivals]] held during the [[winter]] season. These events typically occur over the three-month period from [[:en:Lidong|Rittō]] (the beginning of winter) to [[Risshun]] (the beginning of spring), corresponding to [[November]], [[December]], and [[January]].
== Overview == The '''Winter Festival''' incorporates both the '''harvest festival elements''' of the {{ill|Autumn festival|ja|秋祭り}} and the '''prayer for the year's blessings elements''' of the {{ill|Spring festival|ja|春祭り}}, making its exact positioning somewhat unclear. In ancient times, the '''10th month of the old calendar''' ({{ill|Kannazuki|ja|神無月}}) was considered an '''unlucky month''' ({{ill|Imizuki|ja|忌月}}).<ref>Although the unlucky months are commonly introduced as January, May, and September in the modern calendar, in the old calendar, they were roughly one month later, making the 10th month of the old calendar an unlucky month.</ref> Thus, the '''harvest festival''' was originally held in the '''11th month of the old calendar''', after the month changed and winter began. However, as the '''taboos''' surrounding the 10th month faded, the '''Autumn Festival, held immediately after the harvest''', became established.
{{ill|Orikuchi Shinobu|ja|折口信夫}} viewed this period, spanning from autumn to the following spring, as a time of '''spiritual renewal''' ({{ill|Chinkon|ja|鎮魂}}), during which all souls, including human ones, were refreshed. He theorized that the '''Autumn Festival, Winter Festival, and Spring Festival''' were originally '''one continuous ritual cycle''', dealing with '''harvest, soul renewal, and prayers for the coming year'''. Over time, he suggested, the '''Autumn and Spring Festivals split off''', leaving the remainder as the '''Winter Festival'''.
Similarly, {{ill|Yanagita Kunio|ja|柳田國男}} pointed out that the '''11th month of the old calendar''', which includes the '''Winter Solstice''', was a turning point toward spring. He argued that Japanese festivals traditionally placed great importance on this month.
In the {{ill|Jingikan|ja|神祇官}} (Department of Shinto Affairs) of the {{ill|Imperial Court of Japan|ja|朝廷 (日本)}}, the {{ill|Four Seasonal Festivals|ja|四時祭}} designated the '''Ainame Festival''' ({{ill|Ainame Festival|ja|相嘗祭|lt=Ainame-sai|Ainame Festival=Ainame Festival}}), '''Chinkon Festival''' ({{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭}}), and '''Niiname Festival''' ({{ill|Niiname-sai|ja|新嘗祭}}) in the '''11th month of the old calendar''' as '''Winter Festivals'''. Additionally, although the '''Tsukinami Festival''' ({{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭}}), '''Chinka (Fire-Calming Ritual)''' ({{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|ja|鎮火|Fire-Pacification Festival=Fire-Pacification Festival|lt=Chinka}}), and '''Dōkyō Festival''' ({{ill|Dōkyō|ja|道饗}}) were not inherently winter-specific, they were conducted as part of the Winter Festival period.
Today, many '''Winter Festivals''' retain elements of the '''Chinkon Festival (Ritual for Soul-Pacification)''', and practices such as '''Yutate (Hot Water Purification Rituals)''' ({{ill|Yutate|ja|湯立}}) and '''Kagura (Sacred Dance and Music)''' ({{ill|Kagura|ja|神楽}}), which originated from '''Shimotsuki Kagura''' ({{ill|Shimotsuki Kagura|ja|霜月神楽}}), are often performed.

In the {{ill|Jingikan|ja|神祇官}} (Department of Shinto Affairs) of the {{ill|Imperial Court of Japan|ja|朝廷 (日本)}}, the {{ill|Four Seasonal Festivals|ja|四時祭}} designated the '''Ainame Festival''' ({{ill|Ainame Festival|ja|相嘗祭|lt=Ainame-sai|Ainame Festival=Ainame Festival}}), '''Chinkon Festival''' ({{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭}}), and '''Niiname Festival''' ({{ill|Niiname-sai|ja|新嘗祭}}) in the '''11th month of the old calendar''' as '''Winter Festivals'''. Additionally, although the '''Tsukinami Festival''' ({{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭}}), '''Chinka (Fire-Calming Ritual)''' ({{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|ja|鎮火|Fire-Pacification Festival=Fire-Pacification Festival|lt=Chinka}}), and '''Dōkyō Festival''' ({{ill|Dōkyō|ja|道饗}}) were not inherently winter-specific, they were conducted as part of the Winter Festival period.
Today, many '''Winter Festivals''' retain elements of the '''Chinkon Festival (Ritual for Soul-Pacification)''', and practices such as '''Yutate (Hot Water Purification Rituals)''' ({{ill|Yutate|ja|湯立}}) and '''Kagura (Sacred Dance and Music)''' ({{ill|Kagura|ja|神楽}}), which originated from '''Shimotsuki Kagura''' ({{ill|Shimotsuki Kagura|ja|霜月神楽}}), are often performed.

{{出典の明記|date=2017年2月8日 (水) 11:32 (UTC)}} [[File:Ako Gishisai De09 13.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Ako Goshi Festival|ja|赤穂義士祭}}]] Winter festivals or '''Winter Matsuri''' (冬祭り) refer to [[festivals]] held during the [[winter]] season. These events typically occur over the three-month period from [[:en:Lidong|Rittō]] (the beginning of winter) to [[Risshun]] (the beginning of spring), corresponding to [[November]], [[December]], and [[January]].
== Overview == The '''Winter Festival''' incorporates both the '''harvest festival elements''' of the {{ill|Autumn festival|ja|秋祭り}} and the '''prayer for the year's blessings elements''' of the {{ill|Spring festival|ja|春祭り}}, making its exact positioning somewhat unclear. In ancient times, the '''10th month of the old calendar''' ({{ill|Kannazuki|ja|神無月}}) was considered an '''unlucky month''' ({{ill|Imizuki|ja|忌月}}).<ref>Although the unlucky months are commonly introduced as January, May, and September in the modern calendar, in the old calendar, they were roughly one month later, making the 10th month of the old calendar an unlucky month.</ref> Thus, the '''harvest festival''' was originally held in the '''11th month of the old calendar''', after the month changed and winter began. However, as the '''taboos''' surrounding the 10th month faded, the '''Autumn Festival, held immediately after the harvest''', became established.
{{ill|Orikuchi Shinobu|ja|折口信夫}} viewed this period, spanning from autumn to the following spring, as a time of '''spiritual renewal''' ({{ill|Chinkon|ja|鎮魂}}), during which all souls, including human ones, were refreshed. He theorized that the '''Autumn Festival, Winter Festival, and Spring Festival''' were originally '''one continuous ritual cycle''', dealing with '''harvest, soul renewal, and prayers for the coming year'''. Over time, he suggested, the '''Autumn and Spring Festivals split off''', leaving the remainder as the '''Winter Festival'''.
Similarly, {{ill|Yanagita Kunio|ja|柳田國男}} pointed out that the '''11th month of the old calendar''', which includes the '''Winter Solstice''', was a turning point toward spring. He argued that Japanese festivals traditionally placed great importance on this month.
In the {{ill|Jingikan|ja|神祇官}} (Department of Shinto Affairs) of the {{ill|Imperial Court of Japan|ja|朝廷 (日本)}}, the {{ill|Four Seasonal Festivals|ja|四時祭}} designated the '''Ainame Festival''' ({{ill|Ainame Festival|ja|相嘗祭|lt=Ainame-sai|Ainame Festival=Ainame Festival}}), '''Chinkon Festival''' ({{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭}}), and '''Niiname Festival''' ({{ill|Niiname-sai|ja|新嘗祭}}) in the '''11th month of the old calendar''' as '''Winter Festivals'''. Additionally, although the '''Tsukinami Festival''' ({{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭}}), '''Chinka (Fire-Calming Ritual)''' ({{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|ja|鎮火|Fire-Pacification Festival=Fire-Pacification Festival|lt=Chinka}}), and '''Dōkyō Festival''' ({{ill|Dōkyō|ja|道饗}}) were not inherently winter-specific, they were conducted as part of the Winter Festival period.
Today, many '''Winter Festivals''' retain elements of the '''Chinkon Festival (Ritual for Soul-Pacification)''', and practices such as '''Yutate (Hot Water Purification Rituals)''' ({{ill|Yutate|ja|湯立}}) and '''Kagura (Sacred Dance and Music)''' ({{ill|Kagura|ja|神楽}}), which originated from '''Shimotsuki Kagura''' ({{ill|Shimotsuki Kagura|ja|霜月神楽}}), are often performed.

In the {{ill|Jingikan|ja|神祇官}} (Department of Shinto Affairs) of the {{ill|Imperial Court of Japan|ja|朝廷 (日本)}}, the {{ill|Four Seasonal Festivals|ja|四時祭}} designated the '''Ainame Festival''' ({{ill|Ainame Festival|ja|相嘗祭|lt=Ainame-sai|Ainame Festival=Ainame Festival}}), '''Chinkon Festival''' ({{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭}}), and '''Niiname Festival''' ({{ill|Niiname-sai|ja|新嘗祭}}) in the '''11th month of the old calendar''' as '''Winter Festivals'''. Additionally, although the '''Tsukinami Festival''' ({{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭}}), '''Chinka (Fire-Calming Ritual)''' ({{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|ja|鎮火|Fire-Pacification Festival=Fire-Pacification Festival|lt=Chinka}}), and '''Dōkyō Festival''' ({{ill|Dōkyō|ja|道饗}}) were not inherently winter-specific, they were conducted as part of the Winter Festival period.
Today, many '''Winter Festivals''' retain elements of the '''Chinkon Festival (Ritual for Soul-Pacification)''', and practices such as '''Yutate (Hot Water Purification Rituals)''' ({{ill|Yutate|ja|湯立}}) and '''Kagura (Sacred Dance and Music)''' ({{ill|Kagura|ja|神楽}}), which originated from '''Shimotsuki Kagura''' ({{ill|Shimotsuki Kagura|ja|霜月神楽}}), are often performed.

{{出典の明記|date=2017年2月8日 (水) 11:32 (UTC)}} [[File:Ako Gishisai De09 13.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Ako Goshi Festival|ja|赤穂義士祭}}]] Winter festivals or '''Winter Matsuri''' (冬祭り) refer to [[festivals]] held during the [[winter]] season. These events typically occur over the three-month period from [[:en:Lidong|Rittō]] (the beginning of winter) to [[Risshun]] (the beginning of spring), corresponding to [[November]], [[December]], and [[January]].
== Overview == The '''Winter Festival''' incorporates both the '''harvest festival elements''' of the {{ill|Autumn festival|ja|秋祭り}} and the '''prayer for the year's blessings elements''' of the {{ill|Spring festival|ja|春祭り}}, making its exact positioning somewhat unclear. In ancient times, the '''10th month of the old calendar''' ({{ill|Kannazuki|ja|神無月}}) was considered an '''unlucky month''' ({{ill|Imizuki|ja|忌月}}).<ref>Although the unlucky months are commonly introduced as January, May, and September in the modern calendar, in the old calendar, they were roughly one month later, making the 10th month of the old calendar an unlucky month.</ref> Thus, the '''harvest festival''' was originally held in the '''11th month of the old calendar''', after the month changed and winter began. However, as the '''taboos''' surrounding the 10th month faded, the '''Autumn Festival, held immediately after the harvest''', became established.
{{ill|Orikuchi Shinobu|ja|折口信夫}} viewed this period, spanning from autumn to the following spring, as a time of '''spiritual renewal''' ({{ill|Chinkon|ja|鎮魂}}), during which all souls, including human ones, were refreshed. He theorized that the '''Autumn Festival, Winter Festival, and Spring Festival''' were originally '''one continuous ritual cycle''', dealing with '''harvest, soul renewal, and prayers for the coming year'''. Over time, he suggested, the '''Autumn and Spring Festivals split off''', leaving the remainder as the '''Winter Festival'''.
Similarly, {{ill|Yanagita Kunio|ja|柳田國男}} pointed out that the '''11th month of the old calendar''', which includes the '''Winter Solstice''', was a turning point toward spring. He argued that Japanese festivals traditionally placed great importance on this month.
In the {{ill|Jingikan|ja|神祇官}} (Department of Shinto Affairs) of the {{ill|Imperial Court of Japan|ja|朝廷 (日本)}}, the {{ill|Four Seasonal Festivals|ja|四時祭}} designated the '''Ainame Festival''' ({{ill|Ainame Festival|ja|相嘗祭|lt=Ainame-sai|Ainame Festival=Ainame Festival}}), '''Chinkon Festival''' ({{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭}}), and '''Niiname Festival''' ({{ill|Niiname-sai|ja|新嘗祭}}) in the '''11th month of the old calendar''' as '''Winter Festivals'''. Additionally, although the '''Tsukinami Festival''' ({{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭}}), '''Chinka (Fire-Calming Ritual)''' ({{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|ja|鎮火|Fire-Pacification Festival=Fire-Pacification Festival|lt=Chinka}}), and '''Dōkyō Festival''' ({{ill|Dōkyō|ja|道饗}}) were not inherently winter-specific, they were conducted as part of the Winter Festival period.
Today, many '''Winter Festivals''' retain elements of the '''Chinkon Festival (Ritual for Soul-Pacification)''', and practices such as '''Yutate (Hot Water Purification Rituals)''' ({{ill|Yutate|ja|湯立}}) and '''Kagura (Sacred Dance and Music)''' ({{ill|Kagura|ja|神楽}}), which originated from '''Shimotsuki Kagura''' ({{ill|Shimotsuki Kagura|ja|霜月神楽}}), are often performed.

Similarly, {{ill|Yanagita Kunio|ja|柳田國男}} pointed out that the '''11th month of the old calendar''', which includes the '''Winter Solstice''', was a turning point toward spring. He argued that Japanese festivals traditionally placed great importance on this month.
In the {{ill|Jingikan|ja|神祇官}} (Department of Shinto Affairs) of the {{ill|Imperial Court of Japan|ja|朝廷 (日本)}}, the {{ill|Four Seasonal Festivals|ja|四時祭}} designated the '''Ainame Festival''' ({{ill|Ainame Festival|ja|相嘗祭|lt=Ainame-sai|Ainame Festival=Ainame Festival}}), '''Chinkon Festival''' ({{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭}}), and '''Niiname Festival''' ({{ill|Niiname-sai|ja|新嘗祭}}) in the '''11th month of the old calendar''' as '''Winter Festivals'''. Additionally, although the '''Tsukinami Festival''' ({{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭}}), '''Chinka (Fire-Calming Ritual)''' ({{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|ja|鎮火|Fire-Pacification Festival=Fire-Pacification Festival|lt=Chinka}}), and '''Dōkyō Festival''' ({{ill|Dōkyō|ja|道饗}}) were not inherently winter-specific, they were conducted as part of the Winter Festival period.
Today, many '''Winter Festivals''' retain elements of the '''Chinkon Festival (Ritual for Soul-Pacification)''', and practices such as '''Yutate (Hot Water Purification Rituals)''' ({{ill|Yutate|ja|湯立}}) and '''Kagura (Sacred Dance and Music)''' ({{ill|Kagura|ja|神楽}}), which originated from '''Shimotsuki Kagura''' ({{ill|Shimotsuki Kagura|ja|霜月神楽}}), are often performed.

{{出典の明記|date=2017年2月8日 (水) 11:32 (UTC)}} [[File:Ako Gishisai De09 13.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Ako Goshi Festival|ja|赤穂義士祭}}]] Winter festivals or '''Winter Matsuri''' (冬祭り) refer to [[festivals]] held during the [[winter]] season. These events typically occur over the three-month period from [[:en:Lidong|Rittō]] (the beginning of winter) to [[Risshun]] (the beginning of spring), corresponding to [[November]], [[December]], and [[January]].
== Overview == The '''Winter Festival''' incorporates both the '''harvest festival elements''' of the {{ill|Autumn festival|ja|秋祭り}} and the '''prayer for the year's blessings elements''' of the {{ill|Spring festival|ja|春祭り}}, making its exact positioning somewhat unclear. In ancient times, the '''10th month of the old calendar''' ({{ill|Kannazuki|ja|神無月}}) was considered an '''unlucky month''' ({{ill|Imizuki|ja|忌月}}).<ref>Although the unlucky months are commonly introduced as January, May, and September in the modern calendar, in the old calendar, they were roughly one month later, making the 10th month of the old calendar an unlucky month.</ref> Thus, the '''harvest festival''' was originally held in the '''11th month of the old calendar''', after the month changed and winter began. However, as the '''taboos''' surrounding the 10th month faded, the '''Autumn Festival, held immediately after the harvest''', became established.
{{ill|Orikuchi Shinobu|ja|折口信夫}} viewed this period, spanning from autumn to the following spring, as a time of '''spiritual renewal''' ({{ill|Chinkon|ja|鎮魂}}), during which all souls, including human ones, were refreshed. He theorized that the '''Autumn Festival, Winter Festival, and Spring Festival''' were originally '''one continuous ritual cycle''', dealing with '''harvest, soul renewal, and prayers for the coming year'''. Over time, he suggested, the '''Autumn and Spring Festivals split off''', leaving the remainder as the '''Winter Festival'''.
Similarly, {{ill|Yanagita Kunio|ja|柳田國男}} pointed out that the '''11th month of the old calendar''', which includes the '''Winter Solstice''', was a turning point toward spring. He argued that Japanese festivals traditionally placed great importance on this month.
In the {{ill|Jingikan|ja|神祇官}} (Department of Shinto Affairs) of the {{ill|Imperial Court of Japan|ja|朝廷 (日本)}}, the {{ill|Four Seasonal Festivals|ja|四時祭}} designated the '''Ainame Festival''' ({{ill|Ainame Festival|ja|相嘗祭|lt=Ainame-sai|Ainame Festival=Ainame Festival}}), '''Chinkon Festival''' ({{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭}}), and '''Niiname Festival''' ({{ill|Niiname-sai|ja|新嘗祭}}) in the '''11th month of the old calendar''' as '''Winter Festivals'''. Additionally, although the '''Tsukinami Festival''' ({{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭}}), '''Chinka (Fire-Calming Ritual)''' ({{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|ja|鎮火|Fire-Pacification Festival=Fire-Pacification Festival|lt=Chinka}}), and '''Dōkyō Festival''' ({{ill|Dōkyō|ja|道饗}}) were not inherently winter-specific, they were conducted as part of the Winter Festival period.
Today, many '''Winter Festivals''' retain elements of the '''Chinkon Festival (Ritual for Soul-Pacification)''', and practices such as '''Yutate (Hot Water Purification Rituals)''' ({{ill|Yutate|ja|湯立}}) and '''Kagura (Sacred Dance and Music)''' ({{ill|Kagura|ja|神楽}}), which originated from '''Shimotsuki Kagura''' ({{ill|Shimotsuki Kagura|ja|霜月神楽}}), are often performed.

Over time, he suggested, the '''Autumn and Spring Festivals split off''', leaving the remainder as the '''Winter Festival'''.
Similarly, {{ill|Yanagita Kunio|ja|柳田國男}} pointed out that the '''11th month of the old calendar''', which includes the '''Winter Solstice''', was a turning point toward spring. He argued that Japanese festivals traditionally placed great importance on this month.
In the {{ill|Jingikan|ja|神祇官}} (Department of Shinto Affairs) of the {{ill|Imperial Court of Japan|ja|朝廷 (日本)}}, the {{ill|Four Seasonal Festivals|ja|四時祭}} designated the '''Ainame Festival''' ({{ill|Ainame Festival|ja|相嘗祭|lt=Ainame-sai|Ainame Festival=Ainame Festival}}), '''Chinkon Festival''' ({{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭}}), and '''Niiname Festival''' ({{ill|Niiname-sai|ja|新嘗祭}}) in the '''11th month of the old calendar''' as '''Winter Festivals'''. Additionally, although the '''Tsukinami Festival''' ({{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭}}), '''Chinka (Fire-Calming Ritual)''' ({{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|ja|鎮火|Fire-Pacification Festival=Fire-Pacification Festival|lt=Chinka}}), and '''Dōkyō Festival''' ({{ill|Dōkyō|ja|道饗}}) were not inherently winter-specific, they were conducted as part of the Winter Festival period.
Today, many '''Winter Festivals''' retain elements of the '''Chinkon Festival (Ritual for Soul-Pacification)''', and practices such as '''Yutate (Hot Water Purification Rituals)''' ({{ill|Yutate|ja|湯立}}) and '''Kagura (Sacred Dance and Music)''' ({{ill|Kagura|ja|神楽}}), which originated from '''Shimotsuki Kagura''' ({{ill|Shimotsuki Kagura|ja|霜月神楽}}), are often performed.

{{出典の明記|date=2017年2月8日 (水) 11:32 (UTC)}} [[File:Ako Gishisai De09 13.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Ako Goshi Festival|ja|赤穂義士祭}}]] Winter festivals or '''Winter Matsuri''' (冬祭り) refer to [[festivals]] held during the [[winter]] season. These events typically occur over the three-month period from [[:en:Lidong|Rittō]] (the beginning of winter) to [[Risshun]] (the beginning of spring), corresponding to [[November]], [[December]], and [[January]].
== Overview == The '''Winter Festival''' incorporates both the '''harvest festival elements''' of the {{ill|Autumn festival|ja|秋祭り}} and the '''prayer for the year's blessings elements''' of the {{ill|Spring festival|ja|春祭り}}, making its exact positioning somewhat unclear. In ancient times, the '''10th month of the old calendar''' ({{ill|Kannazuki|ja|神無月}}) was considered an '''unlucky month''' ({{ill|Imizuki|ja|忌月}}).<ref>Although the unlucky months are commonly introduced as January, May, and September in the modern calendar, in the old calendar, they were roughly one month later, making the 10th month of the old calendar an unlucky month.</ref> Thus, the '''harvest festival''' was originally held in the '''11th month of the old calendar''', after the month changed and winter began. However, as the '''taboos''' surrounding the 10th month faded, the '''Autumn Festival, held immediately after the harvest''', became established.
{{ill|Orikuchi Shinobu|ja|折口信夫}} viewed this period, spanning from autumn to the following spring, as a time of '''spiritual renewal''' ({{ill|Chinkon|ja|鎮魂}}), during which all souls, including human ones, were refreshed. He theorized that the '''Autumn Festival, Winter Festival, and Spring Festival''' were originally '''one continuous ritual cycle''', dealing with '''harvest, soul renewal, and prayers for the coming year'''. Over time, he suggested, the '''Autumn and Spring Festivals split off''', leaving the remainder as the '''Winter Festival'''.
Similarly, {{ill|Yanagita Kunio|ja|柳田國男}} pointed out that the '''11th month of the old calendar''', which includes the '''Winter Solstice''', was a turning point toward spring. He argued that Japanese festivals traditionally placed great importance on this month.
In the {{ill|Jingikan|ja|神祇官}} (Department of Shinto Affairs) of the {{ill|Imperial Court of Japan|ja|朝廷 (日本)}}, the {{ill|Four Seasonal Festivals|ja|四時祭}} designated the '''Ainame Festival''' ({{ill|Ainame Festival|ja|相嘗祭|lt=Ainame-sai|Ainame Festival=Ainame Festival}}), '''Chinkon Festival''' ({{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭}}), and '''Niiname Festival''' ({{ill|Niiname-sai|ja|新嘗祭}}) in the '''11th month of the old calendar''' as '''Winter Festivals'''. Additionally, although the '''Tsukinami Festival''' ({{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭}}), '''Chinka (Fire-Calming Ritual)''' ({{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|ja|鎮火|Fire-Pacification Festival=Fire-Pacification Festival|lt=Chinka}}), and '''Dōkyō Festival''' ({{ill|Dōkyō|ja|道饗}}) were not inherently winter-specific, they were conducted as part of the Winter Festival period.
Today, many '''Winter Festivals''' retain elements of the '''Chinkon Festival (Ritual for Soul-Pacification)''', and practices such as '''Yutate (Hot Water Purification Rituals)''' ({{ill|Yutate|ja|湯立}}) and '''Kagura (Sacred Dance and Music)''' ({{ill|Kagura|ja|神楽}}), which originated from '''Shimotsuki Kagura''' ({{ill|Shimotsuki Kagura|ja|霜月神楽}}), are often performed.

{{出典の明記|date=2017年2月8日 (水) 11:32 (UTC)}} [[File:Ako Gishisai De09 13.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Ako Goshi Festival|ja|赤穂義士祭}}]] Winter festivals or '''Winter Matsuri''' (冬祭り) refer to [[festivals]] held during the [[winter]] season. These events typically occur over the three-month period from [[:en:Lidong|Rittō]] (the beginning of winter) to [[Risshun]] (the beginning of spring), corresponding to [[November]], [[December]], and [[January]].
== Overview == The '''Winter Festival''' incorporates both the '''harvest festival elements''' of the {{ill|Autumn festival|ja|秋祭り}} and the '''prayer for the year's blessings elements''' of the {{ill|Spring festival|ja|春祭り}}, making its exact positioning somewhat unclear. In ancient times, the '''10th month of the old calendar''' ({{ill|Kannazuki|ja|神無月}}) was considered an '''unlucky month''' ({{ill|Imizuki|ja|忌月}}).<ref>Although the unlucky months are commonly introduced as January, May, and September in the modern calendar, in the old calendar, they were roughly one month later, making the 10th month of the old calendar an unlucky month.</ref> Thus, the '''harvest festival''' was originally held in the '''11th month of the old calendar''', after the month changed and winter began. However, as the '''taboos''' surrounding the 10th month faded, the '''Autumn Festival, held immediately after the harvest''', became established.
{{ill|Orikuchi Shinobu|ja|折口信夫}} viewed this period, spanning from autumn to the following spring, as a time of '''spiritual renewal''' ({{ill|Chinkon|ja|鎮魂}}), during which all souls, including human ones, were refreshed. He theorized that the '''Autumn Festival, Winter Festival, and Spring Festival''' were originally '''one continuous ritual cycle''', dealing with '''harvest, soul renewal, and prayers for the coming year'''. Over time, he suggested, the '''Autumn and Spring Festivals split off''', leaving the remainder as the '''Winter Festival'''.
Similarly, {{ill|Yanagita Kunio|ja|柳田國男}} pointed out that the '''11th month of the old calendar''', which includes the '''Winter Solstice''', was a turning point toward spring. He argued that Japanese festivals traditionally placed great importance on this month.
In the {{ill|Jingikan|ja|神祇官}} (Department of Shinto Affairs) of the {{ill|Imperial Court of Japan|ja|朝廷 (日本)}}, the {{ill|Four Seasonal Festivals|ja|四時祭}} designated the '''Ainame Festival''' ({{ill|Ainame Festival|ja|相嘗祭|lt=Ainame-sai|Ainame Festival=Ainame Festival}}), '''Chinkon Festival''' ({{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭}}), and '''Niiname Festival''' ({{ill|Niiname-sai|ja|新嘗祭}}) in the '''11th month of the old calendar''' as '''Winter Festivals'''. Additionally, although the '''Tsukinami Festival''' ({{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭}}), '''Chinka (Fire-Calming Ritual)''' ({{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|ja|鎮火|Fire-Pacification Festival=Fire-Pacification Festival|lt=Chinka}}), and '''Dōkyō Festival''' ({{ill|Dōkyō|ja|道饗}}) were not inherently winter-specific, they were conducted as part of the Winter Festival period.
Today, many '''Winter Festivals''' retain elements of the '''Chinkon Festival (Ritual for Soul-Pacification)''', and practices such as '''Yutate (Hot Water Purification Rituals)''' ({{ill|Yutate|ja|湯立}}) and '''Kagura (Sacred Dance and Music)''' ({{ill|Kagura|ja|神楽}}), which originated from '''Shimotsuki Kagura''' ({{ill|Shimotsuki Kagura|ja|霜月神楽}}), are often performed.

{{出典の明記|date=2017年2月8日 (水) 11:32 (UTC)}} [[File:Ako Gishisai De09 13.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Ako Goshi Festival|ja|赤穂義士祭}}]] Winter festivals or '''Winter Matsuri''' (冬祭り) refer to [[festivals]] held during the [[winter]] season. These events typically occur over the three-month period from [[:en:Lidong|Rittō]] (the beginning of winter) to [[Risshun]] (the beginning of spring), corresponding to [[November]], [[December]], and [[January]].
== Overview == The '''Winter Festival''' incorporates both the '''harvest festival elements''' of the {{ill|Autumn festival|ja|秋祭り}} and the '''prayer for the year's blessings elements''' of the {{ill|Spring festival|ja|春祭り}}, making its exact positioning somewhat unclear. In ancient times, the '''10th month of the old calendar''' ({{ill|Kannazuki|ja|神無月}}) was considered an '''unlucky month''' ({{ill|Imizuki|ja|忌月}}).<ref>Although the unlucky months are commonly introduced as January, May, and September in the modern calendar, in the old calendar, they were roughly one month later, making the 10th month of the old calendar an unlucky month.</ref> Thus, the '''harvest festival''' was originally held in the '''11th month of the old calendar''', after the month changed and winter began. However, as the '''taboos''' surrounding the 10th month faded, the '''Autumn Festival, held immediately after the harvest''', became established.
{{ill|Orikuchi Shinobu|ja|折口信夫}} viewed this period, spanning from autumn to the following spring, as a time of '''spiritual renewal''' ({{ill|Chinkon|ja|鎮魂}}), during which all souls, including human ones, were refreshed. He theorized that the '''Autumn Festival, Winter Festival, and Spring Festival''' were originally '''one continuous ritual cycle''', dealing with '''harvest, soul renewal, and prayers for the coming year'''. Over time, he suggested, the '''Autumn and Spring Festivals split off''', leaving the remainder as the '''Winter Festival'''.
Similarly, {{ill|Yanagita Kunio|ja|柳田國男}} pointed out that the '''11th month of the old calendar''', which includes the '''Winter Solstice''', was a turning point toward spring. He argued that Japanese festivals traditionally placed great importance on this month.
In the {{ill|Jingikan|ja|神祇官}} (Department of Shinto Affairs) of the {{ill|Imperial Court of Japan|ja|朝廷 (日本)}}, the {{ill|Four Seasonal Festivals|ja|四時祭}} designated the '''Ainame Festival''' ({{ill|Ainame Festival|ja|相嘗祭|lt=Ainame-sai|Ainame Festival=Ainame Festival}}), '''Chinkon Festival''' ({{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭}}), and '''Niiname Festival''' ({{ill|Niiname-sai|ja|新嘗祭}}) in the '''11th month of the old calendar''' as '''Winter Festivals'''. Additionally, although the '''Tsukinami Festival''' ({{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭}}), '''Chinka (Fire-Calming Ritual)''' ({{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|ja|鎮火|Fire-Pacification Festival=Fire-Pacification Festival|lt=Chinka}}), and '''Dōkyō Festival''' ({{ill|Dōkyō|ja|道饗}}) were not inherently winter-specific, they were conducted as part of the Winter Festival period.
Today, many '''Winter Festivals''' retain elements of the '''Chinkon Festival (Ritual for Soul-Pacification)''', and practices such as '''Yutate (Hot Water Purification Rituals)''' ({{ill|Yutate|ja|湯立}}) and '''Kagura (Sacred Dance and Music)''' ({{ill|Kagura|ja|神楽}}), which originated from '''Shimotsuki Kagura''' ({{ill|Shimotsuki Kagura|ja|霜月神楽}}), are often performed.

{{出典の明記|date=2017年2月8日 (水) 11:32 (UTC)}} [[File:Ako Gishisai De09 13.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Ako Goshi Festival|ja|赤穂義士祭}}]] Winter festivals or '''Winter Matsuri''' (冬祭り) refer to [[festivals]] held during the [[winter]] season. These events typically occur over the three-month period from [[:en:Lidong|Rittō]] (the beginning of winter) to [[Risshun]] (the beginning of spring), corresponding to [[November]], [[December]], and [[January]].
== Overview == The '''Winter Festival''' incorporates both the '''harvest festival elements''' of the {{ill|Autumn festival|ja|秋祭り}} and the '''prayer for the year's blessings elements''' of the {{ill|Spring festival|ja|春祭り}}, making its exact positioning somewhat unclear. In ancient times, the '''10th month of the old calendar''' ({{ill|Kannazuki|ja|神無月}}) was considered an '''unlucky month''' ({{ill|Imizuki|ja|忌月}}).<ref>Although the unlucky months are commonly introduced as January, May, and September in the modern calendar, in the old calendar, they were roughly one month later, making the 10th month of the old calendar an unlucky month.</ref> Thus, the '''harvest festival''' was originally held in the '''11th month of the old calendar''', after the month changed and winter began. However, as the '''taboos''' surrounding the 10th month faded, the '''Autumn Festival, held immediately after the harvest''', became established.
{{ill|Orikuchi Shinobu|ja|折口信夫}} viewed this period, spanning from autumn to the following spring, as a time of '''spiritual renewal''' ({{ill|Chinkon|ja|鎮魂}}), during which all souls, including human ones, were refreshed. He theorized that the '''Autumn Festival, Winter Festival, and Spring Festival''' were originally '''one continuous ritual cycle''', dealing with '''harvest, soul renewal, and prayers for the coming year'''. Over time, he suggested, the '''Autumn and Spring Festivals split off''', leaving the remainder as the '''Winter Festival'''.
Similarly, {{ill|Yanagita Kunio|ja|柳田國男}} pointed out that the '''11th month of the old calendar''', which includes the '''Winter Solstice''', was a turning point toward spring. He argued that Japanese festivals traditionally placed great importance on this month.
In the {{ill|Jingikan|ja|神祇官}} (Department of Shinto Affairs) of the {{ill|Imperial Court of Japan|ja|朝廷 (日本)}}, the {{ill|Four Seasonal Festivals|ja|四時祭}} designated the '''Ainame Festival''' ({{ill|Ainame Festival|ja|相嘗祭|lt=Ainame-sai|Ainame Festival=Ainame Festival}}), '''Chinkon Festival''' ({{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭}}), and '''Niiname Festival''' ({{ill|Niiname-sai|ja|新嘗祭}}) in the '''11th month of the old calendar''' as '''Winter Festivals'''. Additionally, although the '''Tsukinami Festival''' ({{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭}}), '''Chinka (Fire-Calming Ritual)''' ({{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|ja|鎮火|Fire-Pacification Festival=Fire-Pacification Festival|lt=Chinka}}), and '''Dōkyō Festival''' ({{ill|Dōkyō|ja|道饗}}) were not inherently winter-specific, they were conducted as part of the Winter Festival period.
Today, many '''Winter Festivals''' retain elements of the '''Chinkon Festival (Ritual for Soul-Pacification)''', and practices such as '''Yutate (Hot Water Purification Rituals)''' ({{ill|Yutate|ja|湯立}}) and '''Kagura (Sacred Dance and Music)''' ({{ill|Kagura|ja|神楽}}), which originated from '''Shimotsuki Kagura''' ({{ill|Shimotsuki Kagura|ja|霜月神楽}}), are often performed.

{{出典の明記|date=2017年2月8日 (水) 11:32 (UTC)}} [[File:Ako Gishisai De09 13.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Ako Goshi Festival|ja|赤穂義士祭}}]] Winter festivals or '''Winter Matsuri''' (冬祭り) refer to [[festivals]] held during the [[winter]] season. These events typically occur over the three-month period from [[:en:Lidong|Rittō]] (the beginning of winter) to [[Risshun]] (the beginning of spring), corresponding to [[November]], [[December]], and [[January]].
== Overview == The '''Winter Festival''' incorporates both the '''harvest festival elements''' of the {{ill|Autumn festival|ja|秋祭り}} and the '''prayer for the year's blessings elements''' of the {{ill|Spring festival|ja|春祭り}}, making its exact positioning somewhat unclear. In ancient times, the '''10th month of the old calendar''' ({{ill|Kannazuki|ja|神無月}}) was considered an '''unlucky month''' ({{ill|Imizuki|ja|忌月}}).<ref>Although the unlucky months are commonly introduced as January, May, and September in the modern calendar, in the old calendar, they were roughly one month later, making the 10th month of the old calendar an unlucky month.</ref> Thus, the '''harvest festival''' was originally held in the '''11th month of the old calendar''', after the month changed and winter began. However, as the '''taboos''' surrounding the 10th month faded, the '''Autumn Festival, held immediately after the harvest''', became established.
{{ill|Orikuchi Shinobu|ja|折口信夫}} viewed this period, spanning from autumn to the following spring, as a time of '''spiritual renewal''' ({{ill|Chinkon|ja|鎮魂}}), during which all souls, including human ones, were refreshed. He theorized that the '''Autumn Festival, Winter Festival, and Spring Festival''' were originally '''one continuous ritual cycle''', dealing with '''harvest, soul renewal, and prayers for the coming year'''. Over time, he suggested, the '''Autumn and Spring Festivals split off''', leaving the remainder as the '''Winter Festival'''.[[Category:Completion tier 2]]
Similarly, {{ill|Yanagita Kunio|ja|柳田國男}} pointed out that the '''11th month of the old calendar''', which includes the '''Winter Solstice''', was a turning point toward spring. He argued that Japanese festivals traditionally placed great importance on this month.
In the {{ill|Jingikan|ja|神祇官}} (Department of Shinto Affairs) of the {{ill|Imperial Court of Japan|ja|朝廷 (日本)}}, the {{ill|Four Seasonal Festivals|ja|四時祭}} designated the '''Ainame Festival''' ({{ill|Ainame Festival|ja|相嘗祭|lt=Ainame-sai|Ainame Festival=Ainame Festival}}), '''Chinkon Festival''' ({{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭}}), and '''Niiname Festival''' ({{ill|Niiname-sai|ja|新嘗祭}}) in the '''11th month of the old calendar''' as '''Winter Festivals'''. Additionally, although the '''Tsukinami Festival''' ({{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭}}), '''Chinka (Fire-Calming Ritual)''' ({{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|ja|鎮火|Fire-Pacification Festival=Fire-Pacification Festival|lt=Chinka}}), and '''Dōkyō Festival''' ({{ill|Dōkyō|ja|道饗}}) were not inherently winter-specific, they were conducted as part of the Winter Festival period.
Today, many '''Winter Festivals''' retain elements of the '''Chinkon Festival (Ritual for Soul-Pacification)''', and practices such as '''Yutate (Hot Water Purification Rituals)''' ({{ill|Yutate|ja|湯立}}) and '''Kagura (Sacred Dance and Music)''' ({{ill|Kagura|ja|神楽}}), which originated from '''Shimotsuki Kagura''' ({{ill|Shimotsuki Kagura|ja|霜月神楽}}), are often performed.

{{出典の明記|date=2017年2月8日 (水) 11:32 (UTC)}} [[File:Ako Gishisai De09 13.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Ako Goshi Festival|ja|赤穂義士祭}}]] Winter festivals or '''Winter Matsuri''' (冬祭り) refer to [[festivals]] held during the [[winter]] season. These events typically occur over the three-month period from [[:en:Lidong|Rittō]] (the beginning of winter) to [[Risshun]] (the beginning of spring), corresponding to [[November]], [[December]], and [[January]].
== Overview == The '''Winter Festival''' incorporates both the '''harvest festival elements''' of the {{ill|Autumn festival|ja|秋祭り}} and the '''prayer for the year's blessings elements''' of the {{ill|Spring festival|ja|春祭り}}, making its exact positioning somewhat unclear. In ancient times, the '''10th month of the old calendar''' ({{ill|Kannazuki|ja|神無月}}) was considered an '''unlucky month''' ({{ill|Imizuki|ja|忌月}}).<ref>Although the unlucky months are commonly introduced as January, May, and September in the modern calendar, in the old calendar, they were roughly one month later, making the 10th month of the old calendar an unlucky month.</ref> Thus, the '''harvest festival''' was originally held in the '''11th month of the old calendar''', after the month changed and winter began. However, as the '''taboos''' surrounding the 10th month faded, the '''Autumn Festival, held immediately after the harvest''', became established.
{{ill|Orikuchi Shinobu|ja|折口信夫}} viewed this period, spanning from autumn to the following spring, as a time of '''spiritual renewal''' ({{ill|Chinkon|ja|鎮魂}}), during which all souls, including human ones, were refreshed. He theorized that the '''Autumn Festival, Winter Festival, and Spring Festival''' were originally '''one continuous ritual cycle''', dealing with '''harvest, soul renewal, and prayers for the coming year'''. Over time, he suggested, the '''Autumn and Spring Festivals split off''', leaving the remainder as the '''Winter Festival'''.[[Category:Completion tier 2]]
Similarly, {{ill|Yanagita Kunio|ja|柳田國男}} pointed out that the '''11th month of the old calendar''', which includes the '''Winter Solstice''', was a turning point toward spring. He argued that Japanese festivals traditionally placed great importance on this month.
In the {{ill|Jingikan|ja|神祇官}} (Department of Shinto Affairs) of the {{ill|Imperial Court of Japan|ja|朝廷 (日本)}}, the {{ill|Four Seasonal Festivals|ja|四時祭}} designated the '''Ainame Festival''' ({{ill|Ainame Festival|ja|相嘗祭|lt=Ainame-sai|Ainame Festival=Ainame Festival}}), '''Chinkon Festival''' ({{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭}}), and '''Niiname Festival''' ({{ill|Niiname-sai|ja|新嘗祭}}) in the '''11th month of the old calendar''' as '''Winter Festivals'''. Additionally, although the '''Tsukinami Festival''' ({{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭}}), '''Chinka (Fire-Calming Ritual)''' ({{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|ja|鎮火|Fire-Pacification Festival=Fire-Pacification Festival|lt=Chinka}}), and '''Dōkyō Festival''' ({{ill|Dōkyō|ja|道饗}}) were not inherently winter-specific, they were conducted as part of the Winter Festival period.
Today, many '''Winter Festivals''' retain elements of the '''Chinkon Festival (Ritual for Soul-Pacification)''', and practices such as '''Yutate (Hot Water Purification Rituals)''' ({{ill|Yutate|ja|湯立}}) and '''Kagura (Sacred Dance and Music)''' ({{ill|Kagura|ja|神楽}}), which originated from '''Shimotsuki Kagura''' ({{ill|Shimotsuki Kagura|ja|霜月神楽}}), are often performed.

{{出典の明記|date=2017年2月8日 (水) 11:32 (UTC)}} [[File:Ako Gishisai De09 13.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Ako Goshi Festival|ja|赤穂義士祭}}]] Winter festivals or '''Winter Matsuri''' (冬祭り) refer to [[festivals]] held during the [[winter]] season. These events typically occur over the three-month period from [[:en:Lidong|Rittō]] (the beginning of winter) to [[Risshun]] (the beginning of spring), corresponding to [[November]], [[December]], and [[January]].
== Overview == The '''Winter Festival''' incorporates both the '''harvest festival elements''' of the {{ill|Autumn festival|ja|秋祭り}} and the '''prayer for the year's blessings elements''' of the {{ill|Spring festival|ja|春祭り}}, making its exact positioning somewhat unclear. In ancient times, the '''10th month of the old calendar''' ({{ill|Kannazuki|ja|神無月}}) was considered an '''unlucky month''' ({{ill|Imizuki|ja|忌月}}).<ref>Although the unlucky months are commonly introduced as January, May, and September in the modern calendar, in the old calendar, they were roughly one month later, making the 10th month of the old calendar an unlucky month.</ref> Thus, the '''harvest festival''' was originally held in the '''11th month of the old calendar''', after the month changed and winter began. However, as the '''taboos''' surrounding the 10th month faded, the '''Autumn Festival, held immediately after the harvest''', became established.
{{ill|Orikuchi Shinobu|ja|折口信夫}} viewed this period, spanning from autumn to the following spring, as a time of '''spiritual renewal''' ({{ill|Chinkon|ja|鎮魂}}), during which all souls, including human ones, were refreshed. He theorized that the '''Autumn Festival, Winter Festival, and Spring Festival''' were originally '''one continuous ritual cycle''', dealing with '''harvest, soul renewal, and prayers for the coming year'''. Over time, he suggested, the '''Autumn and Spring Festivals split off''', leaving the remainder as the '''Winter Festival'''.[[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Fuyu Matsuri]][[Categoery:qq]] Similarly, {{ill|Yanagita Kunio|ja|柳田國男}} pointed out that the '''11th month of the old calendar''', which includes the '''Winter Solstice''', was a turning point toward spring. He argued that Japanese festivals traditionally placed great importance on this month.
In the {{ill|Jingikan|ja|神祇官}} (Department of Shinto Affairs) of the {{ill|Imperial Court of Japan|ja|朝廷 (日本)}}, the {{ill|Four Seasonal Festivals|ja|四時祭}} designated the '''Ainame Festival''' ({{ill|Ainame Festival|ja|相嘗祭|lt=Ainame-sai|Ainame Festival=Ainame Festival}}), '''Chinkon Festival''' ({{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭}}), and '''Niiname Festival''' ({{ill|Niiname-sai|ja|新嘗祭}}) in the '''11th month of the old calendar''' as '''Winter Festivals'''. Additionally, although the '''Tsukinami Festival''' ({{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭}}), '''Chinka (Fire-Calming Ritual)''' ({{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|ja|鎮火|Fire-Pacification Festival=Fire-Pacification Festival|lt=Chinka}}), and '''Dōkyō Festival''' ({{ill|Dōkyō|ja|道饗}}) were not inherently winter-specific, they were conducted as part of the Winter Festival period.
Today, many '''Winter Festivals''' retain elements of the '''Chinkon Festival (Ritual for Soul-Pacification)''', and practices such as '''Yutate (Hot Water Purification Rituals)''' ({{ill|Yutate|ja|湯立}}) and '''Kagura (Sacred Dance and Music)''' ({{ill|Kagura|ja|神楽}}), which originated from '''Shimotsuki Kagura''' ({{ill|Shimotsuki Kagura|ja|霜月神楽}}), are often performed.
== Notes == {{reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Spring Festivals|en}} * [[Summer festivals]] * {{ill|Autumn Festivals|en}} * [[:en:Winter_break|Winter break]] * {{DEFAULTSORT:Fuyumatsuri}} [[Category:Japanese culture]] [[Category:Festivals in Japan]] [[Category:Festivals]] [[Category:Winter events]]
* [[:en:Yule|Yule]] – Ancient European winter solstice celebrations

{{出典の明記|date=2017年2月8日 (水) 11:32 (UTC)}} [[File:Ako Gishisai De09 13.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Ako Goshi Festival|ja|赤穂義士祭}}]] Winter festivals or '''Winter Matsuri''' (冬祭り) refer to [[festivals]] held during the [[winter]] season. These events typically occur over the three-month period from [[:en:Lidong|Rittō]] (the beginning of winter) to [[Risshun]] (the beginning of spring), corresponding to [[November]], [[December]], and [[January]].

{{ill|Orikuchi Shinobu|ja|折口信夫}} viewed this period, spanning from autumn to the following spring, as a time of '''spiritual renewal''' ({{ill|Chinkon|ja|鎮魂}}), during which all souls, including human ones, were refreshed. He theorized that the '''Autumn Festival, Winter Festival, and Spring Festival''' were originally '''one continuous ritual cycle''', dealing with '''harvest, soul renewal, and prayers for the coming year'''. Over time, he suggested, the '''Autumn and Spring Festivals split off''', leaving the remainder as the '''Winter Festival'''.[[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Fuyu Matsuri]][[Category: :qq]] Similarly, {{ill|Yanagita Kunio|ja|柳田國男}} pointed out that the '''11th month of the old calendar''', which includes the '''Winter Solstice''', was a turning point toward spring. {{出典の明記|date=2017年2月8日 (水) 11:32 (UTC)}} [[File:Ako Gishisai De09 13.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Ako Goshi Festival|ja|赤穂義士祭}}]] Winter festivals or '''Winter Matsuri''' (冬祭り) refer to [[festivals]] held during the [[winter]] season. These events typically occur over the three-month period from [[:en:Lidong|Rittō]] (the beginning of winter) to [[Risshun]] (the beginning of spring), corresponding to [[November]], [[December]], and [[January]].
== Overview == The '''Winter Festival''' incorporates both the '''harvest festival elements''' of the {{ill|Autumn festival|ja|秋祭り}} and the '''prayer for the year's blessings elements''' of the {{ill|Spring festival|ja|春祭り}}, making its exact positioning somewhat unclear. In ancient times, the '''10th month of the old calendar''' ({{ill|Kannazuki|ja|神無月}}) was considered an '''unlucky month''' ({{ill|Imizuki|ja|忌月}}).<ref>Although the unlucky months are commonly introduced as January, May, and September in the modern calendar, in the old calendar, they were roughly one month later, making the 10th month of the old calendar an unlucky month.</ref> Thus, the '''harvest festival''' was originally held in the '''11th month of the old calendar''', after the month changed and winter began. However, as the '''taboos''' surrounding the 10th month faded, the '''Autumn Festival, held immediately after the harvest''', became established.
{{ill|Orikuchi Shinobu|ja|折口信夫}} viewed this period, spanning from autumn to the following spring, as a time of '''spiritual renewal''' ({{ill|Chinkon|ja|鎮魂}}), during which all souls, including human ones, were refreshed. He theorized that the '''Autumn Festival, Winter Festival, and Spring Festival''' were originally '''one continuous ritual cycle''', dealing with '''harvest, soul renewal, and prayers for the coming year'''. Over time, he suggested, the '''Autumn and Spring Festivals split off''', leaving the remainder as the '''Winter Festival'''.[[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Fuyu Matsuri]][[Category: :qq]] Similarly, {{ill|Yanagita Kunio|ja|柳田國男}} pointed out that the '''11th month of the old calendar''', which includes the '''Winter Solstice''', was a turning point toward spring. He argued that Japanese festivals traditionally placed great importance on this month.
In the {{ill|Jingikan|ja|神祇官}} (Department of Shinto Affairs) of the {{ill|Imperial Court of Japan|ja|朝廷 (日本)}}, the {{ill|Four Seasonal Festivals|ja|四時祭}} designated the '''Ainame Festival''' ({{ill|Ainame Festival|ja|相嘗祭|lt=Ainame-sai|Ainame Festival=Ainame Festival}}), '''Chinkon Festival''' ({{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭}}), and '''Niiname Festival''' ({{ill|Niiname-sai|ja|新嘗祭}}) in the '''11th month of the old calendar''' as '''Winter Festivals'''. Additionally, although the '''Tsukinami Festival''' ({{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭}}), '''Chinka (Fire-Calming Ritual)''' ({{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|ja|鎮火|Fire-Pacification Festival=Fire-Pacification Festival|lt=Chinka}}), and '''Dōkyō Festival''' ({{ill|Dōkyō|ja|道饗}}) were not inherently winter-specific, they were conducted as part of the Winter Festival period.
Today, many '''Winter Festivals''' retain elements of the '''Chinkon Festival (Ritual for Soul-Pacification)''', and practices such as '''Yutate (Hot Water Purification Rituals)''' ({{ill|Yutate|ja|湯立}}) and '''Kagura (Sacred Dance and Music)''' ({{ill|Kagura|ja|神楽}}), which originated from '''Shimotsuki Kagura''' ({{ill|Shimotsuki Kagura|ja|霜月神楽}}), are often performed.

{{出典の明記|date=2017年2月8日 (水) 11:32 (UTC)}} [[File:Ako Gishisai De09 13.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Ako Goshi Festival|ja|赤穂義士祭}}]] Winter festivals or '''Winter Matsuri''' (冬祭り) refer to [[festivals]] held during the [[winter]] season. These events typically occur over the three-month period from [[:en:Lidong|Rittō]] (the beginning of winter) to [[Risshun]] (the beginning of spring), corresponding to [[November]], [[December]], and [[January]].
== Overview == The '''Winter Festival''' incorporates both the '''harvest festival elements''' of the {{ill|Autumn festival|ja|秋祭り}} and the '''prayer for the year's blessings elements''' of the {{ill|Spring festival|ja|春祭り}}, making its exact positioning somewhat unclear. In ancient times, the '''10th month of the old calendar''' ({{ill|Kannazuki|ja|神無月}}) was considered an '''unlucky month''' ({{ill|Imizuki|ja|忌月}}).<ref>Although the unlucky months are commonly introduced as January, May, and September in the modern calendar, in the old calendar, they were roughly one month later, making the 10th month of the old calendar an unlucky month.</ref> Thus, the '''harvest festival''' was originally held in the '''11th month of the old calendar''', after the month changed and winter began. However, as the '''taboos''' surrounding the 10th month faded, the '''Autumn Festival, held immediately after the harvest''', became established.
{{ill|Orikuchi Shinobu|ja|折口信夫}} viewed this period, spanning from autumn to the following spring, as a time of '''spiritual renewal''' ({{ill|Chinkon|ja|鎮魂}}), during which all souls, including human ones, were refreshed. He theorized that the '''Autumn Festival, Winter Festival, and Spring Festival''' were originally '''one continuous ritual cycle''', dealing with '''harvest, soul renewal, and prayers for the coming year'''. Over time, he suggested, the '''Autumn and Spring Festivals split off''', leaving the remainder as the '''Winter Festival'''.[[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Fuyu Matsuri]][[Category: :qq]] Similarly, {{ill|Yanagita Kunio|ja|柳田國男}} pointed out that the '''11th month of the old calendar''', which includes the '''Winter Solstice''', was a turning point toward spring. He argued that Japanese festivals traditionally placed great importance on this month.
In the {{ill|Jingikan|ja|神祇官}} (Department of Shinto Affairs) of the {{ill|Imperial Court of Japan|ja|朝廷 (日本)}}, the {{ill|Four Seasonal Festivals|ja|四時祭}} designated the '''Ainame Festival''' ({{ill|Ainame Festival|ja|相嘗祭|lt=Ainame-sai|Ainame Festival=Ainame Festival}}), '''Chinkon Festival''' ({{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭}}), and '''Niiname Festival''' ({{ill|Niiname-sai|ja|新嘗祭}}) in the '''11th month of the old calendar''' as '''Winter Festivals'''. Additionally, although the '''Tsukinami Festival''' ({{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭}}), '''Chinka (Fire-Calming Ritual)''' ({{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|ja|鎮火|Fire-Pacification Festival=Fire-Pacification Festival|lt=Chinka}}), and '''Dōkyō Festival''' ({{ill|Dōkyō|ja|道饗}}) were not inherently winter-specific, they were conducted as part of the Winter Festival period.
Today, many '''Winter Festivals''' retain elements of the '''Chinkon Festival (Ritual for Soul-Pacification)''', and practices such as '''Yutate (Hot Water Purification Rituals)''' ({{ill|Yutate|ja|湯立}}) and '''Kagura (Sacred Dance and Music)''' ({{ill|Kagura|ja|神楽}}), which originated from '''Shimotsuki Kagura''' ({{ill|Shimotsuki Kagura|ja|霜月神楽}}), are often performed.

{{出典の明記|date=2017年2月8日 (水) 11:32 (UTC)}} [[File:Ako Gishisai De09 13.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Ako Goshi Festival|ja|赤穂義士祭}}]] Winter festivals or '''Winter Matsuri''' (冬祭り) refer to [[festivals]] held during the [[winter]] season. These events typically occur over the three-month period from [[:en:Lidong|Rittō]] (the beginning of winter) to [[Risshun]] (the beginning of spring), corresponding to [[November]], [[December]], and [[January]].
== Overview == The '''Winter Festival''' incorporates both the '''harvest festival elements''' of the {{ill|Autumn festival|ja|秋祭り}} and the '''prayer for the year's blessings elements''' of the {{ill|Spring festival|ja|春祭り}}, making its exact positioning somewhat unclear. In ancient times, the '''10th month of the old calendar''' ({{ill|Kannazuki|ja|神無月}}) was considered an '''unlucky month''' ({{ill|Imizuki|ja|忌月}}).<ref>Although the unlucky months are commonly introduced as January, May, and September in the modern calendar, in the old calendar, they were roughly one month later, making the 10th month of the old calendar an unlucky month.</ref> Thus, the '''harvest festival''' was originally held in the '''11th month of the old calendar''', after the month changed and winter began. However, as the '''taboos''' surrounding the 10th month faded, the '''Autumn Festival, held immediately after the harvest''', became established.
{{ill|Orikuchi Shinobu|ja|折口信夫}} viewed this period, spanning from autumn to the following spring, as a time of '''spiritual renewal''' ({{ill|Chinkon|ja|鎮魂}}), during which all souls, including human ones, were refreshed. He theorized that the '''Autumn Festival, Winter Festival, and Spring Festival''' were originally '''one continuous ritual cycle''', dealing with '''harvest, soul renewal, and prayers for the coming year'''. Over time, he suggested, the '''Autumn and Spring Festivals split off''', leaving the remainder as the '''Winter Festival'''.[[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category:tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Fuyu Matsuri]] Similarly, {{ill|Yanagita Kunio|ja|柳田國男}} pointed out that the '''11th month of the old calendar''', which includes the '''Winter Solstice''', was a turning point toward spring. He argued that Japanese festivals traditionally placed great importance on this month.
In the {{ill|Jingikan|ja|神祇官}} (Department of Shinto Affairs) of the {{ill|Imperial Court of Japan|ja|朝廷 (日本)}}, the {{ill|Four Seasonal Festivals|ja|四時祭}} designated the '''Ainame Festival''' ({{ill|Ainame Festival|ja|相嘗祭|lt=Ainame-sai|Ainame Festival=Ainame Festival}}), '''Chinkon Festival''' ({{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭}}), and '''Niiname Festival''' ({{ill|Niiname-sai|ja|新嘗祭}}) in the '''11th month of the old calendar''' as '''Winter Festivals'''. Additionally, although the '''Tsukinami Festival''' ({{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭}}), '''Chinka (Fire-Calming Ritual)''' ({{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|ja|鎮火|Fire-Pacification Festival=Fire-Pacification Festival|lt=Chinka}}), and '''Dōkyō Festival''' ({{ill|Dōkyō|ja|道饗}}) were not inherently winter-specific, they were conducted as part of the Winter Festival period.
Today, many '''Winter Festivals''' retain elements of the '''Chinkon Festival (Ritual for Soul-Pacification)''', and practices such as '''Yutate (Hot Water Purification Rituals)''' ({{ill|Yutate|ja|湯立}}) and '''Kagura (Sacred Dance and Music)''' ({{ill|Kagura|ja|神楽}}), which originated from '''Shimotsuki Kagura''' ({{ill|Shimotsuki Kagura|ja|霜月神楽}}), are often performed.

{{出典の明記|date=2017年2月8日 (水) 11:32 (UTC)}} [[File:Ako Gishisai De09 13.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Ako Goshi Festival|ja|赤穂義士祭}}]] Winter festivals or '''Winter Matsuri''' (冬祭り) refer to [[festivals]] held during the [[winter]] season. These events typically occur over the three-month period from [[:en:Lidong|Rittō]] (the beginning of winter) to [[Risshun]] (the beginning of spring), corresponding to [[November]], [[December]], and [[January]].
== Overview == The '''Winter Festival''' incorporates both the '''harvest festival elements''' of the {{ill|Autumn festival|ja|秋祭り}} and the '''prayer for the year's blessings elements''' of the {{ill|Spring festival|ja|春祭り}}, making its exact positioning somewhat unclear. In ancient times, the '''10th month of the old calendar''' ({{ill|Kannazuki|ja|神無月}}) was considered an '''unlucky month''' ({{ill|Imizuki|ja|忌月}}).<ref>Although the unlucky months are commonly introduced as January, May, and September in the modern calendar, in the old calendar, they were roughly one month later, making the 10th month of the old calendar an unlucky month.</ref> Thus, the '''harvest festival''' was originally held in the '''11th month of the old calendar''', after the month changed and winter began. However, as the '''taboos''' surrounding the 10th month faded, the '''Autumn Festival, held immediately after the harvest''', became established.
{{ill|Orikuchi Shinobu|ja|折口信夫}} viewed this period, spanning from autumn to the following spring, as a time of '''spiritual renewal''' ({{ill|Chinkon|ja|鎮魂}}), during which all souls, including human ones, were refreshed. He theorized that the '''Autumn Festival, Winter Festival, and Spring Festival''' were originally '''one continuous ritual cycle''', dealing with '''harvest, soul renewal, and prayers for the coming year'''. Over time, he suggested, the '''Autumn and Spring Festivals split off''', leaving the remainder as the '''Winter Festival'''.[[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category:tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Fuyu Matsuri]] Similarly, {{ill|Yanagita Kunio|ja|柳田國男}} pointed out that the '''11th month of the old calendar''', which includes the '''Winter Solstice''', was a turning point toward spring. He argued that Japanese festivals traditionally placed great importance on this month.
In the {{ill|Jingikan|ja|神祇官}} (Department of Shinto Affairs) of the {{ill|Imperial Court of Japan|ja|朝廷 (日本)}}, the {{ill|Four Seasonal Festivals|ja|四時祭}} designated the '''Ainame Festival''' ({{ill|Ainame Festival|ja|相嘗祭|lt=Ainame-sai|Ainame Festival=Ainame Festival}}), '''Chinkon Festival''' ({{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭}}), and '''Niiname Festival''' ({{ill|Niiname-sai|ja|新嘗祭}}) in the '''11th month of the old calendar''' as '''Winter Festivals'''. Additionally, although the '''Tsukinami Festival''' ({{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭}}), '''Chinka (Fire-Calming Ritual)''' ({{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|ja|鎮火|Fire-Pacification Festival=Fire-Pacification Festival|lt=Chinka}}), and '''Dōkyō Festival''' ({{ill|Dōkyō|ja|道饗}}) were not inherently winter-specific, they were conducted as part of the Winter Festival period.
Today, many '''Winter Festivals''' retain elements of the '''Chinkon Festival (Ritual for Soul-Pacification)''', and practices such as '''Yutate (Hot Water Purification Rituals)''' ({{ill|Yutate|ja|湯立}}) and '''Kagura (Sacred Dance and Music)''' ({{ill|Kagura|ja|神楽}}), which originated from '''Shimotsuki Kagura''' ({{ill|Shimotsuki Kagura|ja|霜月神楽}}), are often performed.
== Notes == {{reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Spring Festivals|en}} * [[Summer festivals]] * {{ill|Autumn Festivals|en}} * [[:en:Winter_break|Winter break]] * {{DEFAULTSORT:Fuyumatsuri}} [[Category:Japanese culture]] [[Category:Festivals in Japan]] [[Category:Festivals]] [[Category:Winter events]]
* [[:en:Yule|Yule]] – Ancient European winter solstice celebrations

{{出典の明記|date=2017年2月8日 (水) 11:32 (UTC)}} [[File:Ako Gishisai De09 13.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Ako Goshi Festival|ja|赤穂義士祭}}]] Winter festivals or '''Winter Matsuri''' (冬祭り) refer to [[festivals]] held during the [[winter]] season. {{出典の明記|date=2017年2月8日 (水) 11:32 (UTC)}} [[File:Ako Gishisai De09 13.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Ako Goshi Festival|ja|赤穂義士祭}}]] Winter festivals or '''Winter Matsuri''' (冬祭り) refer to [[festivals]] held during the [[winter]] season. These events typically occur over the three-month period from [[:en:Lidong|Rittō]] (the beginning of winter) to [[Risshun]] (the beginning of spring), corresponding to [[November]], [[December]], and [[January]].
== Overview == The '''Winter Festival''' incorporates both the '''harvest festival elements''' of the {{ill|Autumn festival|ja|秋祭り}} and the '''prayer for the year's blessings elements''' of the {{ill|Spring festival|ja|春祭り}}, making its exact positioning somewhat unclear. In ancient times, the '''10th month of the old calendar''' ({{ill|Kannazuki|ja|神無月}}) was considered an '''unlucky month''' ({{ill|Imizuki|ja|忌月}}).<ref>Although the unlucky months are commonly introduced as January, May, and September in the modern calendar, in the old calendar, they were roughly one month later, making the 10th month of the old calendar an unlucky month.</ref> Thus, the '''harvest festival''' was originally held in the '''11th month of the old calendar''', after the month changed and winter began. However, as the '''taboos''' surrounding the 10th month faded, the '''Autumn Festival, held immediately after the harvest''', became established.
{{ill|Orikuchi Shinobu|ja|折口信夫}} viewed this period, spanning from autumn to the following spring, as a time of '''spiritual renewal''' ({{ill|Chinkon|ja|鎮魂}}), during which all souls, including human ones, were refreshed. He theorized that the '''Autumn Festival, Winter Festival, and Spring Festival''' were originally '''one continuous ritual cycle''', dealing with '''harvest, soul renewal, and prayers for the coming year'''. Over time, he suggested, the '''Autumn and Spring Festivals split off''', leaving the remainder as the '''Winter Festival'''.[[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category:Tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Fuyu Matsuri]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Fuyu Matsuri]] Similarly, {{ill|Yanagita Kunio|ja|柳田國男}} pointed out that the '''11th month of the old calendar''', which includes the '''Winter Solstice''', was a turning point toward spring. He argued that Japanese festivals traditionally placed great importance on this month.
In the {{ill|Jingikan|ja|神祇官}} (Department of Shinto Affairs) of the {{ill|Imperial Court of Japan|ja|朝廷 (日本)}}, the {{ill|Four Seasonal Festivals|ja|四時祭}} designated the '''Ainame Festival''' ({{ill|Ainame Festival|ja|相嘗祭|lt=Ainame-sai|Ainame Festival=Ainame Festival}}), '''Chinkon Festival''' ({{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭}}), and '''Niiname Festival''' ({{ill|Niiname-sai|ja|新嘗祭}}) in the '''11th month of the old calendar''' as '''Winter Festivals'''. Additionally, although the '''Tsukinami Festival''' ({{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭}}), '''Chinka (Fire-Calming Ritual)''' ({{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|ja|鎮火|Fire-Pacification Festival=Fire-Pacification Festival|lt=Chinka}}), and '''Dōkyō Festival''' ({{ill|Dōkyō|ja|道饗}}) were not inherently winter-specific, they were conducted as part of the Winter Festival period.
Today, many '''Winter Festivals''' retain elements of the '''Chinkon Festival (Ritual for Soul-Pacification)''', and practices such as '''Yutate (Hot Water Purification Rituals)''' ({{ill|Yutate|ja|湯立}}) and '''Kagura (Sacred Dance and Music)''' ({{ill|Kagura|ja|神楽}}), which originated from '''Shimotsuki Kagura''' ({{ill|Shimotsuki Kagura|ja|霜月神楽}}), are often performed.
