{{出典の明記|date=2017th year 2月8日 (水) dd:32 (UTC)}} [[File:Ako Gishisai De09 13.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Ako Goshi Festival|ja|赤穂義士祭|12=draft}}]] Winter festivals or '''Winter Matsuri''' (冬祭り) refer to [[festivals]] held during the [[winter]] season. These events typically occur over the three-month period from [[:en:Lidong|Rittō]] (the beginning of winter) to [[Risshun]] (the beginning of spring), corresponding to [[November]], [[December]], and [[January]].
== Overview == The '''Winter Festival''' incorporates both the '''harvest festival elements''' of the {{ill|Autumn festival|ja|秋祭り|12=draft}} and the '''prayer for the year's blessings elements''' of the {{ill|Spring festival|ja|春祭り|12=draft}}, making its exact positioning somewhat unclear. In ancient times, the '''10th month of the old calendar''' ({{ill|Kannazuki|ja|神無月|12=draft}}) was considered an '''unlucky month''' ({{ill|Imizuki|ja|忌月|12=draft}}).<ref>Although the unlucky months are commonly introduced as January, May, and September in the modern calendar, in the old calendar, they were roughly one month later, making the 10th month of the old calendar an unlucky month.</ref> Thus, the '''harvest festival''' was originally held in the '''ddth month of the old calendar''', after the month changed and winter began. However, as the '''taboos''' surrounding the 10th month faded, the '''Autumn Festival, held immediately after the harvest''', became established.
{{ill|Orikuchi Shinobu|ja|折口信夫|12=draft}} viewed this period, spanning from autumn to the following spring, as a time of '''spiritual renewal''' ({{ill|Chinkon|ja|鎮魂|12=draft}}), during which all souls, including human ones, were refreshed. He theorized that the '''Autumn Festival, Winter Festival, and Spring Festival''' were originally '''one continuous ritual cycle''', dealing with '''harvest, soul renewal, and prayers for the coming year'''. Over time, he suggested, the '''Autumn and Spring Festivals split off''', leaving the remainder as the '''Winter Festival'''.
Similarly, {{ill|Yanagita Kunio|ja|柳田國男|12=draft}} pointed out that the '''ddth month of the old calendar''', which includes the '''Winter Solstice''', was a turning point toward spring. He argued that Japanese festivals traditionally placed great importance on this month.
In the {{ill|Jingikan|ja|神祇官|1=Department of Divinities|blank=|12=draft}} (Department of Shinto Affairs) of the {{ill|Imperial Court of Japan|ja|朝廷 (日本)|12=draft}}, the {{ill|Four Seasonal Festivals|ja|四時祭|12=draft}} designated the '''Ainame Festival''' ({{ill|Ainame Festival|ja|相嘗祭|lt=Ainame-sai|Ainame Festival=Ainame Festival|12=draft}}), '''Chinkon Festival''' ({{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭|12=draft}}), and '''Niiname Festival''' ({{ill|Niiname-sai|ja|新嘗祭|12=draft}}) in the '''ddth month of the old calendar''' as '''Winter Festivals'''. Additionally, although the '''Tsukinami Festival''' ({{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭|1=Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|12=draft}}), '''Chinka (Fire-Calming Ritual)''' ({{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|ja|鎮火|Fire-Pacification Festival=Fire-Pacification Festival|lt=Chinka|12=draft}}), and '''Dōkyō Festival''' ({{ill|Dōkyō|ja|道饗|12=draft}}) were not inherently winter-specific, they were conducted as part of the Winter Festival period.
Today, many '''Winter Festivals''' retain elements of the '''Chinkon Festival (Ritual for Soul-Pacification)''', and practices such as '''Yutate (Hot Water Purification Rituals)''' ({{ill|Yutate|ja|湯立|12=draft}}) and '''Kagura (Sacred Dance and Music)''' ({{ill|Kagura|ja|神楽|12=draft}}), which originated from '''Shimotsuki Kagura''' ({{ill|Shimotsuki Kagura|ja|霜月神楽|12=draft}}), are often performed.
== Notes == {{reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Spring Festivals|en}} * [[Summer festivals]] * {{ill|Autumn Festivals|en|12=draft}} * [[:en:Winter_break|Winter break]] *
* [[:en:Yule|Yule]] – Ancient European winter solstice celebrations
<!--Fuyu Matsuri--> * {{ill|Tanabata|en}} <!--Fuyu Matsuri--> * {{ill|Community building|en|12=draft}} * <!--Fuyu Matsuri--> <!--Fuyu Matsuri--> <!--Fuyu Matsuri-->
*{{Event-stub}} <!--Fuyu Matsuri--><!--Fuyu Matsuri--><!--Fuyu Matsuri--><!--Fuyu Matsuri--><!--Fuyu Matsuri--><!--Fuyu Matsuri--><!--Fuyu Matsuri--> <!--Fuyu Matsuri--><!--Fuyu Matsuri--><!--Fuyu Matsuri--><!--Fuyu Matsuri--><!--Fuyu Matsuri--><!--Fuyu Matsuri--><!--Fuyu Matsuri--> <!--Fuyu Matsuri--><!--Fuyu Matsuri--><!--Fuyu Matsuri--><!--Fuyu Matsuri--><!--Fuyu Matsuri--><!--Fuyu Matsuri--><!--Fuyu Matsuri--> <!--Fuyu Matsuri--><!--Fuyu Matsuri--><!--Fuyu Matsuri--><!--Fuyu Matsuri--><!--Fuyu Matsuri--><!--Fuyu Matsuri--><!--Fuyu Matsuri--> <!--Fuyu Matsuri--><!--Fuyu Matsuri--><!--Fuyu Matsuri--><!--Fuyu Matsuri--><!--Fuyu Matsuri--><!--Fuyu Matsuri--><!--Fuyu Matsuri--> <!--Fuyu Matsuri-->

revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2017年2月8日 (水) 11:32 (UTC)}} [[File:Ako Gishisai De09 13.jpg|thumb|[[赤穂義士祭]]]] 冬祭および'''冬祭り'''（ふゆまつり）とは[[冬]]に行われる[[祭り]]のことである。[[立冬]]から[[立春]]にかけての3か月間（[[11月]]・[[12月]]・[[1月]]）に行われるものを指す場合が多い。
== 概要 == 冬祭りは、[[秋祭り]]が持つ'''[[収穫祭]]的要素'''と、[[春祭り]]が持つ'''[[祈年祭]]的要素'''の両方を併せ持っており、その位置づけについては不明な部分も多い。古来、[[旧暦]]の[[10月 (旧暦)|10月]]は[[忌月]]とされ<ref>忌月は、新暦においては1月・5月・9月と紹介されるが、旧暦では約1か月づつ遅れるため旧暦の10月が忌月というのは正しい。</ref>、したがって月が変わって冬に突入した旧暦の[[11月 (旧暦)|11月]]に収穫祭を行ったが、後にその[[忌み|禁忌]]が失われて'''収穫直後の秋祭り'''が成立したともされる。
[[折口信夫]]は、秋から翌春にかけてのこの時期が、[[魂振]]、つまり鎮魂を行い、人間を含めた全ての魂が更新されるための時期として捉えた。さらに秋祭り・冬祭り・春祭りを、元来、収穫・魂の更新・祈年を扱う一続きの祭りであったとし、これから秋祭りと春祭りが分離し、残りが冬祭りとなったと説く。
また、[[柳田國男]]は、冬祭りの中心となる旧暦の11月が[[冬至]]を含む時期であり、春に向かう転機であることを指摘し、日本の祭りは本来、旧暦の11月を重要視したと説く。
[[朝廷 (日本)|朝廷]]の[[神祇官]]における[[四時祭]]では旧暦の11月の[[相嘗祭]]や[[鎮魂祭]]、[[新嘗祭]]が冬祭りとされ、翌月の[[月次祭]]や[[鎮火]]、[[道饗]]も、冬特有の祭事ではないものの冬祭りとして挙行されている。
現在、冬祭りとして行われるものは鎮魂祭の要素を残すものが多く、鎮魂祭の際に行われた[[霜月神楽]]を祖とする[[湯立]]や[[神楽]]などが行われることが多い。
== 脚注 == {{reflist}}
== 関連項目 == * [[春祭り]] * [[夏祭り]] * [[秋祭り]] * [[冬休み]] * [[ユール]] - 古代ヨーロッパの冬至の頃の祭り
{{Event-stub}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:ふゆまつり}} [[Category:日本の文化]] [[Category:日本の祭り]] [[Category:祭]] [[Category:冬のイベント]]

revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[冬祭り]] to [[Fuyu Matsuri]] without leaving a redirect: Bot: merge import

== 概要 == 冬祭りは、[[秋祭り]]が持つ'''[[収穫祭]]的要素'''と、[[春祭り]]が持つ'''[[祈年祭]]的要素'''の両方を併せ持っており、その位置づけについては不明な部分も多い。古来、[[旧暦]]の[[10月 (旧暦)|10月]]は[[忌月]]とされ<ref>忌月は、新暦においては1月・5月・9月と紹介されるが、旧暦では約1か月づつ遅れるため旧暦の10月が忌月というのは正しい。</ref>、したがって月が変わって冬に突入した旧暦の[[11月 (旧暦)|11月]]に収穫祭を行ったが、後にその[[忌み|禁忌]]が失われて'''収穫直後の秋祭り'''が成立したともされる。

現在、冬祭りとして行われるものは鎮魂祭の要素を残すものが多く、鎮魂祭の際に行われた[[霜月神楽]]を祖とする[[湯立]]や[[神楽]]などが行われることが多い。
== 脚注 == {{reflist}}
== 関連項目 == * [[春祭り]] * [[夏祭り]] * [[秋祭り]] * [[冬休み]] * [[ユール]] - 古代ヨーロッパの冬至の頃の祭り
{{Event-stub}}

{{出典の明記|date=2017th year 2月8日 (水) dd:32 (UTC)}}
Winter festivals or '''Winter Matsuri''' (冬祭り) refer to [[festivals]] held during the [[winter]] season. These events typically occur over the three-month period from [[:en:Lidong|Rittō]] (the beginning of winter) to [[Risshun]] (the beginning of spring), corresponding to [[November]], [[December]], and [[January]].
== Overview == The '''Winter Festival''' incorporates both the '''harvest festival elements''' of the {{ill|Autumn festival|ja|秋祭り|12=draft}} and the '''prayer for the year's blessings elements''' of the {{ill|Spring festival|ja|春祭り|12=draft}}, making its exact positioning somewhat unclear. In ancient times, the '''10th month of the old calendar''' ({{ill|Kannazuki|ja|神無月|12=draft}}) was considered an '''unlucky month''' ({{ill|Imizuki|ja|忌月|12=draft}}).<ref>Although the unlucky months are commonly introduced as January, May, and September in the modern calendar, in the old calendar, they were roughly one month later, making the 10th month of the old calendar an unlucky month.</ref> Thus, the '''harvest festival''' was originally held in the '''ddth month of the old calendar''', after the month changed and winter began. However, as the '''taboos''' surrounding the 10th month faded, the '''Autumn Festival, held immediately after the harvest''', became established.
{{ill|Orikuchi Shinobu|ja|折口信夫|12=draft}} viewed this period, spanning from autumn to the following spring, as a time of '''spiritual renewal''' ({{ill|Chinkon|ja|鎮魂|12=draft}}), during which all souls, including human ones, were refreshed. He theorized that the '''Autumn Festival, Winter Festival, and Spring Festival''' were originally '''one continuous ritual cycle''', dealing with '''harvest, soul renewal, and prayers for the coming year'''. Over time, he suggested, the '''Autumn and Spring Festivals split off''', leaving the remainder as the '''Winter Festival'''.
Similarly, {{ill|Yanagita Kunio|ja|柳田國男|12=draft}} pointed out that the '''ddth month of the old calendar''', which includes the '''Winter Solstice''', was a turning point toward spring. He argued that Japanese festivals traditionally placed great importance on this month.
In the {{ill|Jingikan|ja|神祇官|1=Department of Divinities|blank=|12=draft}} (Department of Shinto Affairs) of the {{ill|Imperial Court of Japan|ja|朝廷 (日本)|12=draft}}, the {{ill|Four Seasonal Festivals|ja|四時祭|12=draft}} designated the '''Ainame Festival''' ({{ill|Ainame Festival|ja|相嘗祭|lt=Ainame-sai|Ainame Festival=Ainame Festival|12=draft}}), '''Chinkon Festival''' ({{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭|12=draft}}), and '''Niiname Festival''' ({{ill|Niiname-sai|ja|新嘗祭|12=draft}}) in the '''ddth month of the old calendar''' as '''Winter Festivals'''. Additionally, although the '''Tsukinami Festival''' ({{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭|1=Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|12=draft}}), '''Chinka (Fire-Calming Ritual)''' ({{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|ja|鎮火|Fire-Pacification Festival=Fire-Pacification Festival|lt=Chinka|12=draft}}), and '''Dōkyō Festival''' ({{ill|Dōkyō|ja|道饗|12=draft}}) were not inherently winter-specific, they were conducted as part of the Winter Festival period.
Today, many '''Winter Festivals''' retain elements of the '''Chinkon Festival (Ritual for Soul-Pacification)''', and practices such as '''Yutate (Hot Water Purification Rituals)''' ({{ill|Yutate|ja|湯立|12=draft}}) and '''Kagura (Sacred Dance and Music)''' ({{ill|Kagura|ja|神楽|12=draft}}), which originated from '''Shimotsuki Kagura''' ({{ill|Shimotsuki Kagura|ja|霜月神楽|12=draft}}), are often performed.
== Notes == {{reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Spring Festivals|en}} * [[Summer festivals]] * {{ill|Autumn Festivals|en|12=draft}} * [[:en:Winter_break|Winter break]] *
* [[:en:Yule|Yule]] – Ancient European winter solstice celebrations
* {{ill|Tanabata|en}} * {{ill|Community building|en|12=draft}} *
*{{Event-stub}}
[[File:Ako Gishisai De09 13.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Ako Goshi Festival|ja|赤穂義士祭|12=draft}}]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:Fuyumatsuri}} {{translated page|ja|冬祭り|version=103553580|comment=Imported full ja history}}

{{出典の明記|date=2017th year 2月8日 (水) dd:32 (UTC)}}
Winter festivals or '''Winter Matsuri''' (冬祭り) refer to [[festivals]] held during the [[winter]] season. These events typically occur over the three-month period from [[:en:Lidong|Rittō]] (the beginning of winter) to [[Risshun]] (the beginning of spring), corresponding to [[November]], [[December]], and [[January]].
== Overview == The '''Winter Festival''' incorporates both the '''harvest festival elements''' of the {{ill|Autumn festival|ja|秋祭り|qq=draft}} and the '''prayer for the year's blessings elements''' of the {{ill|Spring festival|ja|春祭り|qq=draft}}, making its exact positioning somewhat unclear. In ancient times, the '''10th month of the old calendar''' ({{ill|Kannazuki|ja|神無月|qq=draft}}) was considered an '''unlucky month''' ({{ill|Imizuki|ja|忌月|qq=draft}}).<ref>Although the unlucky months are commonly introduced as January, May, and September in the modern calendar, in the old calendar, they were roughly one month later, making the 10th month of the old calendar an unlucky month.</ref> Thus, the '''harvest festival''' was originally held in the '''ddth month of the old calendar''', after the month changed and winter began. However, as the '''taboos''' surrounding the 10th month faded, the '''Autumn Festival, held immediately after the harvest''', became established.
{{ill|Orikuchi Shinobu|ja|折口信夫|qq=draft}} viewed this period, spanning from autumn to the following spring, as a time of '''spiritual renewal''' ({{ill|Chinkon|ja|鎮魂|qq=draft}}), during which all souls, including human ones, were refreshed. He theorized that the '''Autumn Festival, Winter Festival, and Spring Festival''' were originally '''one continuous ritual cycle''', dealing with '''harvest, soul renewal, and prayers for the coming year'''. Over time, he suggested, the '''Autumn and Spring Festivals split off''', leaving the remainder as the '''Winter Festival'''.
Similarly, {{ill|Yanagita Kunio|ja|柳田國男|qq=draft}} pointed out that the '''ddth month of the old calendar''', which includes the '''Winter Solstice''', was a turning point toward spring. He argued that Japanese festivals traditionally placed great importance on this month.
In the {{ill|Jingikan|ja|神祇官|1=Department of Divinities|blank=|qq=draft}} (Department of Shinto Affairs) of the {{ill|Imperial Court of Japan|ja|朝廷 (日本)|qq=draft}}, the {{ill|Four Seasonal Festivals|ja|四時祭|qq=draft}} designated the '''Ainame Festival''' ({{ill|Ainame Festival|ja|相嘗祭|lt=Ainame-sai|Ainame Festival=Ainame Festival|qq=draft}}), '''Chinkon Festival''' ({{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭|qq=draft}}), and '''Niiname Festival''' ({{ill|Niiname-sai|ja|新嘗祭|qq=draft}}) in the '''ddth month of the old calendar''' as '''Winter Festivals'''. Additionally, although the '''Tsukinami Festival''' ({{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭|1=Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|qq=draft}}), '''Chinka (Fire-Calming Ritual)''' ({{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|ja|鎮火|Fire-Pacification Festival=Fire-Pacification Festival|lt=Chinka|qq=draft}}), and '''Dōkyō Festival''' ({{ill|Dōkyō|ja|道饗|qq=draft}}) were not inherently winter-specific, they were conducted as part of the Winter Festival period.
Today, many '''Winter Festivals''' retain elements of the '''Chinkon Festival (Ritual for Soul-Pacification)''', and practices such as '''Yutate (Hot Water Purification Rituals)''' ({{ill|Yutate|ja|湯立|qq=draft}}) and '''Kagura (Sacred Dance and Music)''' ({{ill|Kagura|ja|神楽|qq=draft}}), which originated from '''Shimotsuki Kagura''' ({{ill|Shimotsuki Kagura|ja|霜月神楽|qq=draft}}), are often performed.

{{出典の明記|date=2017th year 2月8日 (水) dd:32 (UTC)}}
Winter festivals or '''Winter Matsuri''' (冬祭り) refer to [[:en:Festivals|Festivals]] held during the [[winter]] season. These events typically occur over the three-month period from [[:en:Lidong|Rittō]] (the beginning of winter) to [[Risshun]] (the beginning of spring), corresponding to [[November]], [[:en:December|December]], and [[January]].
== Overview == The '''Winter Festival''' incorporates both the '''harvest festival elements''' of the {{ill|Autumn festival|ja|秋祭り|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Autumn festival|qq=}} and the '''prayer for the year's blessings elements''' of the {{ill|Haru Matsuri|lt=Haru Matsuri|lt=Spring festival|ja|春祭り|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spring festival|qq=}}, making its exact positioning somewhat unclear. In ancient times, the '''10th month of the old calendar''' ({{ill|Kannazuki|ja|神無月|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannazuki|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}) was considered an '''unlucky month''' ({{ill|Imizuki|ja|忌月|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imizuki|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}).<ref>Although the unlucky months are commonly introduced as January, May, and September in the modern calendar, in the old calendar, they were roughly one month later, making the 10th month of the old calendar an unlucky month.</ref> Thus, the '''harvest festival''' was originally held in the '''ddth month of the old calendar''', after the month changed and winter began. However, as the '''taboos''' surrounding the 10th month faded, the '''Autumn Festival, held immediately after the harvest''', became established.
[[:en:Shinobu Orikuchi|Orikuchi Shinobu]] viewed this period, spanning from autumn to the following spring, as a time of '''spiritual renewal''' ({{ill|Chinkon|ja|鎮魂|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinkon|qq=}}), during which all souls, including human ones, were refreshed. He theorized that the '''Autumn Festival, Winter Festival, and Spring Festival''' were originally '''one continuous ritual cycle''', dealing with '''harvest, soul renewal, and prayers for the coming year'''. Over time, he suggested, the '''Autumn and Spring Festivals split off''', leaving the remainder as the '''Winter Festival'''.
Similarly, [[:en:Kunio Yanagita|Yanagita Kunio]] pointed out that the '''ddth month of the old calendar''', which includes the '''Winter Solstice''', was a turning point toward spring. He argued that Japanese festivals traditionally placed great importance on this month.
In the [[:en:Department of Divinities|Department of Divinities]] (Department of Shinto Affairs) of the {{ill|Imperial Court of Japan|ja|朝廷 (日本)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Court of Japan|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, the {{ill|Four Seasonal Festivals|ja|四時祭|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Four Seasonal Festivals|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} designated the '''Ainame Festival''' ({{ill|Ainame Festival|ja|相嘗祭|lt=Ainame-sai|Ainame Festival=Ainame Festival|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ainame Festival|qq=}}), '''Chinkon Festival''' ({{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinkon-sai|qq=}}), and '''Niiname Festival''' ([[:en:Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niiname-sai]]) in the '''ddth month of the old calendar''' as '''Winter Festivals'''. Additionally, although the '''Tsukinami Festival''' ({{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭|1=Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukinami-sai|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|qq=}}), '''Chinka (Fire-Calming Ritual)''' ({{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|ja|鎮火|Fire-Pacification Festival=Fire-Pacification Festival|lt=Chinka|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fire-Pacification Festival|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 消火}}), and '''Dōkyō Festival''' ({{ill|Dōkyō|ja|道饗|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dōkyō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) were not inherently winter-specific, they were conducted as part of the Winter Festival period.
Today, many '''Winter Festivals''' retain elements of the '''Chinkon Festival (Ritual for Soul-Pacification)''', and practices such as '''Yutate (Hot Water Purification Rituals)''' ({{ill|Yutate|ja|湯立|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yutate|qq=}}) and '''Kagura (Sacred Dance and Music)''' ([[:en:Kagura|Kagura]]), which originated from '''Shimotsuki Kagura''' ({{ill|Shimotsuki Kagura|ja|霜月神楽|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimotsuki Kagura|qq=}}), are often performed.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:冬祭り]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q121920335]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:21, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q121920335]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:12, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Referencesの明記|date=2017th year 2月8日 (水) dd:32 (UTC)}}
Winter festivals or '''Winter Matsuri''' (冬祭り) refer to [[:en:Festivals|Festivals]] held during the [[:en:Winter|Winter]] season. These events typically occur over the three-month period from [[:en:Lidong|Rittō]] (the beginning of winter) to [[:en:Risshun|Risshun]] (the beginning of spring), corresponding to [[:en:November|November]], [[:en:December|December]], and [[:en:January|January]].
== Overview == The '''Winter Festival''' incorporates both the '''harvest festival elements''' of the {{ill|Autumn festival|ja|秋祭り|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Autumn festival|qq=|1=Autumn Matsuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11595783}} and the '''prayer for the year's blessings elements''' of the {{ill|Spring Matsuri|lt=Spring Matsuri|lt=Spring festival|ja|春祭り|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spring festival|qq=}}, making its exact positioning somewhat unclear. In ancient times, the '''10th month of the old calendar''' ({{ill|Kannazuki|ja|神無月|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannazuki|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}) was considered an '''unlucky month''' ({{ill|Imizuki|ja|忌月|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imizuki|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}).<ref>Although the unlucky months are commonly introduced as January, May, and September in the modern calendar, in the old calendar, they were roughly one month later, making the 10th month of the old calendar an unlucky month.</ref> Thus, the '''harvest festival''' was originally held in the '''ddth month of the old calendar''', after the month changed and winter began. However, as the '''taboos''' surrounding the 10th month faded, the '''Autumn Festival, held immediately after the harvest''', became established.
[[:en:Shinobu Orikuchi|Orikuchi Shinobu]] viewed this period, spanning from autumn to the following spring, as a time of '''spiritual renewal''' ({{ill|Chinkon|ja|鎮魂|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinkon|qq=|1=chinkon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10297557}}), during which all souls, including human ones, were refreshed. He theorized that the '''Autumn Festival, Winter Festival, and Spring Festival''' were originally '''one continuous ritual cycle''', dealing with '''harvest, soul renewal, and prayers for the coming year'''. Over time, he suggested, the '''Autumn and Spring Festivals split off''', leaving the remainder as the '''Winter Festival'''.
Similarly, [[:en:Kunio Yanagita|Yanagita Kunio]] pointed out that the '''ddth month of the old calendar''', which includes the '''Winter Solstice''', was a turning point toward spring. He argued that Japanese festivals traditionally placed great importance on this month.
In the [[:en:Department of Divinities|Department of Divinities]] (Department of Shinto Affairs) of the {{ill|Imperial Court of Japan|ja|朝廷 (日本)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Court of Japan|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Imperial Court of Japan|WD=Q30253177}}, the {{ill|Four Seasonal Festivals|ja|四時祭|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Four Seasonal Festivals|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} designated the '''Ainame Festival''' ({{ill|Ainame Festival|ja|相嘗祭|lt=Ainame-sai|Ainame Festival=Ainame Festival|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ainame Festival|qq=}}), '''Chinkon Festival''' ({{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinkon-sai|qq=}}), and '''Niiname Festival''' ([[:en:Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niiname-sai]]) in the '''ddth month of the old calendar''' as '''Winter Festivals'''. Additionally, although the '''Tsukinami Festival''' ({{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭|1=Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukinami-sai|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|qq=}}), '''Chinka (Fire-Calming Ritual)''' ({{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|ja|鎮火|Fire-Pacification Festival=Fire-Pacification Festival|lt=Chinka|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fire-Pacification Festival|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 消火}}), and '''Dōkyō Festival''' ({{ill|Dōkyō|ja|道饗|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dōkyō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) were not inherently winter-specific, they were conducted as part of the Winter Festival period.
Today, many '''Winter Festivals''' retain elements of the '''Chinkon Festival (Ritual for Soul-Pacification)''', and practices such as '''Yutate (Hot Water Purification Rituals)''' ({{ill|Yutate|ja|湯立|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yutate|qq=|1=yudate|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563775}}) and '''Kagura (Sacred Dance and Music)''' ([[:en:Kagura|Kagura]]), which originated from '''Shimotsuki Kagura''' ({{ill|Shimotsuki Kagura|ja|霜月神楽|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimotsuki Kagura|qq=}}), are often performed.
== Notes == {{reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Spring Matsuri|lt=Spring Matsuri|lt=Spring Festivals|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spring Festivals|qq=}} * [[Summer Matsuri|Summer festivals]] * {{ill|Autumn Matsuri|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Autumn Festivals|qq=}} * [[:en:Winter_break|Winter break]] *
* [[:en:Yule|Yule]] – Ancient European winter solstice celebrations
* {{ill|Tanabata|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanabata|qq=}} * {{ill|Community building|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Community building|qq=}} *
*{{Event-stub}}

Winter festivals or '''Winter Matsuri''' (冬祭り) refer to [[:en:Festivals|Festivals]] held during the [[:en:Winter|Winter]] season. These events typically occur over the three-month period from [[:en:Lidong|Rittō]] (the beginning of winter) to [[:en:Risshun|Risshun]] (the beginning of spring), corresponding to [[:en:November|November]], [[:en:December|December]], and [[:en:January|January]].
== Overview == The '''Winter Festival''' incorporates both the '''harvest festival elements''' of the {{ill|Autumn festival|ja|秋祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Autumn festival|1=Autumn Matsuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11595783}} and the '''prayer for the year's blessings elements''' of the {{ill|Spring Matsuri|lt=Spring Matsuri|lt=Spring festival|ja|春祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spring festival|qq=}}, making its exact positioning somewhat unclear. In ancient times, the '''10th month of the old calendar''' ({{ill|Kannazuki|ja|神無月|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannazuki|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}) was considered an '''unlucky month''' ({{ill|Imizuki|ja|忌月|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imizuki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}).<ref>Although the unlucky months are commonly introduced as January, May, and September in the modern calendar, in the old calendar, they were roughly one month later, making the 10th month of the old calendar an unlucky month.</ref> Thus, the '''harvest festival''' was originally held in the '''ddth month of the old calendar''', after the month changed and winter began. However, as the '''taboos''' surrounding the 10th month faded, the '''Autumn Festival, held immediately after the harvest''', became established.
[[:en:Shinobu Orikuchi|Orikuchi Shinobu]] viewed this period, spanning from autumn to the following spring, as a time of '''spiritual renewal''' ({{ill|Chinkon|ja|鎮魂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinkon|1=chinkon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10297557}}), during which all souls, including human ones, were refreshed. He theorized that the '''Autumn Festival, Winter Festival, and Spring Festival''' were originally '''one continuous ritual cycle''', dealing with '''harvest, soul renewal, and prayers for the coming year'''. Over time, he suggested, the '''Autumn and Spring Festivals split off''', leaving the remainder as the '''Winter Festival'''.
Similarly, [[:en:Kunio Yanagita|Yanagita Kunio]] pointed out that the '''ddth month of the old calendar''', which includes the '''Winter Solstice''', was a turning point toward spring. He argued that Japanese festivals traditionally placed great importance on this month.
In the [[:en:Department of Divinities|Department of Divinities]] (Department of Shinto Affairs) of the {{ill|Imperial Court of Japan|ja|朝廷 (日本)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Court of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Imperial Court of Japan|WD=Q30253177}}, the {{ill|Four Seasonal Festivals|ja|四時祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Four Seasonal Festivals|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} designated the '''Ainame Festival''' ({{ill|Ainame Festival|ja|相嘗祭|lt=Ainame-sai|Ainame Festival=Ainame Festival|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ainame Festival|qq=}}), '''Chinkon Festival''' ({{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinkon-sai|qq=}}), and '''Niiname Festival''' ([[:en:Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niiname-sai]]) in the '''ddth month of the old calendar''' as '''Winter Festivals'''. Additionally, although the '''Tsukinami Festival''' ({{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭|1=Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukinami-sai|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|qq=}}), '''Chinka (Fire-Calming Ritual)''' ({{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|ja|鎮火|Fire-Pacification Festival=Fire-Pacification Festival|lt=Chinka|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fire-Pacification Festival|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 消火}}), and '''Dōkyō Festival''' ({{ill|Dōkyō|ja|道饗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dōkyō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) were not inherently winter-specific, they were conducted as part of the Winter Festival period.
Today, many '''Winter Festivals''' retain elements of the '''Chinkon Festival (Ritual for Soul-Pacification)''', and practices such as '''Yutate (Hot Water Purification Rituals)''' ({{ill|Yutate|ja|湯立|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yutate|1=yudate|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563775}}) and '''Kagura (Sacred Dance and Music)''' ([[:en:Kagura|Kagura]]), which originated from '''Shimotsuki Kagura''' ({{ill|Shimotsuki Kagura|ja|霜月神楽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimotsuki Kagura|qq=}}), are often performed.
== Notes == {{reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Spring Matsuri|lt=Spring Matsuri|lt=Spring Festivals|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spring Festivals|qq=}} * [[Summer Matsuri|Summer festivals]] * {{ill|Autumn Matsuri|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Autumn Festivals|qq=}} * [[:en:Winter_break|Winter break]] *
* [[:en:Yule|Yule]] – Ancient European winter solstice celebrations
* {{ill|Tanabata|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanabata|qq=}} * {{ill|Community building|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Community building|qq=}} *

Winter festivals or '''Winter Matsuri''' (冬祭り) refer to [[:en:Festivals|Festivals]] held during the [[:en:Winter|Winter]] season. These events typically occur over the three-month period from [[:en:Lidong|Rittō]] (the beginning of winter) to [[:en:Risshun|Risshun]] (the beginning of spring), corresponding to [[:en:November|November]], [[:en:December|December]], and [[:en:January|January]].

In ancient times, the '''10th month of the old calendar''' ({{ill|Kannazuki|ja|神無月|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannazuki|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}) was considered an '''unlucky month''' ({{ill|Imizuki|ja|忌月|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imizuki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}).<ref>Although the unlucky months are commonly introduced as January, May, and September in the modern calendar, in the old calendar, they were roughly one month later, making the 10th month of the old calendar an unlucky month.</ref> Thus, the '''harvest festival''' was originally held in the '''ddth month of the old calendar''', after the month changed and winter began. However, as the '''taboos''' surrounding the 10th month faded, the '''Autumn Festival, held immediately after the harvest''', became established.
[[:en:Shinobu Orikuchi|Orikuchi Shinobu]] viewed this period, spanning from autumn to the following spring, as a time of '''spiritual renewal''' ({{ill|Chinkon|ja|鎮魂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinkon|1=chinkon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10297557}}), during which all souls, including human ones, were refreshed. He theorized that the '''Autumn Festival, Winter Festival, and Spring Festival''' were originally '''one continuous ritual cycle''', dealing with '''harvest, soul renewal, and prayers for the coming year'''. Over time, he suggested, the '''Autumn and Spring Festivals split off''', leaving the remainder as the '''Winter Festival'''.

In the [[:en:Department of Divinities|Department of Divinities]] (Department of Shinto Affairs) of the {{ill|Imperial Court of Japan|ja|朝廷 (日本)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Court of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Imperial Court of Japan|WD=Q30253177}}, the {{ill|Four Seasonal Festivals|ja|四時祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Four Seasonal Festivals|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} designated the '''Ainame Festival''' ({{ill|Ainame Festival|ja|相嘗祭|lt=Ainame-sai|Ainame Festival=Ainame Festival|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ainame Festival|qq=}}), '''Chinkon Festival''' ({{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinkon-sai|qq=}}), and '''Niiname Festival''' ([[:en:Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niiname-sai]]) in the '''ddth month of the old calendar''' as '''Winter Festivals'''. In the [[:en:Department of Divinities|Department of Divinities]] (Department of Shinto Affairs) of the {{ill|Imperial Court of Japan|ja|朝廷 (日本)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Court of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Imperial Court of Japan|WD=Q30253177}}, the {{ill|Four Seasonal Festivals|ja|四時祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Four Seasonal Festivals|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} designated the '''Ainame Festival''' ({{ill|Ainame Festival|ja|相嘗祭|lt=Ainame-sai|Ainame Festival=Ainame Festival|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ainame Festival|qq=}}), '''Chinkon Festival''' ({{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinkon-sai|qq=}}), and '''Niiname Festival''' ([[:en:Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niiname-sai]]) in the '''ddth month of the old calendar''' as '''Winter Festivals'''. Additionally, although the '''Tsukinami Festival''' ({{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭|1=Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukinami-sai|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|qq=}}), '''Chinka (Fire-Calming Ritual)''' ({{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|ja|鎮火|Fire-Pacification Festival=Fire-Pacification Festival|lt=Chinka|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fire-Pacification Festival|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 消火}}), and '''Dōkyō Festival''' ({{ill|Dōkyō|ja|道饗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dōkyō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) were not inherently winter-specific, they were conducted as part of the Winter Festival period.
Today, many '''Winter Festivals''' retain elements of the '''Chinkon Festival (Ritual for Soul-Pacification)''', and practices such as '''Yutate (Hot Water Purification Rituals)''' ({{ill|Yutate|ja|湯立|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yutate|1=yudate|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563775}}) and '''Kagura (Sacred Dance and Music)''' ([[:en:Kagura|Kagura]]), which originated from '''Shimotsuki Kagura''' ({{ill|Shimotsuki Kagura|ja|霜月神楽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimotsuki Kagura|qq=}}), are often performed.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:冬祭り]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q121920335]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:21, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q121920335]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:12, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Referencesの明記|date=2017th year 2月8日 (水) dd:32 (UTC)}}
Winter festivals or '''Winter Matsuri''' (冬祭り) refer to [[:en:Festivals|Festivals]] held during the [[:en:Winter|Winter]] season. These events typically occur over the three-month period from [[:en:Lidong|Rittō]] (the beginning of winter) to [[:en:Risshun|Risshun]] (the beginning of spring), corresponding to [[:en:November|November]], [[:en:December|December]], and [[:en:January|January]].
== Overview == The '''Winter Festival''' incorporates both the '''harvest festival elements''' of the {{ill|Autumn festival|ja|秋祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Autumn festival|1=Autumn Matsuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11595783}} and the '''prayer for the year's blessings elements''' of the {{ill|Spring Matsuri|lt=Spring Matsuri|lt=Spring festival|ja|春祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spring festival|qq=}}, making its exact positioning somewhat unclear. In ancient times, the '''10th month of the old calendar''' ({{ill|Kannazuki|ja|神無月|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannazuki|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}) was considered an '''unlucky month''' ({{ill|Imizuki|ja|忌月|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imizuki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}).<ref>Although the unlucky months are commonly introduced as January, May, and September in the modern calendar, in the old calendar, they were roughly one month later, making the 10th month of the old calendar an unlucky month.</ref> Thus, the '''harvest festival''' was originally held in the '''ddth month of the old calendar''', after the month changed and winter began. However, as the '''taboos''' surrounding the 10th month faded, the '''Autumn Festival, held immediately after the harvest''', became established.
[[:en:Shinobu Orikuchi|Orikuchi Shinobu]] viewed this period, spanning from autumn to the following spring, as a time of '''spiritual renewal''' ({{ill|Chinkon|ja|鎮魂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinkon|1=chinkon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10297557}}), during which all souls, including human ones, were refreshed. He theorized that the '''Autumn Festival, Winter Festival, and Spring Festival''' were originally '''one continuous ritual cycle''', dealing with '''harvest, soul renewal, and prayers for the coming year'''. Over time, he suggested, the '''Autumn and Spring Festivals split off''', leaving the remainder as the '''Winter Festival'''.
Similarly, [[:en:Kunio Yanagita|Yanagita Kunio]] pointed out that the '''ddth month of the old calendar''', which includes the '''Winter Solstice''', was a turning point toward spring. He argued that Japanese festivals traditionally placed great importance on this month.
In the [[:en:Department of Divinities|Department of Divinities]] (Department of Shinto Affairs) of the {{ill|Imperial Court of Japan|ja|朝廷 (日本)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Court of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Imperial Court of Japan|WD=Q30253177}}, the {{ill|Four Seasonal Festivals|ja|四時祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Four Seasonal Festivals|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} designated the '''Ainame Festival''' ({{ill|Ainame Festival|ja|相嘗祭|lt=Ainame-sai|Ainame Festival=Ainame Festival|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ainame Festival|qq=}}), '''Chinkon Festival''' ({{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinkon-sai|qq=}}), and '''Niiname Festival''' ([[:en:Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niiname-sai]]) in the '''ddth month of the old calendar''' as '''Winter Festivals'''. Additionally, although the '''Tsukinami Festival''' ({{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭|1=Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukinami-sai|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|qq=}}), '''Chinka (Fire-Calming Ritual)''' ({{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|ja|鎮火|Fire-Pacification Festival=Fire-Pacification Festival|lt=Chinka|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fire-Pacification Festival|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 消火}}), and '''Dōkyō Festival''' ({{ill|Dōkyō|ja|道饗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dōkyō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) were not inherently winter-specific, they were conducted as part of the Winter Festival period.
Today, many '''Winter Festivals''' retain elements of the '''Chinkon Festival (Ritual for Soul-Pacification)''', and practices such as '''Yutate (Hot Water Purification Rituals)''' ({{ill|Yutate|ja|湯立|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yutate|1=yudate|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563775}}) and '''Kagura (Sacred Dance and Music)''' ([[:en:Kagura|Kagura]]), which originated from '''Shimotsuki Kagura''' ({{ill|Shimotsuki Kagura|ja|霜月神楽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimotsuki Kagura|qq=}}), are often performed.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:冬祭り]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q121920335]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:21, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q121920335]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:12, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Referencesの明記|date=2017th year 2月8日 (水) dd:32 (UTC)}}
Winter festivals or '''Winter Matsuri''' (冬祭り) refer to [[:en:Festivals|Festivals]] held during the [[:en:Winter|Winter]] season. These events typically occur over the three-month period from [[:en:Lidong|Rittō]] (the beginning of winter) to [[:en:Risshun|Risshun]] (the beginning of spring), corresponding to [[:en:November|November]], [[:en:December|December]], and [[:en:January|January]].
== Overview == The '''Winter Festival''' incorporates both the '''harvest festival elements''' of the {{ill|Autumn festival|ja|秋祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Autumn festival|1=Autumn Matsuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11595783}} and the '''prayer for the year's blessings elements''' of the {{ill|Spring Matsuri|lt=Spring Matsuri|lt=Spring festival|ja|春祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spring festival|qq=}}, making its exact positioning somewhat unclear. In ancient times, the '''10th month of the old calendar''' ({{ill|Kannazuki|ja|神無月|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannazuki|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}) was considered an '''unlucky month''' ({{ill|Imizuki|ja|忌月|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imizuki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}).<ref>Although the unlucky months are commonly introduced as January, May, and September in the modern calendar, in the old calendar, they were roughly one month later, making the 10th month of the old calendar an unlucky month.</ref> Thus, the '''harvest festival''' was originally held in the '''ddth month of the old calendar''', after the month changed and winter began. However, as the '''taboos''' surrounding the 10th month faded, the '''Autumn Festival, held immediately after the harvest''', became established.
[[:en:Shinobu Orikuchi|Orikuchi Shinobu]] viewed this period, spanning from autumn to the following spring, as a time of '''spiritual renewal''' ({{ill|Chinkon|ja|鎮魂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinkon|1=chinkon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10297557}}), during which all souls, including human ones, were refreshed. He theorized that the '''Autumn Festival, Winter Festival, and Spring Festival''' were originally '''one continuous ritual cycle''', dealing with '''harvest, soul renewal, and prayers for the coming year'''. Over time, he suggested, the '''Autumn and Spring Festivals split off''', leaving the remainder as the '''Winter Festival'''.
Similarly, [[:en:Kunio Yanagita|Yanagita Kunio]] pointed out that the '''ddth month of the old calendar''', which includes the '''Winter Solstice''', was a turning point toward spring. He argued that Japanese festivals traditionally placed great importance on this month.
In the [[:en:Department of Divinities|Department of Divinities]] (Department of Shinto Affairs) of the {{ill|Imperial Court of Japan|ja|朝廷 (日本)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Court of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Imperial Court of Japan|WD=Q30253177}}, the {{ill|Four Seasonal Festivals|ja|四時祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Four Seasonal Festivals|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} designated the '''Ainame Festival''' ({{ill|Ainame Festival|ja|相嘗祭|lt=Ainame-sai|Ainame Festival=Ainame Festival|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ainame Festival|qq=}}), '''Chinkon Festival''' ({{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinkon-sai|qq=}}), and '''Niiname Festival''' ([[:en:Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niiname-sai]]) in the '''ddth month of the old calendar''' as '''Winter Festivals'''. Additionally, although the '''Tsukinami Festival''' ({{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭|1=Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukinami-sai|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|qq=}}), '''Chinka (Fire-Calming Ritual)''' ({{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|ja|鎮火|Fire-Pacification Festival=Fire-Pacification Festival|lt=Chinka|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fire-Pacification Festival|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 消火}}), and '''Dōkyō Festival''' ({{ill|Dōkyō|ja|道饗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dōkyō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) were not inherently winter-specific, they were conducted as part of the Winter Festival period.
Today, many '''Winter Festivals''' retain elements of the '''Chinkon Festival (Ritual for Soul-Pacification)''', and practices such as '''Yutate (Hot Water Purification Rituals)''' ({{ill|Yutate|ja|湯立|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yutate|1=yudate|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563775}}) and '''Kagura (Sacred Dance and Music)''' ([[:en:Kagura|Kagura]]), which originated from '''Shimotsuki Kagura''' ({{ill|Shimotsuki Kagura|ja|霜月神楽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimotsuki Kagura|qq=}}), are often performed.
== Notes == {{reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Spring Matsuri|lt=Spring Matsuri|lt=Spring Festivals|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spring Festivals|qq=}} * [[Summer Matsuri|Summer festivals]] * {{ill|Autumn Matsuri|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Autumn Festivals|qq=}} * [[:en:Winter_break|Winter break]] *
* [[:en:Yule|Yule]] – Ancient European winter solstice celebrations
* {{ill|Tanabata|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanabata|qq=}} * {{ill|Community building|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Community building|qq=}} *
*{{Event-stub}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:冬祭り]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q121920335]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:21, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q121920335]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:12, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Referencesの明記|date=2017th year 2月8日 (水) dd:32 (UTC)}}
Winter festivals or '''Winter Matsuri''' (冬祭り) refer to [[:en:Festivals|Festivals]] held during the [[:en:Winter|Winter]] season. These events typically occur over the three-month period from [[:en:Lidong|Rittō]] (the beginning of winter) to [[:en:Risshun|Risshun]] (the beginning of spring), corresponding to [[:en:November|November]], [[:en:December|December]], and [[:en:January|January]].
== Overview == The '''Winter Festival''' incorporates both the '''harvest festival elements''' of the {{ill|Autumn festival|ja|秋祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Autumn festival|1=Autumn Matsuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11595783}} and the '''prayer for the year's blessings elements''' of the {{ill|Spring Matsuri|lt=Spring Matsuri|lt=Spring festival|ja|春祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spring festival|qq=}}, making its exact positioning somewhat unclear. In ancient times, the '''10th month of the old calendar''' ({{ill|Kannazuki|ja|神無月|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannazuki|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}) was considered an '''unlucky month''' ({{ill|Imizuki|ja|忌月|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imizuki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}).<ref>Although the unlucky months are commonly introduced as January, May, and September in the modern calendar, in the old calendar, they were roughly one month later, making the 10th month of the old calendar an unlucky month.</ref> Thus, the '''harvest festival''' was originally held in the '''ddth month of the old calendar''', after the month changed and winter began. However, as the '''taboos''' surrounding the 10th month faded, the '''Autumn Festival, held immediately after the harvest''', became established.
[[:en:Shinobu Orikuchi|Orikuchi Shinobu]] viewed this period, spanning from autumn to the following spring, as a time of '''spiritual renewal''' ({{ill|Chinkon|ja|鎮魂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinkon|1=chinkon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10297557}}), during which all souls, including human ones, were refreshed. He theorized that the '''Autumn Festival, Winter Festival, and Spring Festival''' were originally '''one continuous ritual cycle''', dealing with '''harvest, soul renewal, and prayers for the coming year'''. Over time, he suggested, the '''Autumn and Spring Festivals split off''', leaving the remainder as the '''Winter Festival'''.
Similarly, [[:en:Kunio Yanagita|Yanagita Kunio]] pointed out that the '''ddth month of the old calendar''', which includes the '''Winter Solstice''', was a turning point toward spring. He argued that Japanese festivals traditionally placed great importance on this month.
In the [[:en:Department of Divinities|Department of Divinities]] (Department of Shinto Affairs) of the {{ill|Imperial Court of Japan|ja|朝廷 (日本)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Court of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Imperial Court of Japan|WD=Q30253177}}, the {{ill|Four Seasonal Festivals|ja|四時祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Four Seasonal Festivals|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} designated the '''Ainame Festival''' ({{ill|Ainame Festival|ja|相嘗祭|lt=Ainame-sai|Ainame Festival=Ainame Festival|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ainame Festival|qq=}}), '''Chinkon Festival''' ({{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinkon-sai|qq=}}), and '''Niiname Festival''' ([[:en:Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niiname-sai]]) in the '''ddth month of the old calendar''' as '''Winter Festivals'''. Additionally, although the '''Tsukinami Festival''' ({{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭|1=Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukinami-sai|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|qq=}}), '''Chinka (Fire-Calming Ritual)''' ({{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|ja|鎮火|Fire-Pacification Festival=Fire-Pacification Festival|lt=Chinka|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fire-Pacification Festival|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 消火}}), and '''Dōkyō Festival''' ({{ill|Dōkyō|ja|道饗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dōkyō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) were not inherently winter-specific, they were conducted as part of the Winter Festival period.
Today, many '''Winter Festivals''' retain elements of the '''Chinkon Festival (Ritual for Soul-Pacification)''', and practices such as '''Yutate (Hot Water Purification Rituals)''' ({{ill|Yutate|ja|湯立|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yutate|1=yudate|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563775}}) and '''Kagura (Sacred Dance and Music)''' ([[:en:Kagura|Kagura]]), which originated from '''Shimotsuki Kagura''' ({{ill|Shimotsuki Kagura|ja|霜月神楽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimotsuki Kagura|qq=}}), are often performed.
== Notes == {{reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Spring Matsuri|lt=Spring Matsuri|lt=Spring Festivals|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spring Festivals|qq=}} * [[Summer Matsuri|Summer festivals]] * {{ill|Autumn Matsuri|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Autumn Festivals|qq=}} * [[:en:Winter_break|Winter break]] *
* [[:en:Yule|Yule]] – Ancient European winter solstice celebrations
* {{ill|Tanabata|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanabata|qq=}} * {{ill|Community building|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Community building|qq=}} *
*{{Event-stub}}

Winter festivals or '''Winter Matsuri''' (冬祭り) refer to [[:en:Festivals|Festivals]] held during the [[:en:Winter|Winter]] season. These events typically occur over the three-month period from [[:en:Lidong|Rittō]] (the beginning of winter) to [[:en:Risshun|Risshun]] (the beginning of spring), corresponding to [[:en:November|November]], [[:en:December|December]], and [[:en:January|January]].
== Overview == The '''Winter Festival''' incorporates both the '''harvest festival elements''' of the {{ill|Autumn festival|ja|秋祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Autumn festival|1=Autumn Matsuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11595783}} and the '''prayer for the year's blessings elements''' of the {{ill|Spring Matsuri|lt=Spring Matsuri|lt=Spring festival|ja|春祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spring festival|qq=}}, making its exact positioning somewhat unclear. In ancient times, the '''10th month of the old calendar''' ({{ill|Kannazuki|ja|神無月|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannazuki|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}) was considered an '''unlucky month''' ({{ill|Imizuki|ja|忌月|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imizuki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}).<ref>Although the unlucky months are commonly introduced as January, May, and September in the modern calendar, in the old calendar, they were roughly one month later, making the 10th month of the old calendar an unlucky month.</ref> Thus, the '''harvest festival''' was originally held in the '''ddth month of the old calendar''', after the month changed and winter began. However, as the '''taboos''' surrounding the 10th month faded, the '''Autumn Festival, held immediately after the harvest''', became established.
[[:en:Shinobu Orikuchi|Orikuchi Shinobu]] viewed this period, spanning from autumn to the following spring, as a time of '''spiritual renewal''' ({{ill|Chinkon|ja|鎮魂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinkon|1=chinkon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10297557}}), during which all souls, including human ones, were refreshed. He theorized that the '''Autumn Festival, Winter Festival, and Spring Festival''' were originally '''one continuous ritual cycle''', dealing with '''harvest, soul renewal, and prayers for the coming year'''. Over time, he suggested, the '''Autumn and Spring Festivals split off''', leaving the remainder as the '''Winter Festival'''.
Similarly, [[:en:Kunio Yanagita|Yanagita Kunio]] pointed out that the '''ddth month of the old calendar''', which includes the '''Winter Solstice''', was a turning point toward spring. He argued that Japanese festivals traditionally placed great importance on this month.
In the [[:en:Department of Divinities|Department of Divinities]] (Department of Shinto Affairs) of the {{ill|Imperial Court of Japan|ja|朝廷 (日本)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Court of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Imperial Court of Japan|WD=Q30253177}}, the {{ill|Four Seasonal Festivals|ja|四時祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Four Seasonal Festivals|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} designated the '''Ainame Festival''' ({{ill|Ainame Festival|ja|相嘗祭|lt=Ainame-sai|Ainame Festival=Ainame Festival|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ainame Festival|qq=}}), '''Chinkon Festival''' ({{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinkon-sai|qq=}}), and '''Niiname Festival''' ([[:en:Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niiname-sai]]) in the '''ddth month of the old calendar''' as '''Winter Festivals'''. Additionally, although the '''Tsukinami Festival''' ({{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭|1=Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukinami-sai|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|qq=}}), '''Chinka (Fire-Calming Ritual)''' ({{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|ja|鎮火|Fire-Pacification Festival=Fire-Pacification Festival|lt=Chinka|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fire-Pacification Festival|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 消火}}), and '''Dōkyō Festival''' ({{ill|Dōkyō|ja|道饗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dōkyō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) were not inherently winter-specific, they were conducted as part of the Winter Festival period.
Today, many '''Winter Festivals''' retain elements of the '''Chinkon Festival (Ritual for Soul-Pacification)''', and practices such as '''Yutate (Hot Water Purification Rituals)''' ({{ill|Yutate|ja|湯立|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yutate|1=yudate|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563775}}) and '''Kagura (Sacred Dance and Music)''' ([[:en:Kagura|Kagura]]), which originated from '''Shimotsuki Kagura''' ({{ill|Shimotsuki Kagura|ja|霜月神楽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimotsuki Kagura|qq=}}), are often performed.

In the [[:en:Department of Divinities|Department of Divinities]] (Department of Shinto Affairs) of the {{ill|Imperial Court of Japan|ja|朝廷 (日本)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Court of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Imperial Court of Japan|WD=Q30253177}}, the {{ill|Four Seasonal Festivals|ja|四時祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Four Seasonal Festivals|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} designated the '''Ainame Festival''' ({{ill|Ainame Festival|ja|相嘗祭|lt=Ainame-sai|Ainame Festival=Ainame Festival|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ainame Festival|qq=}}), '''Chinkon Festival''' ({{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinkon-sai|qq=}}), and '''Niiname Festival''' ([[:en:Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niiname-sai]]) in the '''ddth month of the old calendar''' as '''Winter Festivals'''. Additionally, although the '''Tsukinami Festival''' ({{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭|1=Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukinami-sai|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|qq=}}), '''Chinka (Fire-Calming Ritual)''' ({{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|ja|鎮火|Fire-Pacification Festival=Fire-Pacification Festival|lt=Chinka|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fire-Pacification Festival|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 消火}}), and '''Dōkyō Festival''' ({{ill|Dōkyō|ja|道饗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dōkyō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) were not inherently winter-specific, they were conducted as part of the Winter Festival period.
Today, many '''Winter Festivals''' retain elements of the '''Chinkon Festival (Ritual for Soul-Pacification)''', and practices such as '''Yutate (Hot Water Purification Rituals)''' ({{ill|Yutate|ja|湯立|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yutate|1=yudate|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563775}}) and '''Kagura (Sacred Dance and Music)''' ([[:en:Kagura|Kagura]]), which originated from '''Shimotsuki Kagura''' ({{ill|Shimotsuki Kagura|ja|霜月神楽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimotsuki Kagura|qq=}}), are often performed.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:冬祭り]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:冬祭り]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:冬祭り]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q121920335]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:21, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q121920335]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:12, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Referencesの明記|date=2017th year 2月8日 (水) dd:32 (UTC)}}
Winter festivals or '''Winter Matsuri''' (冬祭り) refer to [[:en:Festivals|Festivals]] held during the [[:en:Winter|Winter]] season. These events typically occur over the three-month period from [[:en:Lidong|Rittō]] (the beginning of winter) to [[:en:Risshun|Risshun]] (the beginning of spring), corresponding to [[:en:November|November]], [[:en:December|December]], and [[:en:January|January]].
== Overview == The '''Winter Festival''' incorporates both the '''harvest festival elements''' of the {{ill|Autumn festival|ja|秋祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Autumn festival|1=Autumn Matsuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11595783}} and the '''prayer for the year's blessings elements''' of the {{ill|Spring Matsuri|lt=Spring Matsuri|lt=Spring festival|ja|春祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spring festival|qq=}}, making its exact positioning somewhat unclear. In ancient times, the '''10th month of the old calendar''' ({{ill|Kannazuki|ja|神無月|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannazuki|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}) was considered an '''unlucky month''' ({{ill|Imizuki|ja|忌月|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imizuki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}).<ref>Although the unlucky months are commonly introduced as January, May, and September in the modern calendar, in the old calendar, they were roughly one month later, making the 10th month of the old calendar an unlucky month.</ref> Thus, the '''harvest festival''' was originally held in the '''ddth month of the old calendar''', after the month changed and winter began. However, as the '''taboos''' surrounding the 10th month faded, the '''Autumn Festival, held immediately after the harvest''', became established.
[[:en:Shinobu Orikuchi|Orikuchi Shinobu]] viewed this period, spanning from autumn to the following spring, as a time of '''spiritual renewal''' ({{ill|Chinkon|ja|鎮魂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinkon|1=chinkon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10297557}}), during which all souls, including human ones, were refreshed. He theorized that the '''Autumn Festival, Winter Festival, and Spring Festival''' were originally '''one continuous ritual cycle''', dealing with '''harvest, soul renewal, and prayers for the coming year'''. Over time, he suggested, the '''Autumn and Spring Festivals split off''', leaving the remainder as the '''Winter Festival'''.
Similarly, [[:en:Kunio Yanagita|Yanagita Kunio]] pointed out that the '''ddth month of the old calendar''', which includes the '''Winter Solstice''', was a turning point toward spring. He argued that Japanese festivals traditionally placed great importance on this month.
In the [[:en:Department of Divinities|Department of Divinities]] (Department of Shinto Affairs) of the {{ill|Imperial Court of Japan|ja|朝廷 (日本)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Court of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Imperial Court of Japan|WD=Q30253177}}, the {{ill|Four Seasonal Festivals|ja|四時祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Four Seasonal Festivals|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} designated the '''Ainame Festival''' ({{ill|Ainame Festival|ja|相嘗祭|lt=Ainame-sai|Ainame Festival=Ainame Festival|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ainame Festival|qq=}}), '''Chinkon Festival''' ({{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinkon-sai|qq=}}), and '''Niiname Festival''' ([[:en:Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niiname-sai]]) in the '''ddth month of the old calendar''' as '''Winter Festivals'''. Additionally, although the '''Tsukinami Festival''' ({{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭|1=Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukinami-sai|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|qq=}}), '''Chinka (Fire-Calming Ritual)''' ({{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|ja|鎮火|Fire-Pacification Festival=Fire-Pacification Festival|lt=Chinka|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fire-Pacification Festival|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 消火}}), and '''Dōkyō Festival''' ({{ill|Dōkyō|ja|道饗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dōkyō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) were not inherently winter-specific, they were conducted as part of the Winter Festival period.
Today, many '''Winter Festivals''' retain elements of the '''Chinkon Festival (Ritual for Soul-Pacification)''', and practices such as '''Yutate (Hot Water Purification Rituals)''' ({{ill|Yutate|ja|湯立|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yutate|1=yudate|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563775}}) and '''Kagura (Sacred Dance and Music)''' ([[:en:Kagura|Kagura]]), which originated from '''Shimotsuki Kagura''' ({{ill|Shimotsuki Kagura|ja|霜月神楽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimotsuki Kagura|qq=}}), are often performed.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:冬祭り]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:冬祭り]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:冬祭り]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q121920335]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:21, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q121920335]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:12, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Referencesの明記|date=2017th year 2月8日 (水) dd:32 (UTC)}}
Winter festivals or '''Winter Matsuri''' (冬祭り) refer to [[:en:Festivals|Festivals]] held during the [[:en:Winter|Winter]] season. These events typically occur over the three-month period from [[:en:Lidong|Rittō]] (the beginning of winter) to [[:en:Risshun|Risshun]] (the beginning of spring), corresponding to [[:en:November|November]], [[:en:December|December]], and [[:en:January|January]].
== Overview == The '''Winter Festival''' incorporates both the '''harvest festival elements''' of the {{ill|Autumn festival|ja|秋祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Autumn festival|1=Autumn Matsuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11595783}} and the '''prayer for the year's blessings elements''' of the {{ill|Spring Matsuri|lt=Spring Matsuri|lt=Spring festival|ja|春祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spring festival|qq=}}, making its exact positioning somewhat unclear. In ancient times, the '''10th month of the old calendar''' ({{ill|Kannazuki|ja|神無月|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannazuki|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}) was considered an '''unlucky month''' ({{ill|Imizuki|ja|忌月|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imizuki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}).<ref>Although the unlucky months are commonly introduced as January, May, and September in the modern calendar, in the old calendar, they were roughly one month later, making the 10th month of the old calendar an unlucky month.</ref> Thus, the '''harvest festival''' was originally held in the '''ddth month of the old calendar''', after the month changed and winter began. However, as the '''taboos''' surrounding the 10th month faded, the '''Autumn Festival, held immediately after the harvest''', became established.
[[:en:Shinobu Orikuchi|Orikuchi Shinobu]] viewed this period, spanning from autumn to the following spring, as a time of '''spiritual renewal''' ({{ill|Chinkon|ja|鎮魂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinkon|1=chinkon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10297557}}), during which all souls, including human ones, were refreshed. He theorized that the '''Autumn Festival, Winter Festival, and Spring Festival''' were originally '''one continuous ritual cycle''', dealing with '''harvest, soul renewal, and prayers for the coming year'''. Over time, he suggested, the '''Autumn and Spring Festivals split off''', leaving the remainder as the '''Winter Festival'''.
Similarly, [[:en:Kunio Yanagita|Yanagita Kunio]] pointed out that the '''ddth month of the old calendar''', which includes the '''Winter Solstice''', was a turning point toward spring. He argued that Japanese festivals traditionally placed great importance on this month.
In the [[:en:Department of Divinities|Department of Divinities]] (Department of Shinto Affairs) of the {{ill|Imperial Court of Japan|ja|朝廷 (日本)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Court of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Imperial Court of Japan|WD=Q30253177}}, the {{ill|Four Seasonal Festivals|ja|四時祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Four Seasonal Festivals|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} designated the '''Ainame Festival''' ({{ill|Ainame Festival|ja|相嘗祭|lt=Ainame-sai|Ainame Festival=Ainame Festival|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ainame Festival|qq=}}), '''Chinkon Festival''' ({{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinkon-sai|qq=}}), and '''Niiname Festival''' ([[:en:Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niiname-sai]]) in the '''ddth month of the old calendar''' as '''Winter Festivals'''. Additionally, although the '''Tsukinami Festival''' ({{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭|1=Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukinami-sai|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|qq=}}), '''Chinka (Fire-Calming Ritual)''' ({{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|ja|鎮火|Fire-Pacification Festival=Fire-Pacification Festival|lt=Chinka|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fire-Pacification Festival|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 消火}}), and '''Dōkyō Festival''' ({{ill|Dōkyō|ja|道饗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dōkyō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) were not inherently winter-specific, they were conducted as part of the Winter Festival period.
Today, many '''Winter Festivals''' retain elements of the '''Chinkon Festival (Ritual for Soul-Pacification)''', and practices such as '''Yutate (Hot Water Purification Rituals)''' ({{ill|Yutate|ja|湯立|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yutate|1=yudate|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563775}}) and '''Kagura (Sacred Dance and Music)''' ([[:en:Kagura|Kagura]]), which originated from '''Shimotsuki Kagura''' ({{ill|Shimotsuki Kagura|ja|霜月神楽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimotsuki Kagura|qq=}}), are often performed.
== Notes == {{reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Spring Matsuri|lt=Spring Matsuri|lt=Spring Festivals|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spring Festivals|qq=}} * [[Summer Matsuri|Summer festivals]] * {{ill|Autumn Matsuri|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Autumn Festivals|qq=}} * [[:en:Winter_break|Winter break]] *
* [[:en:Yule|Yule]] – Ancient European winter solstice celebrations
* {{ill|Tanabata|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanabata|qq=}} * {{ill|Community building|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Community building|qq=}} *

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:冬祭り]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:冬祭り]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:冬祭り]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q121920335]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:21, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q121920335]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:12, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Referencesの明記|date=2017th year 2月8日 (水) dd:32 (UTC)}}
Winter festivals or '''Winter Matsuri''' (冬祭り) refer to [[:en:Festivals|Festivals]] held during the {{ill|Winter|en|Winter|WD=Q1311|lt=Winter}} season. These events typically occur over the three-month period from {{ill|Lidong|en|Lidong|WD=Q1191018|lt=Rittō}} (the beginning of winter) to [[:en:Risshun|Risshun]] (the beginning of spring), corresponding to {{ill|November|en|November|WD=Q125|lt=November}}, {{ill|December|en|December|WD=Q126|lt=December}}, and {{ill|January|en|January|WD=Q108|lt=January}}.
== Overview == The '''Winter Festival''' incorporates both the '''harvest festival elements''' of the {{ill|Autumn festival|ja|秋祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Autumn festival|1=Autumn Matsuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11595783}} and the '''prayer for the year's blessings elements''' of the {{ill|Spring Matsuri|lt=Spring Matsuri|lt=Spring festival|ja|春祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spring festival|qq=}}, making its exact positioning somewhat unclear. In ancient times, the '''10th month of the old calendar''' ({{ill|Kannazuki|ja|神無月|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannazuki|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}) was considered an '''unlucky month''' ({{ill|Imizuki|ja|忌月|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imizuki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}).<ref>Although the unlucky months are commonly introduced as January, May, and September in the modern calendar, in the old calendar, they were roughly one month later, making the 10th month of the old calendar an unlucky month.</ref> Thus, the '''harvest festival''' was originally held in the '''ddth month of the old calendar''', after the month changed and winter began. However, as the '''taboos''' surrounding the 10th month faded, the '''Autumn Festival, held immediately after the harvest''', became established.
{{ill|Shinobu Orikuchi|en|Shinobu Orikuchi|WD=Q3482286|lt=Orikuchi Shinobu}} viewed this period, spanning from autumn to the following spring, as a time of '''spiritual renewal''' ({{ill|Chinkon|ja|鎮魂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinkon|1=chinkon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10297557}}), during which all souls, including human ones, were refreshed. He theorized that the '''Autumn Festival, Winter Festival, and Spring Festival''' were originally '''one continuous ritual cycle''', dealing with '''harvest, soul renewal, and prayers for the coming year'''. Over time, he suggested, the '''Autumn and Spring Festivals split off''', leaving the remainder as the '''Winter Festival'''.
Similarly, {{ill|Kunio Yanagita|en|Kunio Yanagita|WD=Q971687|lt=Yanagita Kunio}} pointed out that the '''ddth month of the old calendar''', which includes the '''Winter Solstice''', was a turning point toward spring. He argued that Japanese festivals traditionally placed great importance on this month.
In the {{ill|Department of Divinities|en|Department of Divinities|WD=Q2264720|lt=Department of Divinities}} (Department of Shinto Affairs) of the {{ill|Imperial Court of Japan|ja|朝廷 (日本)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Court of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Imperial Court of Japan|WD=Q30253177}}, the {{ill|Four Seasonal Festivals|ja|四時祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Four Seasonal Festivals|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} designated the '''Ainame Festival''' ({{ill|Ainame Festival|ja|相嘗祭|lt=Ainame-sai|Ainame Festival=Ainame Festival|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ainame Festival|qq=}}), '''Chinkon Festival''' ({{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinkon-sai|qq=}}), and '''Niiname Festival''' ({{ill|Niiname-no-Matsuri|en|Niiname-no-Matsuri|WD=Q11501518|lt=Niiname-sai}}) in the '''ddth month of the old calendar''' as '''Winter Festivals'''. Additionally, although the '''Tsukinami Festival''' ({{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭|1=Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukinami-sai|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|qq=}}), '''Chinka (Fire-Calming Ritual)''' ({{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|ja|鎮火|Fire-Pacification Festival=Fire-Pacification Festival|lt=Chinka|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fire-Pacification Festival|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 消火}}), and '''Dōkyō Festival''' ({{ill|Dōkyō|ja|道饗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dōkyō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) were not inherently winter-specific, they were conducted as part of the Winter Festival period.
Today, many '''Winter Festivals''' retain elements of the '''Chinkon Festival (Ritual for Soul-Pacification)''', and practices such as '''Yutate (Hot Water Purification Rituals)''' ({{ill|Yutate|ja|湯立|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yutate|1=yudate|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563775}}) and '''Kagura (Sacred Dance and Music)''' ({{ill|Kagura|en|Kagura|WD=Q1348260|lt=Kagura}}), which originated from '''Shimotsuki Kagura''' ({{ill|Shimotsuki Kagura|ja|霜月神楽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimotsuki Kagura|qq=}}), are often performed.
== Notes == {{reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Spring Matsuri|lt=Spring Matsuri|lt=Spring Festivals|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spring Festivals|qq=}} * [[Summer Matsuri|Summer festivals]] * {{ill|Autumn Matsuri|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Autumn Festivals|qq=}} * {{ill|Winter_break|en|Winter_break|WD=Q110726465|lt=Winter break}} *
* {{ill|Yule|en|Yule|WD=Q211945|lt=Yule}} – Ancient European winter solstice celebrations
* {{ill|Tanabata|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanabata|qq=}} * {{ill|Community building|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Community building|qq=}} *
*{{Event-stub}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:冬祭り]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:冬祭り]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:冬祭り]]
{{Referencesの明記|date=2017th year 2月8日 (水) dd:32 (UTC)}}
Winter festivals or '''Winter Matsuri''' (冬祭り) refer to [[:en:Festivals|Festivals]] held during the {{ill|Winter|en|Winter|WD=Q1311|lt=Winter}} season. These events typically occur over the three-month period from {{ill|Lidong|en|Lidong|WD=Q1191018|lt=Rittō}} (the beginning of winter) to [[:en:Risshun|Risshun]] (the beginning of spring), corresponding to {{ill|November|en|November|WD=Q125|lt=November}}, {{ill|December|en|December|WD=Q126|lt=December}}, and {{ill|January|en|January|WD=Q108|lt=January}}.
== Overview == The '''Winter Festival''' incorporates both the '''harvest festival elements''' of the {{ill|Autumn festival|ja|秋祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Autumn festival|1=Autumn Matsuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11595783}} and the '''prayer for the year's blessings elements''' of the {{ill|Spring Matsuri|lt=Spring Matsuri|lt=Spring festival|ja|春祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spring festival|qq=}}, making its exact positioning somewhat unclear. In ancient times, the '''10th month of the old calendar''' ({{ill|Kannazuki|ja|神無月|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannazuki|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}) was considered an '''unlucky month''' ({{ill|Imizuki|ja|忌月|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imizuki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}).<ref>Although the unlucky months are commonly introduced as January, May, and September in the modern calendar, in the old calendar, they were roughly one month later, making the 10th month of the old calendar an unlucky month.</ref> Thus, the '''harvest festival''' was originally held in the '''ddth month of the old calendar''', after the month changed and winter began. However, as the '''taboos''' surrounding the 10th month faded, the '''Autumn Festival, held immediately after the harvest''', became established.
{{ill|Shinobu Orikuchi|en|Shinobu Orikuchi|WD=Q3482286|lt=Orikuchi Shinobu}} viewed this period, spanning from autumn to the following spring, as a time of '''spiritual renewal''' ({{ill|Chinkon|ja|鎮魂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinkon|1=chinkon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10297557}}), during which all souls, including human ones, were refreshed. He theorized that the '''Autumn Festival, Winter Festival, and Spring Festival''' were originally '''one continuous ritual cycle''', dealing with '''harvest, soul renewal, and prayers for the coming year'''. Over time, he suggested, the '''Autumn and Spring Festivals split off''', leaving the remainder as the '''Winter Festival'''.
Similarly, {{ill|Kunio Yanagita|en|Kunio Yanagita|WD=Q971687|lt=Yanagita Kunio}} pointed out that the '''ddth month of the old calendar''', which includes the '''Winter Solstice''', was a turning point toward spring. He argued that Japanese festivals traditionally placed great importance on this month.
In the {{ill|Department of Divinities|en|Department of Divinities|WD=Q2264720|lt=Department of Divinities}} (Department of Shinto Affairs) of the {{ill|Imperial Court of Japan|ja|朝廷 (日本)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Court of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Imperial Court of Japan|WD=Q30253177}}, the {{ill|Four Seasonal Festivals|ja|四時祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Four Seasonal Festivals|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} designated the '''Ainame Festival''' ({{ill|Ainame Festival|ja|相嘗祭|lt=Ainame-sai|Ainame Festival=Ainame Festival|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ainame Festival|qq=}}), '''Chinkon Festival''' ({{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinkon-sai|qq=}}), and '''Niiname Festival''' ({{ill|Niiname-no-Matsuri|en|Niiname-no-Matsuri|WD=Q11501518|lt=Niiname-sai}}) in the '''ddth month of the old calendar''' as '''Winter Festivals'''. Additionally, although the '''Tsukinami Festival''' ({{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭|1=Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukinami-sai|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|qq=}}), '''Chinka (Fire-Calming Ritual)''' ({{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|ja|鎮火|Fire-Pacification Festival=Fire-Pacification Festival|lt=Chinka|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fire-Pacification Festival|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 消火}}), and '''Dōkyō Festival''' ({{ill|Dōkyō|ja|道饗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dōkyō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) were not inherently winter-specific, they were conducted as part of the Winter Festival period.
Today, many '''Winter Festivals''' retain elements of the '''Chinkon Festival (Ritual for Soul-Pacification)''', and practices such as '''Yutate (Hot Water Purification Rituals)''' ({{ill|Yutate|ja|湯立|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yutate|1=yudate|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563775}}) and '''Kagura (Sacred Dance and Music)''' ({{ill|Kagura|en|Kagura|WD=Q1348260|lt=Kagura}}), which originated from '''Shimotsuki Kagura''' ({{ill|Shimotsuki Kagura|ja|霜月神楽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimotsuki Kagura|qq=}}), are often performed.
== Notes == {{reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Spring Matsuri|lt=Spring Matsuri|lt=Spring Festivals|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spring Festivals|qq=}} * [[Summer Matsuri|Summer festivals]] * {{ill|Autumn Matsuri|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Autumn Festivals|qq=}} * {{ill|Winter_break|en|Winter_break|WD=Q110726465|lt=Winter break}} *
* {{ill|Yule|en|Yule|WD=Q211945|lt=Yule}} – Ancient European winter solstice celebrations
* {{ill|Tanabata|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanabata|qq=}} * {{ill|Community building|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Community building|qq=}} *
*{{Event-stub}}
[[File:Ako Gishisai De09 13.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Akō Gishi Festival|ja|赤穂義士祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akō Gishi Festival|qq=}}]]
