In ancient times, the '''10th month of the old calendar''' ({{ill|Kannazuki|ja|神無月|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannazuki|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}) was considered an '''unlucky month''' ({{ill|Imizuki|ja|忌月|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imizuki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}).<ref>Although the unlucky months are commonly introduced as January, May, and September in the modern calendar, in the old calendar, they were roughly one month later, making the 10th month of the old calendar an unlucky month.</ref> Thus, the '''harvest festival''' was originally held in the '''ddth month of the old calendar''', after the month changed and winter began. However, as the '''taboos''' surrounding the 10th month faded, the '''Autumn Festival, held immediately after the harvest''', became established.
{{ill|Shinobu Orikuchi|en|Shinobu Orikuchi|WD=Q3482286|lt=Orikuchi Shinobu|1=Shinobu Orikuchi}} viewed this period, spanning from autumn to the following spring, as a time of '''spiritual renewal''' ({{ill|Chinkon|ja|鎮魂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinkon|1=chinkon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10297557}}), during which all souls, including human ones, were refreshed. He theorized that the '''Autumn Festival, Winter Festival, and Spring Festival''' were originally '''one continuous ritual cycle''', dealing with '''harvest, soul renewal, and prayers for the coming year'''. Over time, he suggested, the '''Autumn and Spring Festivals split off''', leaving the remainder as the '''Winter Festival'''.
Similarly, {{ill|Kunio Yanagita|en|Kunio Yanagita|WD=Q971687|lt=Yanagita Kunio|1=Kunio Yanagita}} pointed out that the '''ddth month of the old calendar''', which includes the '''Winter Solstice''', was a turning point toward spring. He argued that Japanese festivals traditionally placed great importance on this month.
In the {{ill|Department of Divinities|en|Department of Divinities|WD=Q2264720|lt=Department of Divinities|1=Department of Divinities}} (Department of Shinto Affairs) of the {{ill|Imperial Court of Japan|ja|朝廷 (日本)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Court of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Imperial Court of Japan|WD=Q30253177}}, the {{ill|Four Seasonal Festivals|ja|四時祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Four Seasonal Festivals|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} designated the '''Ainame Festival''' ({{ill|Ainame Festival|ja|相嘗祭|lt=Ainame-sai|Ainame Festival=Ainame Festival|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ainame Festival|qq=}}), '''Chinkon Festival''' ({{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinkon-sai|qq=}}), and '''Niiname Festival''' ({{ill|Niiname-no-Matsuri|en|Niiname-no-Matsuri|WD=Q11501518|lt=Niiname-sai|1=Niiname-no-Matsuri}}) in the '''ddth month of the old calendar''' as '''Winter Festivals'''. Additionally, although the '''Tsukinami Festival''' ({{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭|1=Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukinami-sai|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|qq=}}), '''Chinka (Fire-Calming Ritual)''' ({{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|ja|鎮火|Fire-Pacification Festival=Fire-Pacification Festival|lt=Chinka|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fire-Pacification Festival|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 消火}}), and '''Dōkyō Festival''' ({{ill|Dōkyō|ja|道饗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dōkyō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) were not inherently winter-specific, they were conducted as part of the Winter Festival period.
Today, many '''Winter Festivals''' retain elements of the '''Chinkon Festival (Ritual for Soul-Pacification)''', and practices such as '''Yutate (Hot Water Purification Rituals)''' ({{ill|Yutate|ja|湯立|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yutate|1=yudate|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563775}}) and '''Kagura (Sacred Dance and Music)''' ({{ill|Kagura|en|Kagura|WD=Q1348260|lt=Kagura|1=Kagura}}), which originated from '''Shimotsuki Kagura''' ({{ill|Shimotsuki Kagura|ja|霜月神楽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimotsuki Kagura|qq=}}), are often performed.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:冬祭り]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:冬祭り]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:冬祭り]]
{{Referencesの明記|date=2017th year 2月8日 (水) dd:32 (UTC)}}
Winter festivals or '''Winter Matsuri''' (冬祭り) refer to [[:en:Festivals|Festivals]] held during the {{ill|Winter|en|Winter|WD=Q1311|lt=Winter|1=Winter}} season. These events typically occur over the three-month period from {{ill|Lidong|en|Lidong|WD=Q1191018|lt=Rittō|1=Lidong}} (the beginning of winter) to [[:en:Risshun|Risshun]] (the beginning of spring), corresponding to {{ill|November|en|November|WD=Q125|lt=November|1=November}}, {{ill|December|en|December|WD=Q126|lt=December|1=December}}, and {{ill|January|en|January|WD=Q108|lt=January|1=January}}.
== Overview == The '''Winter Festival''' incorporates both the '''harvest festival elements''' of the {{ill|Autumn festival|ja|秋祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Autumn festival|1=Autumn Matsuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11595783}} and the '''prayer for the year's blessings elements''' of the {{ill|Spring Matsuri|lt=Spring Matsuri|lt=Spring festival|ja|春祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spring festival|qq=}}, making its exact positioning somewhat unclear. In ancient times, the '''10th month of the old calendar''' ({{ill|Kannazuki|ja|神無月|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannazuki|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}) was considered an '''unlucky month''' ({{ill|Imizuki|ja|忌月|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imizuki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}).<ref>Although the unlucky months are commonly introduced as January, May, and September in the modern calendar, in the old calendar, they were roughly one month later, making the 10th month of the old calendar an unlucky month.</ref> Thus, the '''harvest festival''' was originally held in the '''ddth month of the old calendar''', after the month changed and winter began. However, as the '''taboos''' surrounding the 10th month faded, the '''Autumn Festival, held immediately after the harvest''', became established.
{{ill|Shinobu Orikuchi|en|Shinobu Orikuchi|WD=Q3482286|lt=Orikuchi Shinobu|1=Shinobu Orikuchi}} viewed this period, spanning from autumn to the following spring, as a time of '''spiritual renewal''' ({{ill|Chinkon|ja|鎮魂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinkon|1=chinkon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10297557}}), during which all souls, including human ones, were refreshed. He theorized that the '''Autumn Festival, Winter Festival, and Spring Festival''' were originally '''one continuous ritual cycle''', dealing with '''harvest, soul renewal, and prayers for the coming year'''. Over time, he suggested, the '''Autumn and Spring Festivals split off''', leaving the remainder as the '''Winter Festival'''.
Similarly, {{ill|Kunio Yanagita|en|Kunio Yanagita|WD=Q971687|lt=Yanagita Kunio|1=Kunio Yanagita}} pointed out that the '''ddth month of the old calendar''', which includes the '''Winter Solstice''', was a turning point toward spring. He argued that Japanese festivals traditionally placed great importance on this month.
In the {{ill|Department of Divinities|en|Department of Divinities|WD=Q2264720|lt=Department of Divinities|1=Department of Divinities}} (Department of Shinto Affairs) of the {{ill|Imperial Court of Japan|ja|朝廷 (日本)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Court of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Imperial Court of Japan|WD=Q30253177}}, the {{ill|Four Seasonal Festivals|ja|四時祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Four Seasonal Festivals|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} designated the '''Ainame Festival''' ({{ill|Ainame Festival|ja|相嘗祭|lt=Ainame-sai|Ainame Festival=Ainame Festival|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ainame Festival|qq=}}), '''Chinkon Festival''' ({{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinkon-sai|qq=}}), and '''Niiname Festival''' ({{ill|Niiname-no-Matsuri|en|Niiname-no-Matsuri|WD=Q11501518|lt=Niiname-sai|1=Niiname-no-Matsuri}}) in the '''ddth month of the old calendar''' as '''Winter Festivals'''. Additionally, although the '''Tsukinami Festival''' ({{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭|1=Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukinami-sai|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|qq=}}), '''Chinka (Fire-Calming Ritual)''' ({{ill|Fire-Pacification Festival|ja|鎮火|Fire-Pacification Festival=Fire-Pacification Festival|lt=Chinka|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fire-Pacification Festival|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 消火}}), and '''Dōkyō Festival''' ({{ill|Dōkyō|ja|道饗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dōkyō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) were not inherently winter-specific, they were conducted as part of the Winter Festival period.
Today, many '''Winter Festivals''' retain elements of the '''Chinkon Festival (Ritual for Soul-Pacification)''', and practices such as '''Yutate (Hot Water Purification Rituals)''' ({{ill|Yutate|ja|湯立|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yutate|1=yudate|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563775}}) and '''Kagura (Sacred Dance and Music)''' ({{ill|Kagura|en|Kagura|WD=Q1348260|lt=Kagura|1=Kagura}}), which originated from '''Shimotsuki Kagura''' ({{ill|Shimotsuki Kagura|ja|霜月神楽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimotsuki Kagura|qq=}}), are often performed.
